target: The current work evaluates how Nigella sativa affects thyroid function, blood protein levels, and physical measurements in those with Hashimoto's thyroid inflammation.

source: Methods: Forty patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis, aged between 22 and 50 years old, participated in the trial and were randomly allocated into two groups of intervention and control receiving powdered Nigella sativa or placebo daily for 8 weeks.
target: Forty patients with Hashimoto's thyroid inflammation, aged 22 through 50, were randomly split into two groups receiving powdered Nigella sativa or inactive treatment daily for 8 weeks.

source: Results: Treatment with Nigella sativa significantly reduced body weight and body mass index (BMI).
target: Using an herbal medicine reduced body weight and body mass index.

source: Serum concentrations of thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) and anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies decreased while serum T3 concentrations increased in Nigella sativa-treated group after 8 weeks.
target: Blood levels of thyroid stimulating hormone and anti-thyroid cell proteins decreased while thyroid product levels increased in the Nigella sative-treated group after 8 weeks.

source: There was a significant reduction in serum VEGF concentrations in intervention group.
target: The treatment group showed reduced blood vessel signaling protein.

source: None of these changes had been observed in placebo treated group.
target: No changes occured in the inactive treatment group.

source: In stepwise multiple regression model, changes in waist to hip ratio (WHR) and thyroid hormones were significant predictors of changes in serum VEGF and Nesgfatin-1 values in Nigella sativa treated group (P < 0.05).
target: Changes in waist to hip ratio and thyroid hormones were linked to changes in certain blood protein levels in the Nigella sative-treated group.

source: Conclusions: Our data showed a potent beneficial effect of powdered Nigella sativa in improving thyroid status and anthropometric variables in patients with Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
target: Nigella sativa may improve thyroid and general health in those with Hashimoto's thyroiditis (thyroid inflammation).

source: Moreover, Nigella sativa significantly reduced serum VEGF concentrations in these patients.
target: Also, Nigella sativa (an herbal medicine) reduces blood vessel signaling protein in these patients.

source: Considering observed health- promoting effect of this medicinal plant in ameliorating the disease severity, it can be regarded as a useful therapeutic approach in management of Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
target: Due to its health-promoting effect, this medicinal plant can be a useful treatment for Hashimoto's thyroidits.

source: Objective: To describe the historical refinements, understanding of physiology and clinical outcomes observed with thyroid hormone replacement strategies.
target: Our objective is to summarize the history, understanding of how they work, and patient health changes seen with thyroid hormone replacement treatments.

source: Methods: A Medline search was initiated using the search terms, levothyroxine, thyroid hormone history, levothyroxine mono therapy, thyroid hormone replacement, combination LT4 therapy, levothyroxine Bioequivalence.
target: We searched Medline using the search terms, levothyroxine, thyroid hormone history, levothyroxine mono therapy, thyroid hormone replacement, combination LT4 therapy, levothyroxine Bioequivalence. Both levothyroxine and LT4 are synthetic thryoid hormones.

source: Pertinent articles of interest were identified by title and where available abstract for further review.
target: We identified important papers by title and abstract to look at more closely when possible.

source: Additional references were identified in the course of review of the literature identified.
target: We found more references while reading the important papers.

source: Results: Physicians have intervened in cases of thyroid dysfunction for more than two millennia.
target: Doctors have treated overactive and underactive thyroids for more than 2000 years.

source: Ingestion of animal thyroid derived preparations has been long described but only scientifically documented for the last 130 years.
target: Taking forms of medicine that come from animal thyroid glands has been described for a long time but only written about in scientific papers for the last 130 years.

source: Refinements in hormone preparation, pharmaceutical production and regulation continue to this day.
target: Improvements in hormone medication preparation, drug production and drug laws continue to this day.

source: The literature provides documentation of physiologic, pathologic and clinical outcomes which have been reported and continuously updated.
target: Normal body function, disease, and patient health effects are described and updated regularly in scientific papers.

source: Recommendations for effective and safe use of these hormones for reversal of patho-physiology associated with hypothyroidism and the relief of symptoms of hypothyroidism has documented a progressive refinement in our understanding of thyroid hormone use.
target: Using these hormones to successfully and safely undo changes to body function and improve other symptoms of an underactive thyroid shows our improved understanding of their use.

source: Studies of thyroid hormone metabolism, action and pharmacokinetics have allowed evermore focused recommendations for use in clinical practice.
target: Better patient care has resulted from studies of thyroid hormone metabolism, how it works, and what the body does to it.

source: Levothyroxine mono-therapy has emerged as the therapy of choice of all recent major guidelines.
target: The use of a single drug, levothyroxine, has become the top thyroid medicine based on recent major guidelines.

source: Conclusions: The evolution of thyroid hormone therapies has been significant over an extended period of time.
target: Thyroid hormone treatments have been developed over a long time period.

source: Thyroid hormone replacement is very useful in the treatment of those with hypothyroidism.
target: Thyroid hormone replacement is very useful in treating an underactive thyroid.

source: All of the most recent guidelines of major endocrine societies recommend levothyroxine mono-therapy for first line use in hypothyroidism.
target: Levothyroxine is widely accepted as the first treatment for an underactive thyroid.

source: Objective: Hypothyroidism is relatively common, occurring in approximately 5% of the general US population aged ≥12 years.
target: Underactive thyroid is relatively common, occurring in about 5% of the general U.S. population 12 years and older.

source: Levothyroxine (LT4) monotherapy is the standard of care.
target: The recommended treatment is a single drug, levothyroxine (LT4 - a common thyroid medication).

source: Approximately, 5%-10% of patients who normalise thyroid-stimulating hormone levels with LT4 monotherapy may have persistent symptoms that patients and clinicians may attribute to hypothyroidism.
target: About 5%-10% of patients who use LT4 alone to regulate thyroid-stimulating hormone levels may have ongoing symptoms that patients and doctors may think are caused by underactive thyroid.

source: A long-standing debate in the literature is whether addition of levotriiodothyronine (LT3) to LT4 will ameliorate lingering symptoms.
target: Science disagrees as to whether addition of levotriiodothyronine (LT3 - another thryoid medication) to LT4 will improve these ongoing symptoms.

source: Here, we explore the evidence for and against LT4/LT3 combination therapy as the optimal approach to treat euthyroid patients with persistent complaints.
target: We look at whether combining LT4 and LT3 is the best way to treat patients with normal thyroid function but ongoing symptoms.

source: Methods: Recent literature indexed on PubMed was searched in March 2017 using the terms "hypothyroid" or "hypothyroidism" and "triiodothyronine combination" or "T3 combination."
target: We searched PubMed in March 2017 using the terms "hypothyroid" or "hypothyroidism" and "triiodothyronine combination" or "T3 combination."

source: Relevant non-review articles published in English during the past 10 years were included and supplemented with articles already known to the authors.
target: We looked at English articles in the last 10 years in addition to known articles.

source: Findings: Current clinical evidence is not sufficiently strong to support LT4/LT3 combination therapy in patients with hypothyroidism.
target: We did not find strong proof that combining LT4 and LT3 worked to treat patients with underactive thyroids.

source: Polymorphisms in deiodinase genes that encode the enzymes that convert T4 to T3 in the periphery may provide potential mechanisms underlying unsatisfactory treatment results with LT4 monotherapy.
target: Genes that cause less conversion of T4 to T3 may explain unsuccessful treatment with LT4.

source: However, results of studies on the effect of LT4/LT3 therapy on clinical symptoms and thyroid-responsive genes have thus far not been conclusive.
target: The benefit of adding LT3 to LT4 treatment is uncertain.

source: Conclusions: Persistent symptoms in patients who are biochemically euthyroid with LT4 monotherapy may be caused by several other conditions unrelated to thyroid function, and their cause should be aggressively investigated by the clinician.
target: Ongoing symptoms in patients with normal thyroid function may be due to other causes. A doctor should determine the cause of the ongoing symptoms.

source: Objective: Hypothyroidism is relatively common, occurring in approximately 5% of the general US population aged ≥12 years.
target: Low thyroid function occurs in around 5% of the US population aged ≥12 years.

source: Levothyroxine (LT4) monotherapy is the standard of care.
target: Levothyroxine (LT4) (a thyroid hormonal drug) is standard treatment.

source: Approximately, 5%-10% of patients who normalise thyroid-stimulating hormone levels with LT4 monotherapy may have persistent symptoms that patients and clinicians may attribute to hypothyroidism.
target: Around 5-10% of patients with regular thyroid-stimulating hormone levels after LT4 may still have symptoms attributable to reduced thyroid funciton.

source: A long-standing debate in the literature is whether addition of levotriiodothyronine (LT3) to LT4 will ameliorate lingering symptoms.
target: Experts debate if adding levotriiodothyronine (LT3), another manmade thyroid hormone, to LT4 will help lingering symptoms.

source: Here, we explore the evidence for and against LT4/LT3 combination therapy as the optimal approach to treat euthyroid patients with persistent complaints.
target: We explore evidence for and against LT4/LT3 double treatment for best treating patients with lingering thyroid issues.

source: Methods: Recent literature indexed on PubMed was searched in March 2017 using the terms "hypothyroid" or "hypothyroidism" and "triiodothyronine combination" or "T3 combination."
target: We searched PubMed in March 2017 using the terms "hypothyroid" or "hypothyroidism" (low thyroid function) and "triiodothyronine combination" or "T3 combination" (thyroid hormonal drug therapy).

source: Findings: Current clinical evidence is not sufficiently strong to support LT4/LT3 combination therapy in patients with hypothyroidism.
target: Current evidence does not support LT4/LT3 double treatment for those with reduced thyroid function.

source: Polymorphisms in deiodinase genes that encode the enzymes that convert T4 to T3 in the periphery may provide potential mechanisms underlying unsatisfactory treatment results with LT4 monotherapy.
target: Variations in genes blueprinting enzymes that convert thyroid hormone from one form to another may explain the lack of improvement with LT4 treatment.

source: However, results of studies on the effect of LT4/LT3 therapy on clinical symptoms and thyroid-responsive genes have thus far not been conclusive.
target: However, studies of LT4/LT3 double treatment are not conclusive.

source: Conclusions: Persistent symptoms in patients who are biochemically euthyroid with LT4 monotherapy may be caused by several other conditions unrelated to thyroid function, and their cause should be aggressively investigated by the clinician.
target: Lingering symptoms in patients with normal thyroids from LT4 therapy may be from other thyroid-unrelated causes, which should be investigated by the clinician.

source: Hypothyroidism is one of the most common hormone deficiencies in adults.
target: Underactive thyroid is one of the most common conditions caused by a lack of specific hormones in adults.

source: Most of the cases, particularly those of overt hypothyroidism, are easily diagnosed and managed, with excellent outcomes if treated adequately.
target: Underactive thyroid is usually easily identified and successfully treated.

source: However, minor alterations of thyroid function determine nonspecific manifestations.
target: However, small changes in thyroid function determine symptoms that can be caused by many conditions.

source: Primary hypothyroidism due to chronic autoimmune thyroiditis is largely the most common cause of thyroid hormone deficiency.
target: Primary underactive thyroid caused by a long-term inflamed thyroid, which in turn is caused by thyroid cells that are attacked by infection-preventing cells, is the most common cause of too little thyroid hormones in adults.

source: Central hypothyroidism is a rare and heterogeneous disorder characterized by decreased thyroid hormone secretion by an otherwise normal thyroid gland, due to lack of TSH.
target: Central underactive thyroid, which is not very common and has many causes, is when an otherwise normal thyroid makes too little thyroid hormone due to lack of thyroid-stimulating hormone.

source: The standard treatment of primary and central hypothyroidism is hormone replacement therapy with levothyroxine sodium (LT4).
target: Thyroid hormone replacement with levothyroxine (LT4) is the normal treatment of primary and central underactive thyroid.

source: Treatment guidelines of hypothyroidism recommend monotherapy with LT4 due to its efficacy, long-term experience, favorable side effect profile, ease of administration, good intestinal absorption, long serum half-life and low cost.
target: Treatment guidelines for an underactive thyroid recommend a single drug, levothyroxine (LT4), because of how well it works, how long it has been used, few side effects, how easy it is to use, how well it is absorbed in the stomach, how long it lasts in the blood, and low cost.

source: Despite being easily treatable with a daily dose of LT4, many patients remain hypothyroid due to malabsorption syndromes, autoimmune gastritis, pancreatic and liver disorders, drug interactions, polymorphisms in DIO2 (iodothyronine deiodinase 2), high fiber diet, and more frequently, non-compliance to LT4 therapy.
target: Although taking LT4 daily treats underactive thyroid, many people still have underactive thyroids due to conditions that do not allow absorption in the stomach, inflammation of the stomach lining caused by thyroid cells that are attacked by infection-preventing cells, pancreatic and live disease, two or more drugs interacting with each other, gene variations, eating too much fiber, and more commonly, not following the prescribed LT4 treatment.

source: Compliance to levothyroxine treatment in hypothyroidism is compromised by daily and fasting schedule.
target: Taking levothyroxine as prescribed for underactive thyroid depends on when and if it is taken on an empty stomach.

source: Many adult patients remain hypothyroid due to all the above mentioned and many attempts to improve levothyroxine therapy compliance and absorption have been made.
target: For all of these reasons, many adults still have underactive thyroids. Doctors have tried many ways to make people take levothyroxine as prescribed and make levothyroxine more easily absorbed in the stomach.

source: Hashimoto's thyroiditis (HT) is the most prevalent autoimmune disorder characterized by the destruction of thyroid cells caused by leukocytes and antibody-mediated immune processes accompanied by hypothyroidism.
target: Hashimoto's thyroiditis (HT) is the most common type of disease caused by thyroid cells that are attacked by infection-preventing cells and results in an underactive thyroid.

source: In recent years, evidence has emerged pointing to various roles for vitamin D, including, proliferation and differentiation of normal and cancer cells, cardiovascular function, and immunomodulation.
target: Recently, science has shown vitamin D can change how normal and cancer cells grow, divide, and change from one cell to another, how the heart works, and how the body's immune system changes.

source: Vitamin D deficiency has been especially demonstrated in HT patients.
target: Too little vitamin D is seen in people with Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

source: The aim of this study was to investigate the effect of vitamin D on circulating thyroid autoantibodies and thyroid hormones profile (T4, T3, and TSH) in females with HT.
target: This study aimed to determine the effect of vitamin D on thyroid autoantibodies (substances that develop when a person's immune system mistakenly attacks the thyroid) and thyroid hormone (T4, T3, and thyroid-stimulating hormone) blood levels in women.

source: Forty-two women with HT disease were enrolled in this randomized clinical trial study and divided into vitamin D and placebo groups.
target: We split 42 women with HT disease into two groups and gave one group vitamin D and the other group sham treatment.

source: Patients in the vitamin D and placebo groups received 50 000 IU vitamin D and placebo pearls, weekly for 3 months, respectively.
target: We gave one group 50,000 international units of vitamin D and the other group sugar pills for 3 months.

source: The serum levels of 25-hydroxy vitamin D [25(OH) D], Ca++ion, anti-thyroperoxidase antibody (anti-TPO Ab), anti-thyroglobulin antibody (anti-Tg Ab), T4, T3, and TSH were measured at the baseline and at the end of the study using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays.
target: We measured blood levels of thyroid autoantibodies and thyroid hormones (T4, T3, and thyroid-stimulating hormone) at the beginning and end of the study using a common antibody-measuring tool.

source: The results of this study showed a significant reduction of anti-Tg Ab and TSH hormone in the Vitamin D group compared to the start of the study; however, there was a no significant reduction of anti-TPO Ab in the Vitamin D group compared to the placebo group (p=0.08).
target: Blood levels of one antibody and thyroid-stimulating hormone were lower at the end of the study in the group taking vitamin D. The group taking vitamin D and the group taking sugar pills had similar blood levels of another antibody.

source: No significant changes were observed in the serum levels of T3 and T4 hormones.
target: Blood levels of T3 and T4 hormones did not change in either group.

source: Therefore, vitamin D supplementation can be helpful for alleviation of the disease activity in HT patients; however, further well controlled, large, longitudinal studies are needed to determine whether it can be introduced in clinical practice.
target: Therefore, taking vitamin D can help people with Hashimoto's thyroiditis. However, more large studies done over time are needed to see if it can be used in patient care.

source: In the 1990s, selenium was identified as a component of an enzyme that activates thyroid hormone; since this discovery, the relevance of selenium to thyroid health has been widely studied.
target: In the 1990s, selenium was found to be a part of a protein that helps produce thyroid hormone. Since this discovery, many scientists have studied how selenium might help thyroid health.

source: Selenium, known primarily for the antioxidant properties of selenoenzymes, is obtained mainly from meat, seafood and grains.
target: Selenium, which has antioxidant characteristics, is found in meat, seafood and grains.

source: Intake levels vary across the world owing largely to differences in soil content and factors affecting its bioavailability to plants.
target: The amount of selenium eaten varies across the world due to different soils and how much of it plants absorb.

source: Adverse health effects have been observed at both extremes of intake, with a narrow optimum range.
target: Eating too much or too little selenium can cause health problems, with a small ideal range for consumption.

source: Epidemiological studies have linked an increased risk of autoimmune thyroiditis, Graves disease and goitre to low selenium status.
target: Studies that look at how often diseases occur in different groups of people and why show a relationship between eating too little selenium and higher rates of inflamed thyroid caused by thyroid cells that are attacked by infection-preventing cells, Graves disease (a disease in which infection-preventing cells attack healthy cells and lead to an overactive thyroid), and enlarged thyroids.

source: Trials of selenium supplementation in patients with chronic autoimmune thyroiditis have generally resulted in reduced thyroid autoantibody titre without apparent improvements in the clinical course of the disease.
target: Studies of people taking selenium who have long-term inflamed thyroids caused by thyroid cells attacked by infection-preventing cells generally have bloodwork that suggests more normal thyroid function but no improvement in symptoms.

source: In Graves disease, selenium supplementation might lead to faster remission of hyperthyroidism and improved quality of life and eye involvement in patients with mild thyroid eye disease.
target: In Graves disease, taking selenium might make overactive thyroid go away faster and make quality of life and eye problems better in people with mild thyroid eye disease.

source: Despite recommendations only extending to patients with Graves ophthalmopathy, selenium supplementation is widely used by clinicians for other thyroid phenotypes.
target: Although selenium is only recommended for people with thyroid eye disease, doctors prescribe selenium for other thyroid problems.

source: Ongoing and future trials might help identify individuals who can benefit from selenium supplementation, based, for instance, on individual selenium status or genetic profile.
target: Current and future studies might help determine who should take selenium based, for example, on blood selenium levels and heredity.

source: Purpose of review: The aim of the article is to present the basics of oral levothyroxine (LT4) absorption, reasons why patients may have persistently elevated serum thyroid stimulation hormone (TSH) levels, and alternative strategies for LT4 dosing.
target: This article aims to cover the basics of how well levothyroxine (LT4 - a common thyroid medication) is absorbed when taken by mouth, reasons why people may have continuously high blood levels of thyroid stimulating hormone, and other ways to take levothyroxine.

source: Recent findings: Although oral LT4 tablets are most commonly used for thyroid hormone replacement in patients with hypothyroidism, case studies report that liquid oral LT4, intravenous, intramuscular, and rectal administration of LT4 can successfully treat refractory hypothyroidism.
target: Although LT4 tablets taken by mouth are most commonly used for thyroid hormone replacement in people with underactive thyroids, studies show that taking LT4 in liquid-form by mouth, shot to a vein or muscle, and through the rectum can work to treat poorly-controlled underactive thyroid.

source: Summary: Hypothyroidism is one of the most common endocrine disorders encountered by primary care physicians and endocrinologists.
target: Underactive thyroid is one of the most common hormone disorders doctors see.

source: LT4 is one of the most widely prescribed medications in the world and it is the standard of care treatment for hypothyroidism.
target: LT4 is one of the most commonly prescribed drugs in the world and is the recommended treatment for underactive thyroid.

source: Generally, hypothyroid patients will be treated with LT4 tablets to be taken orally, and monitoring will occur with routine serum thyroid tests, including TSH concentrations.
target: Generally, people with underactive thyroids will take LT4 tablets by mouth and track thryoid levels with common blood thyroid tests, including measuring levels of thyroid-stimulating hormone.

source: However, many patients fail to maintain serum TSH levels in the target range while managed on oral LT4 tablets.
target: Taking LT4 tablets by mouth does not keep blood thyroid-stimulating hormone levels at the right level for many people.

source: A subset of these patients would be considered to have poorly controlled hypothyroidism, sometimes termed refractory hypothyroidism.
target: Some of these people may have poorly-controlled underactive thyroids, or refractory underactive thyroids.

source: For these patients, optimization of ingestion routines and alternative formulations and routes of administration of LT4 can be considered, including oral liquid, intravenous, intramuscular, and even rectal formulations.
target: This group of people might need to take LT4 in other forms or other ways, including liquid-form by mouth, shot to a vein or muscle, or even through the rectum.

source: The chemical structure of a neuroleptic does not relaibly predict the exact profile of its therapeutic action.
target: The chemical structure (arrangement of chemical bonds between atoms in a molecule) of drugs used to treat psychotic disorders does not reliably predict how well the treatment works.

source: We considered the question whether the biochemical action of a neuroleptic, and specifically the ratio between DA-receptor block and NA-receptor block, might have a higher predictive value in this respect.
target: We looked into whether the biological and chemical changes made to the body by a drug to treat psychotic disorders might better predict how well the treatment works.

source: In this context we carried out a double-blind study of the therapeutic value of clozapine and perphenazine in acute psychoses of varying symptomatology and aetiology.
target: We did a study of how well antipsychotics clozapine and perphenazine treat short-term impaired relationships with reality with varying symptoms and causes.

source: There are strong indications that clozapine has only a slight inhibitory effect on transmission in central DA-ergic neurons, but markedly inhibits transmission in central NA-ergic neurons, and that the reverse applies to perphenazine.
target: Data suggest that clozapine and perphenazine have opposite effects on two types of neurons or brain cells.

source: In view of these data we expected perphenazine to be a stronger antipsychotic and a weaker sedative than clozapine, and vice versa.
target: We thought that perphenazine would be a stronger drug to treat impaired relationships with reality and a weaker sedative than clozapine, and vice versa.

source: The plausibility of this hypothesis was demonstrated.
target: This assumption proved reasonable.

source: Partly also on the basis of earlier research, we concluded that the biochemical action of a neuroleptic is a more faithful predictor of its therapeutic action profile than the chemical structure.
target: Partly based on earlier research, we concluded that the biological and chemical changes to the body made by a drug used to treat psychotic disorders better predicts how well the treatment works than the arrangement of chemical bonds between atoms in a molecule.

source: CLINICAL experience with tranquilizers has shown the need for prolonged therapy for chronic neurotic and psychotic disorders.
target: Use of tranquilizers on patients has shown the need for long-term treatment of long-term neurotic and pyschotic mental disorders.

source: Since phenothiazine derivatives such as perphenazine are being employed in this manner and since there have been rare reports of jaundice and leukopenia associated with its administration, questions about a potential deleterious effect of this drug on the liver and blood have to be answered.
target: Because antipsychotic drugs like perphenazine are being used to treat long-term mental disorders and there have been rare cases of yellowing of the skin and a decrease in disease-fighting cells in the blood of people who take it, we must figure out whether this drug harms the liver and blood.

source: Background: Antipsychotic drugs are usually given orally but compliance with medication given by this route may be difficult to quantify.
target: Drugs used to treat psychotic disorders are usually given by mouth, but it is hard to measure if people take the drugs as instructed.

source: The development of depot injections in the 1960s gave rise to extensive use of depots as a means of long-term maintenance treatment.
target: Slow-releasing shots, created in the 1960s, became popular as long-term treatment to keep conditions stable.

source: Perphenazine decanoate and enanthate are depot antipsychotics that belong to the phenothiazine family and have a piperazine ethanol side chain.
target: Perphenazine decanoate and enanthate are slow-release shots used to treat psychotic disorders.

source: Objectives: To assess the effects of depot perphenazine decanoate and enanthate versus placebo, oral antipsychotics and other depot antipsychotic preparations for people with schizophrenia in terms of clinical, social and economic outcomes.
target: We aimed to measure the medical, social, and economic effects of slow-releasing perphenazine decanoate and enanthate shots compared to no treatment, drugs used to treat psychotic disorders by mouth, and other slow-releasing shots used to treat psychotic disorders for people with schizophrenia (a reality-distorting mental illness).

source: Authors' conclusions: Depot perphenazine is in clinical use in the Nordic countries, Belgium, Portugal and the Netherlands.
target: We state that slow-releasing perphenazine shots are used in Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway, Sweden, Belgium, Portugal, and the Netherlands.

source: At a conservative estimate, a quarter of a million people suffer from schizophrenia in those countries and could be treated with depot perphenazine.
target: At least 250,000 people have schizophrenia in these countries and could be given slow-releasing perphenazine shots.

source: The total number of participants in the four trials with useful data is 313.
target: The four trials with useful results had 313 total people.

source: None of the studies observed the effects of oral versus depot antipsychotic drugs.
target: None of the studies compared the effects of antipsychotics taken by mouth to a slow-releasing shot.

source: Until well conducted and reported randomised trials are undertaken clinicians will be in doubt as to the effects of perphenazine depots and people with schizophrenia should exercise their own judgement or ask to be randomised.
target: People with schizophrenia should use their own judgement until more studies are done and doctors know the effects of slow-release perphenazine shots.

source: Background and objective: despite the introduction of newer antiemetics in the prevention of postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV), perphenazine is recommended in current guidelines, as the concept of multimodal management of PONV in high-risk patients requires more than two drugs to be combined.
target: Even though there are newer drugs to prevent nausea and vomiting after surgery, current guidelines recommend the drug perphenazine. A multi-process way to prevent nausea and vomiting in high-risk patients requires using more than two drugs.

source: The aim of this quantitative systematic review was to assess the efficacy and safety of perphenazine in the prophylaxis of PONV in adults and children.
target: We aimed to rate the success and safety of perphenazine in prevention of nausea and vomiting in adults and children.

source: Results: eleven trials published between 1965 and 1999 including a total of 2081 participants fulfilled the inclusion criteria and were further analysed.
target: We looked at eleven studies published between 1965 and 1999 with a total of 2081 people.

source: In children, perphenazine 0.07 mg kg was effective in preventing vomiting (RR, 0.31; 95% CI, 0.18-0.54), whereas in adults, a dose of about 5 mg was effective for the prevention of PONV (RR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.37-0.67).
target: Perphenazine 0.07 mg kg and 5 mg prevented nausea and vomiting in children and adults, respectively.

source: When compared with established newer drugs, for example, ondansetron, dexamethasone or droperidol, no significant differences were observed in the pooled analysis with limited data.
target: We saw no big differences compared to newer drugs like ondansetron, dexamethasone or droperidol in studies with limited results.

source: Reporting of adverse events was poor.
target: Side effects were not well reported.

source: Transient sedation was reported in three eligible trials (RR, 0.9; 95% CI, 0.40-2.05).
target: Short-term sedation occurred in three studies.

source: Conclusion: there is evidence that perphenazine is effective in the prevention of PONV in children and adults without serious adverse effects compared with placebo.
target: Studies show that perphenazine prevents nausea and vomiting in children and adults without serious side effects compared to sham treatment.

source: Background and objective: despite the introduction of newer antiemetics in the prevention of postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV), perphenazine is recommended in current guidelines, as the concept of multimodal management of PONV in high-risk patients requires more than two drugs to be combined.
target: Despite newer anti-nausea drugs, perphenazine (an anti-psychotic) is still used in multimodal treatment of nausea and vomiting post-operation in high-risk patients that need two or more drugs.

source: The aim of this quantitative systematic review was to assess the efficacy and safety of perphenazine in the prophylaxis of PONV in adults and children.
target: This work measures the success and safety of perphenazine in preventing post-operation nausea and vomiting.

source: Results: eleven trials published between 1965 and 1999 including a total of 2081 participants fulfilled the inclusion criteria and were further analysed.
target: Eleven trials published between 1965 and 1999 included 2081 total participats for analysis.

source: In children, perphenazine 0.07 mg kg was effective in preventing vomiting (RR, 0.31; 95% CI, 0.18-0.54), whereas in adults, a dose of about 5 mg was effective for the prevention of PONV (RR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.37-0.67).
target: In children, 0.07 mg kg of perphenzaine prevented vomiting while 5 mg prevented post-operation nausea and vomiting in adults.

source: When compared with established newer drugs, for example, ondansetron, dexamethasone or droperidol, no significant differences were observed in the pooled analysis with limited data.
target: Compared to newer anti-nausea and -vomiting drugs, no differences were observed.

source: Reporting of adverse events was poor.
target: Reporting of harmful events was poor.

source: Transient sedation was reported in three eligible trials (RR, 0.9; 95% CI, 0.40-2.05).
target: Three trials reported temporary sedation or a calming effect.

source: Conclusion: there is evidence that perphenazine is effective in the prevention of PONV in children and adults without serious adverse effects compared with placebo.
target: Perphenazine may be effective in preventing post-operation nausea and vomiting in children and adults without serious harmful effects.

source: We present here a potential new treatment adjunct for glioblastoma.
target: We look at a possible new treatment to add to standard treatment for glioblastoma, a type of brain cancer.

source: Building on murine studies, a series of papers appeared recently showing that therapeutic irradiation of the ipsilateral subventricular zone (SVZ) retards growth of more peripherally growing cortical glioblastomas in humans, suggesting a tumor trophic function for the SVZ.
target: Based on mouse studies, several recent studies showed that radiation of the part of the brain where brain cells are made reduces growth of some glioblastomas in humans, suggesting this part of the brain might play a role in fueling brain tumors.

source: Further studies showed that SVZ cells migrate out towards a peripheral glioblastoma.
target: More studies showed cells from the part of the brain where brain cells are made move outward toward a glioblastoma.

source: Dopamine signaling through D3 subtype receptor indirectly drives this centrifugal migration in humans.
target: Certain molecule signaling causes this cell movement.

source: Since psychiatry has several drugs with good D3 blocking attributes, such as fluphenazine, or perphenazine, we suggest that adding one of these D3 blocking drugs to current standard treatment of resection followed by temozolomide and irradiation might prolong survival by depriving glioblastoma of the trophic functions previously subserved by dopaminergic signaling on SVZ cells.
target: Because many mental health drugs block molecule signaling, such as fluphenazine, or perphenazine, we suggest adding one of these drugs to the current standard treatment of surgery followed by chemotherapy and radiation to possibly increase survival time by cutting off fuel to the glioblastoma.

source: Background: Antipsychotic drugs are the core treatment for schizophrenia.
target: Drugs used to treat psychotic disorders are the main treatment for schizophrenia (a reality-distorting mental illness).

source: Treatment guidelines state that there is no difference in efficacy between the various first-generation antipsychotics, however, low-potency first-generation antipsychotic drugs are sometimes perceived as less efficacious than high-potency first-generation compounds by clinicians, and they also seem to differ in their side effects.
target: Treatment recommendations say there is no difference in treatment effect among older drugs used to treat psychotic disorders. However, doctors sometimes think older drugs with lower strength used to treat psychotic disorders do not work as well as older drugs with higher strength, and they also seem to have different side effects.

source: Authors' conclusions: The results do not show a superiority in efficacy of high-potency perphenazine compared with low-potency first-generation antipsychotics.
target: We conclude that the results do not show that the antipsychotic perphenazine with its higher strength works better than older antipsychotics with lower strength.

source: There is some evidence that perphenazine is more likely to cause akathisia and less likely to cause severe toxicity, but most adverse effect results were equivocal.
target: Some evidence exists that perphenazine is more likely to cause restlessness and less likely to cause drug toxicity in the bloodstream requiring hospitalization, but most side effects were the same.

source: The number of studies as well as the quality of studies is low, with quality of evidence for the main outcomes ranging from moderate to very low, so more randomised evidence would be needed for conclusions to be made.
target: More evidence is needed to make judgements, as the number and quality of studies is low with medium- to very low-quality results.

source: Background: Perphenazine is an old phenothiazine antipsychotic with a potency similar to haloperidol.
target: Perphenazine is an old drug used to treat psychotic disorders with strength similar to haloperidol, another drug to treat psychotic disorders.

source: It has been used for many years and is popular in the northern European countries and Japan.
target: Perphenazine has been used for many years and is popular in the northern European countries and Japan.

source: Objectives: To examine the clinical effects and safety of perphenazine for those with schizophrenia and schizophrenia-like psychoses.
target: We aimed to look at the effects and safety of perphenazine in people with schizophrenia (a reality-distorting mental illness) and schizophrenia-like disorders.

source: Authors' conclusions: Although perphenazine has been used in randomised trials for more than 50 years, incomplete reporting and the variety of comparators used make it impossible to draw clear conclusions.
target: We conclude that although perphenazine has been used for more than 50 years, incomplete results and the variety of drugs used to compare perphenazine to make it impossible to make clear judgements.

source: All data for the main outcomes in this review were of very low quality evidence.
target: The results used in this review were not reliable.

source: At best we can say that perphenazine showed similar effects and adverse events as several of the other antipsychotic drugs.
target: The most we can say is that perphenazine had similar results and side effects as many other drugs used to treat psychotic disorders.

source: Since perphenazine is a relatively inexpensive and frequently used compound, further trials are justified to clarify the properties of this classical antipsychotic drug.
target: Because perphenazine is a cheap and often used drug, more studies are needed to fully understand the drug's properties.

source: Endometrial cancer (EC) is one of the most common and fatal gynecological cancers worldwide, but there is no effective treatment for the EC patients of progesterone resistance.
target: Endometrial cancer (EC), cancer of the lining of the uterus, is one of the most common and deadly cancers of the female reproductive system worldwide, but there is no working treatment for EC patients who do not respond to progesterone, a hormone.

source: Repurposing of existing drugs is a good strategy to discover new candidate drugs.
target: Trying drugs used for other things is a good way to find new ways to treat conditions.

source: In this text, perphenazine (PPZ), approved for psychosis therapy, was identified as a potential agent for the treatment of both progesterone sensitive and resistant endometrial cancer for the first time.
target: Perphenazine, used to treat psychotic disorders, might treat people with endometrial cancer who are both sensitive to and resistant to progesterone.

source: Specifically, perphenazine exhibited good cell proliferation inhibition in Ishikawa (ISK) and KLE cell lines according to the CCK-8 assay and colony formation assay.
target: Perphenazine reduced growth of certain cancer-causing endometrial cells based on common lab tests.

source: It also reduced the cell migration of ISK and KLE cell lines in the light of the transwell migration assay.
target: Tests showed perphenazine also reduced movement of certain cancer-causing endometrial cells.

source: Annexin-V/PI double staining assay suggested that perphenazine could effectively induce ISK and KLE cell apoptosis.
target: Tests suggest that perphenazine could kill certain cancer-causing endometrial cells.

source: Delightedly, PPZ also could significantly attenuate xenograft tumor growth at both 3 mg/kg and 15 mg/kg in mice without influencing the body weights.
target: Perphenazine also could reduce tumor growth in mice without affecting body weight.

source: Endometrial cancer (EC) is one of the most common and fatal gynecological cancers worldwide, but there is no effective treatment for the EC patients of progesterone resistance.
target: Endometrial (outer womb) cancer (EC) is a common, fatal female-related cancer worldwide, but there is no treatment for EC patients of progesterone (specific female hormone) resistance.

source: Repurposing of existing drugs is a good strategy to discover new candidate drugs.
target: Adapting current drugs is useful to discover new candidate drugs.

source: In this text, perphenazine (PPZ), approved for psychosis therapy, was identified as a potential agent for the treatment of both progesterone sensitive and resistant endometrial cancer for the first time.
target: In this text, perphenazine (PPZ), used for psychosis, may treat patients with both progesterone sensitive and resistant outer womb cancer.

source: Specifically, perphenazine exhibited good cell proliferation inhibition in Ishikawa (ISK) and KLE cell lines according to the CCK-8 assay and colony formation assay.
target: Perphenazine (an anti-psychotic) blocked cell growth in two, outer womb cancer cell groups.

source: Annexin-V/PI double staining assay suggested that perphenazine could effectively induce ISK and KLE cell apoptosis.
target: Perphenazine could effectively cause controlled cell death in two, outer womb cancer cell groups.

source: Moreover, results of western blot assay indicated perphenazine obviously inhibited the phosphorylation of Akt.
target: Also, perphenazine could block phosphorylation of Akt, which activates the Akt enzyme which monitors cell growth.

source: Delightedly, PPZ also could significantly attenuate xenograft tumor growth at both 3 mg/kg and 15 mg/kg in mice without influencing the body weights.
target: Delightedly, perphenazine could weaken cancer growth from a different-species organ transplant in mice at various doses without affecting body weight.

source: In the effort to improve treatment effectiveness in glioblastoma, this short note reviewed collected data on the pathophysiology of glioblastoma with particular reference to intersections with the pharmacology of perphenazine.
target: To improve how well treatment for glioblastoma, a type of brain cancer, works, we looked at studies on the disease-related processes associated with glioblastoma and their interaction with how the antipsychotic perphenazine affects the body.

source: That study identified five areas of potentially beneficial intersection.
target: That study found five areas of possibly helpful interaction.

source: Data showed seemingly 5 independent perphenazine attributes of benefit to glioblastoma treatment - i) blocking dopamine receptor 2, ii) reducing centrifugal migration of subventricular zone cells by blocking dopamine receptor 3, iii) blocking serotonin receptor 7, iv) activation of protein phosphatase 2, and v) nausea reduction.
target: Studies showed 5 independent qualities of perphenazine that might help treat glioblastoma, including changing how cells grow and move and reducing nausea.

source: Perphenazine is fully compatible with current chemoirradiation protocols and with the commonly used ancillary medicines used in clinical practice during the course of glioblastoma.
target: Perphenazine can be taken with current chemotherapy and radiation treatments and with commonly prescribed glioblastoma drugs.

source: All these attributes argue for a trial of perphenazine's addition to current standard treatment with temozolomide and irradiation.
target: These qualities suggest the addition of perphenazine to current chemotherapy and radiation treatment should be studied.

source: The subventricular zone seeds the brain with mutated cells that become recurrent glioblastoma after centrifugal migration.
target: Part of the brain sends out damaged cells that become recurring glioblastoma after they move outward.

source: The current paper shows how perphenazine might reduce that contribution.
target: This paper shows how perphenazine might decrease this activity.

source: Perphenazine is an old, generic, cheap, phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that has been in continuous clinical use worldwide since the 1950's.
target: Perphenazine is an old, generic, cheap drug used to treat psychotic disorders worldwide since the 1950s.

source: Clinical experience and research data over these decades have shown perphenazine to be well-tolerated in psychiatric populations, in normals, and in non-psychiatric, medically ill populations for whom perphenazine is used to reduce nausea.
target: Studies over time have shown perphenazine does not cause many side effects in normal people, people with mental illness, and ill people taking perphenazine to reduce nausea.

source: For now (Summer, 2020) the nature of glioblastoma requires a polypharmacy approach until/unless a core feature and means to address it can be identified in the future.
target: As of Summer 2020, glioblastoma requires multiple drugs to treat it until and unless it is better understood and a single treatment drug is found.

source: Conclusions: Perphenazine possesses a remarkable constellation of attributes that recommend its use in GB treatment.
target: We conclude that perphenazine has many qualities that suggest its use in treating glioblastoma.

source: In the effort to improve treatment effectiveness in glioblastoma, this short note reviewed collected data on the pathophysiology of glioblastoma with particular reference to intersections with the pharmacology of perphenazine.

target: 5 perphenazine attributes of benefit to glioblastoma treatment include blocking two target sites for chemical messengers, reducing movement of specific cells, activating certain enzymes, and reducing nausea.

target: Perphenazine is an old, common, cheap antipsychotic drug used clinically worldwide since the 1950's.

target: Perphenzaine has been well-tolerated in psychiatric groups, in healthy groups, and in non-psychiatric but medically ill groups who use perphenazine to reduce nausea.

source: We have found oral perphenazine 8 mg (OP8) useful as follows: (1) as a nonsedating antiemetic; (2) as a preventative measure similar to the antihistamine promethazine against ketamine-induced psychotomimetic effects; and (3) as a safe single-dose drug (only 1.3 extrapyramidal events per 10,000 patients receiving 4–8 mg oral dose, with all events easily treated).
target: Perphenazine 8 mg (OP8) can be taken by mouth for the following uses: (1) as a nonsedating drug to prevent nausea and vomiting; (2) like promethazine, an antihistamine (allergy drug) , to prevent psychotic symptoms caused by ketamine (a pain relief drug); and (3) as a safe one-dose drug.

source: Additionally, we evaluated the efficacy of single-dose OP8 to a single 40 mg dose of aprepitant given preoperatively in colorectal surgery patients at our academic center within an enhanced recovery protocol, which was designed to mitigate opioid utilization, reduce Postoperative Nausea and Vomiting (PONV), and optimize patient recovery.
target: We also rated the success of one-dose OP8 compared to one 40 mg dose of aprepitant, used to prevent nausea and vomiting, given to people before rectum, anus, and colon operations in our center with guidelines designed to help people recover more quickly from surgery, reduce opioid use, and reduce nausea and vomiting after surgery.

source: In this retrospective study, no differences were noted in antiemetic requirement on postoperative days 0 and 1 between patients receiving OP8 versus aprepitant.
target: In this study, we saw no differences in nausea and vomiting prevention on the same day of surgery and one day after surgery between patients who got OP8 versus aprepitant.

source: As enhanced recovery protocols become more widespread and continue to be applied to other surgical specialties, effective PONV prevention is imperative for improving patient outcomes.
target: As guidelines to help people recover more quickly from surgery become more widespread and used for other surgeries, successfully preventing nausea and vomiting after surgery is key to improving patient results.

source: OP8 deserves to be properly evaluated (by clinical study, and/or in routine clinical practice) as a part of a cost-effective multimodal enhanced recovery strategy.
target: OP8 should be studied as part of a cost-effective, multi-process way to help people recover more quickly from surgery.

source: Management of 242 foreign bodies of the upper gastrointestinal tract are reported.
target: We reported removal of 242 objects that shouldn't be eaten which were stuck in the mouth, throat, esophagus, stomach, and upper part of the small intestine.

source: Thirty-nine were in the pharynx, 181 in the esophagus, 19 in the stomach, and 3 in the small bowel.
target: Thirty-nine objects that shouldn't be eaten were located in the throat, 181 in the esophagus, 19 in the stomach, and 3 in the small intestine.

source: The flexible panendoscope was used 211 times (87.2%) to manage these foreign bodies, while the rigid esophagoscope was used 12 times (5.0%).
target: A flexible, telescoping tube with a camera was used 211 times (87.2%) to treat these objects, while the stiff, inflexible tube with a camera was used 12 times (5.0%).

source: Two hundred thirty-nine foreign bodies (98.8%) were successfully managed endoscopically.
target: Two hundred thirty-nine objects that shouldn't be eaten (98.8%) were successfully removed by putting a long, thin tube directly into the body.

source: The surgery rate was 0.4%.
target: 0.4% of people had surgery to remove the object that shouldn't be eaten.

source: There was no morbidity or mortality.
target: Nobody had an illness or died.

source: Twenty-five percent of the cases were done under general endotracheal anesthesia.
target: Twenty-five percent of the cases required people to be put in a sleep-like state with a breathing tube.

source: Coins in the esophagus are removed promptly if in the cervical or mid esophagus, and within 12 hours if in the distal esophagus.
target: Coins in the esophagus are removed quickly if they are in the uppermost or mid esophagus, and within 12 hours if in the lower esophagus.

source: Once in the stomach, they will usually pass without difficulty.
target: If coins makes it to the stomach, they are usually passed easily.

source: Meat impaction resulting in an obstructed esophagus is an urgent problem and the bolus should be removed within hours.
target: Meat that gets stuck and blocks the esophagus is an urgent problem, and the blockage should be removed within hours.

source: Sharp and pointed foreign bodies can be very difficult to manage.
target: Sharp and pointed objects can be hard to remove.

source: Dry runs with a reproduction of the foreign body are essential to successful removal.
target: Practicing with a similar object is very important.

source: Button batteries lodged in the esophagus represent an emergency and should be removed without delay.
target: Button batteries in the esophagus are an emergency and should be removed immediately.

source: Once in the stomach, they will usually pass through the gastrointestinal tract without difficulty.
target: If button batteries make it to the stomach, they are usually passed easily.

source: The forward-viewing flexible panendoscope has become the instrument of choice in managing foreign bodies in most tertiary medical centers as well as in the community hospitals.
target: A new kind of flexible, telescoping tube with a camera has become the tool to treat objects that shouldn't be eaten in most specialized medical centers and community hospitals.

source: Objective: To evaluate management of foreign bodies in the upper gastrointestinal tract.
target: Our objective is to rate treatment of objects that shouldn't be eaten found in the mouth, throat, esophagus, stomach, and upper part of the small intestine.

source: Patients and methods: A total of 103 patients with history of foreign body ingestion were included in this study.
target: We included 103 patients who ate objects that shouldn't be eaten.

source: X-ray neck and rigid oesophagoscopy was carried out in all patients for diagnosis and removal of foreign bodies.
target: Neck x-rays and a procedure that uses a stiff, inflexible tube with a camera were done to diagnose and remove the objects that shouldn't be eaten.

source: A structured questionnaire was designed to record all information.
target: We recorded all information using a questionnaire.

source: Results: Dysphagia (92%) and tenderness in neck (60%) were the most common clinical features.
target: Trouble swallowing (92%) and soreness in neck (60%) were the most common symptoms.

source: Majority (89%) patients had come to the hospital within 24 hours.
target: Most of the patients (89%) had come to the hospital within 24 hours.

source: X-ray of the neck (lateral view) was the most useful investigation with presence of air in the esophagus being a significant finding.
target: X-rays of the side of the neck helped the most with air in the esophagus being an important finding.

source: Post-cricoid region was the site of impaction of foreign bodies in 84% of the subjects.
target: The part of the throat that allows food to pass into the esophagus was where objects that shouldn't be eaten got stuck in 84% of people.

source: The procedure of esophagoscopy was successful in 90 patients (97%) and failed in 3 patients (3%).
target: Using a flexible tube with a camera worked in 90 patients (97%) and didn't work in 3 patients (3%).

source: Coins were the most common foreign bodies (60%), followed by meat related foreign bodies (22.5%) and dentures in 5% cases.
target: Coins were swallowed most often (60%), followed by meat (22.5%) and dentures (5%).

source: Complications occurred in 18% patients and were more common in adults (37.1%) compared to children (8.8%).
target: Complications happened in 18% of patients and were more common in adults (37.1%) compared to children (8.8%).

source: The most serious complication was pneumomediastinum.
target: The most serious complication was air in the center of the chest.

source: Maximum complications occurred with dentures (80%) and bone chips (42%).
target: People who swallowed dentures (80%) and bone chips (42%) had the most complications.

source: Conclusion: Foreign body in the esophagus is a serious condition and early removal by rigid esophagoscopy is recommended which is a safe and effective procedure.
target: We concluded that objects in the esophagus that shouldn't be eaten are a serious condition and early removal by inserting a flexible tube with a camera is safe and works.

source: Objective: To evaluate management of foreign bodies in the upper gastrointestinal tract.
target: The objective is to rate treatment of foreign objects stuck in the upper digestive tract.

source: Patients and methods: A total of 103 patients with history of foreign body ingestion were included in this study.
target: 103 patients that swallowed foreign objects were examined.

source: X-ray neck and rigid oesophagoscopy was carried out in all patients for diagnosis and removal of foreign bodies.
target: X-ray neck and esophagus checks were carried out in all patients for identifying and removing foreign objects.

source: A structured questionnaire was designed to record all information.
target: A structured questionnaire was created to record all information.

source: Results: Dysphagia (92%) and tenderness in neck (60%) were the most common clinical features.
target: Difficulty swallowing (92%) and neck tenderness (60%) were the most common medical attributes.

source: Majority (89%) patients had come to the hospital within 24 hours.
target: Most (89%) patients came to the hospital within 24 hours.

source: X-ray of the neck (lateral view) was the most useful investigation with presence of air in the esophagus being a significant finding.
target: Neck x-rays (from the side) were the most useful scan, showing presence of air in the esophagus as an important finding.

source: Post-cricoid region was the site of impaction of foreign bodies in 84% of the subjects.
target: A specific site near the bottom of the throat was the site of stuck foreign objects in 84% of patients.

source: The procedure of esophagoscopy was successful in 90 patients (97%) and failed in 3 patients (3%).
target: Inserting a tube with a viewing lens into the esophagus worked in 90 patients (97%) and failed in 3 patients (3%).

source: Coins were the most common foreign bodies (60%), followed by meat related foreign bodies (22.5%) and dentures in 5% cases.
target: Coins were the most common foreign objects (60%), followed by meat-related substances (22.5%) and dentures (5%).

source: Complications occurred in 18% patients and were more common in adults (37.1%) compared to children (8.8%).
target: Issues occured in 18% of patients and were more common in adults (37.1%) compared to children (8.8%).

source: The most serious complication was pneumomediastinum.
target: The most serious issue was air trapped in the area between the lungs.

source: Maximum complications occurred with dentures (80%) and bone chips (42%).
target: Serious issues occured with dentures (80%) and bone chips (42%).

source: Conclusion: Foreign body in the esophagus is a serious condition and early removal by rigid esophagoscopy is recommended which is a safe and effective procedure.
target: Foreign objects in the esophagus is serious and early removal with esophagus checks, which are safe and effective, is recommended.

source: Foreign bodies in the ear, nose, and throat are occasionally seen in family medicine, usually in children.
target: Objects that shouldn't be in the ear, nose, and throat are sometimes seen by family doctors, usually in children.

source: The most common foreign bodies are food, plastic toys, and small household items.
target: The most common objects are food, plastic toys, and small household items.

source: Diagnosis is often delayed because the causative event is usually unobserved, the symptoms are nonspecific, and patients often are misdiagnosed initially.
target: Diagnosis is often late because the event that caused the object to get stuck wasn't seen, the symptoms are vague, and people often are diagnosed incorrectly at first.

source: Most ear and nose foreign bodies can be removed by a skilled physician in the office with minimal risk of complications.
target: Most objects stuck in the ear and nose can be removed by a skilled doctor in the office with low risk of other problems.

source: Common removal methods include use of forceps, water irrigation, and suction catheter.
target: Common methods to remove the object include the use of forceps, flushing with water, and using a long, flexible suction tube.

source: Pharyngeal or tracheal foreign bodies are medical emergencies requiring surgical consultation.
target: Objects stuck in the throat or windpipe are emergencies and considered for surgery.

source: Radiography results are often normal.
target: X-rays are often normal.

source: Flexible or rigid endoscopy usually is required to confirm the diagnosis and to remove the foreign body.
target: Flexible or stiff tubes with cameras are usually needed to make sure the diagnosis is correct and to remove the object.

source: Physicians need to have a high index of suspicion for foreign bodies in children with unexplained upper airway symptoms.
target: Doctors need to have a good reason to think there might be an object stuck in children with unexplained upper airway symptoms.

source: It is important to understand the anatomy and the indications for subspecialist referral.
target: It is important to understand the body’s structure and reasons to refer to a subspecialist.

source: The evidence is inadequate to make strong recommendations for specific removal techniques.
target: The available information is not enough to make strong recommendations for specific ways to remove objects.

source: Objective: In the present study, an attempt was made to examine the effects of aural stimulation with ointment containing capsaicin on swallowing function in order to develop a novel and safe treatment for non-obstructive dysphagia in elderly patients.
target: We looked at the effects of rubbing an ointment with capsaicin, found in different types of hot peppers, on the ears to improve swallowing function and create a new and safe treatment for old people who have the sensation of food stuck in the esophagus without physical blockage.

source: Patients and methods: The present study included 26 elderly patients with non-obstructive dysphagia.
target: We looked at 26 old people who had the sensation of food stuck in the esophagus without physical blockage.

source: Ointment containing 0.025% capsaicin (0.5 g) was applied to the external auditory canal with a cotton swab under otoscope only once or once a day for 7 days before swallowing of a bolus of colored water (3 mL), which was recorded by transnasal videoendoscopy and evaluated according to the endoscopic swallowing score.
target: We used a Q-tip to put ointment with 0.025% capsaicin (0.5 g) in the ear only once or once a day for 7 days before drinking 3 mL of colored water, which was recorded using a flexible tube with a camera and rated using a common test that measures how well one swallows.

source: Results: After a single application of 0.025% capsaicin ointment to the right external auditory canal, the endoscopic swallowing score was significantly decreased, and this effect lasted for 60 minutes.
target: After putting 0.025% capsaicin ointment to the right ear, the common swallowing test showed less difficulty swallowing. This effect lasted for one hour.

source: After repeated applications of the ointment to each external auditory canal alternatively once a day for 7 days, the endoscopic swallowing score decreased significantly in patients with more severe non-obstructive dysphagia.
target: After putting the ointment on alternating ears once a day for 7 days, the common swallowing test showed less difficulty swallowing in people with more serious sensations of food stuck in the esophagus without physical blockage.

source: Of the eight tube-fed patients of this group, three began direct swallowing exercises using jelly, which subsequently restored their oral food intake.
target: Of the eight people fed with tubes in this group, three began direct swallowing exercises using jelly, which then returned their ability to eat food by mouth.

source: Conclusion: These findings suggest that stimulation of the external auditory canal with ointment containing capsaicin improves swallowing function in elderly patients with non-obstructive dysphagia.
target: We concluded that putting ointment with capsaicin on the ear improves swallowing function in old people who have the sensation of food stuck in the esophagus without physical blockage.

source: By the same mechanism used by angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors to induce cough reflex, which has been shown to prevent aspiration pneumonia, aural stimulation with capsaicin may reduce the incidence of aspiration pneumonia in dysphagia patients via Arnold's ear-cough reflex stimulation.
target: In the same way as other methods to stimulate the cough reflex, putting capsaicin on the ears may decrease how often people with difficulty swallowing develop pneumonia, caused by something entering the airway or lungs by accident, through a cough reflex stimulated by a nerve.

source: Most swallowing problems can be treated, although the treatment depends on the type of dysphagia.
target: Most swallowing problems can be fixed, although the treatment depends on the type of trouble swallowing.

source: A multidisciplinary team of surgeons, phoniatrists, and speech-language therapists is necessary to develop the appropriate treatment strategy.
target: A team of surgeons, doctors who treat organs involved in speech production, and people who treat communication and swallowing disorders is needed to figure out the best treatment.

source: A thorough medical history, clinical investigation of the swallowing muscles, and fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing with foods of different consistencies help to decide whether swallowing exercises alone are sufficient, or whether an additional pharmacologic or surgical treatment is needed to prevent aspiration, malnutrition, and dehydration.
target: Information provided by the patient, examination of the swallowing muscles, and using a flexible tube with a camera to see swallowing of different foods help decide whether swallowing exercises are enough, or whether drugs or surgery are also needed to prevent something entering the airway or lungs by accident, prevent a lack of nutrients in the body, and prevent a lack of fluid in the body.

source: Most swallowing problems can be treated, although the treatment depends on the type of dysphagia.
target: Most swallowing problems can be treated, but the treatment depends on the type of swallowing difficulty.

source: A multidisciplinary team of surgeons, phoniatrists, and speech-language therapists is necessary to develop the appropriate treatment strategy.
target: A multi-type team of surgeons, speech-related organ specialists, and speech-language therapists is needed to create appropriate treatment.

source: A thorough medical history, clinical investigation of the swallowing muscles, and fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing with foods of different consistencies help to decide whether swallowing exercises alone are sufficient, or whether an additional pharmacologic or surgical treatment is needed to prevent aspiration, malnutrition, and dehydration.
target: A clear medical history, swallowing muscle examination, and monitoring with small devices of swallowing of food with various textures help decide if swallowing exercises alone or additional drug or surgical treatment is needed to prevent breathing, eating, and hydration issues.

source: Foreign bodies to the ear, nose, and throat often can be managed in the emergency department, particularly if the patient offers a history consistent with foreign body and is calm and compliant with the examination and removal attempts.
target: Objects that shouldn't be found in the ear, nose, and throat can be removed in the emergency room, especially if the information the patient provides lines up with an object that shouldn't be in the body and the patient is calm and agreeable with the exam and efforts to remove the object.

source: Tips for success include analgesia, adequate visualization, immobilization of the patient's head, dexterity and experience level of the provider, and minimizing attempts at removal.
target: Tips for success include painkillers, the ability to see the object, keeping the patient's head still, skill and experience of the doctor, and removing the object with the least number of tries.

source: It is critical to recognize the risks involved with certain retained objects (button batteries or sharp objects) and when to call a consultant to help facilitate safe, successful removal of objects to the ear, nose, and throat.
target: It is very important to realize the risks involved with certain objects (button batteries or sharp objects) and when to call a specialist to help ensure safe, successful removal of objects to the ear, nose, and throat.

source: Background: Patients with foreign bodies in their ear, nose or throat typically present to general practitioners.
target: People with objects in their ear, nose or throat that shouldn't be there usually go to general doctors.

source: The safe and timely removal of foreign bodies ensures good patient outcomes and limits complications.
target: The safe and quick removal of objects that shouldn't be swallowed provides good results and reduces complications.

source: Objectives: The aim of this paper is to outline common foreign objects and review the associated anatomy that may make removal difficult.
target: We aimed to list common objects that shouldn't be swallowed and look at the parts of the body that may make removing them hard.

source: A description of instruments and indications for use is provided, along with circumstances where specialist referral is warranted.
target: We described tools and how to use them, along with times when a specialist should see the patient.

source: Discussion: The use of appropriate techniques for removal of foreign bodies reduces the complications of removal and associated distress, and limits the number of cases that require surgical input.
target: Using the right ways to remove objects that shouldn't be swallowed lowers the complications of removal and possible pain, and reduces the cases that require surgery.

source: Objective: This study was designed to explore the clinical application of video laryngoscopy in the diagnosis and treatment of throat foreign bodies (FBs).
target: The study aimed to look at the use of a camera on the tip of a curved blade to diagnose and remove objects in the throat that shouldn’t be eaten.

source: Method: In total, 1572 patients diagnosed with throat FBs at the Department of Otolaryngology of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital were retrospectively analysed.
target: We studied 1572 people who had objects in the throat that shouldn't be eaten.

source: The covariables collected were the time from FB ingestion to admission, age, sex, duration of admission, and site of impaction.
target: We wrote down the time between eating the object and coming to the hospital, age, gender, how long the patient was in the hospital, and where the object was stuck.

source: Result: The most common FBs were fish bones, which accounted for 1446 (91.98%) of 1572 FBs.
target: The most common objects stuck were fish bones, which made up 1446 (91.98%) of 1572 objects.

source: Among all 1572 FBs, 1004 (63.87%) were successfully removed by video laryngoscopy without complications.
target: Among all 1572 objects, 1004 (63.87%) were successfully removed using a camera on the tip of a curved blade without problems.

source: A shorter duration of admission was associated with a higher diagnostic rate under video laryngoscopy.
target: When a camera on the tip of a curved blade was used to diagnose the object, people left the hospital sooner.

source: The diagnostic rate of sharp FBs was significantly higher than that of non-sharp FBs.
target: Diagnosing sharp objects was higher than that of non-sharp objects.

source: The most common sites of throat FBs were the tongue root (42.29%), epiglottic vallecula (19.40%), tonsil (18.21%), and piriform fossa (10.65%).
target: The most common place that objects were stuck in the throat were the far back and bottom of the tongue (42.29%), the space between the back of the tongue and the windpipe entrance (19.40%), the tonsil (18.21%), and the bottom of the throat (10.65%).

source: Conclusion: Video laryngoscopy is a powerful tool for the diagnosis and treatment of throat FBs, allowing for identification of rare locations of FBs as well as refractory FBs.
target: We conclude that using a camera on the tip of a curved blade is a strong tool for the diagnosis and removal of objects in the throat that shouldn't be eaten, allowing for the finding of uncommon locations of objects and of objects not easily removed.

source: Objective: This study was designed to explore the clinical application of video laryngoscopy in the diagnosis and treatment of throat foreign bodies (FBs).
target: This work explored the use of video viewing of the voice box with a small device to identify and treat throat foreign bodies (FBs) or substances.

source: Method: In total, 1572 patients diagnosed with throat FBs at the Department of Otolaryngology of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital were retrospectively analysed.
target: 1572 total patients with throat foreign substances at the Department of Otolarynology of Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital were analysed.

source: The covariables collected were the time from FB ingestion to admission, age, sex, duration of admission, and site of impaction.
target: Time from foreign substance consumption to hospital admission, age, sex, duration of admission, and site of blockage were measured.

source: Result: The most common FBs were fish bones, which accounted for 1446 (91.98%) of 1572 FBs.
target: The most common foreign substance was fish bones, seen in  91.98% of patients.

source: Among all 1572 FBs, 1004 (63.87%) were successfully removed by video laryngoscopy without complications.
target: Among all 1572 foreign substances, 1004 (63.87%) were removed by video throat treatment with a small device without issues.

source: A shorter duration of admission was associated with a higher diagnostic rate under video laryngoscopy.
target: A shorter hospital stay was linked to higher identification rate by video viewing of the voice box with a small device.

source: The diagnostic rate of sharp FBs was significantly higher than that of non-sharp FBs.
target: The identification rate of sharp foreign substances was higher than that of non-sharp ones.

source: The most common sites of throat FBs were the tongue root (42.29%), epiglottic vallecula (19.40%), tonsil (18.21%), and piriform fossa (10.65%).
target: The most common sites of throat foreign substances were areas near the tonsil and bottom of the tongue and throat.

source: Conclusion: Video laryngoscopy is a powerful tool for the diagnosis and treatment of throat FBs, allowing for identification of rare locations of FBs as well as refractory FBs.
target: Video viewing of the voice box with a small device is powerful for identifying and treating throat foreign substances. It allows identification of rare locations of foreign substances and stubborn ones.

source: Achalasia is an esophageal motility disorder characterized by aberrant peristalsis and insufficient relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter.
target: Achalasia describes a disorder in which your esophagus is unable to move food and liquids down into your stomach.

source: Patients most commonly present with dysphagia to solids and liquids, regurgitation, and occasional chest pain with or without weight loss.
target: People often have difficulty swallowing solids and liquids, spitting up undigested or partially digested food from the stomach, and occasional chest pain with or without weight loss.

source: High-resolution manometry has identified 3 subtypes of achalasia distinguished by pressurization and contraction patterns.
target: Measuring the strength and muscle coordination of your esophagus when you swallow led to the finding of 3 subtypes of achalasia that have different pressure and contraction patterns.

source: Endoscopic findings of retained saliva with puckering of the gastroesophageal junction or esophagram findings of a dilated esophagus with bird beaking are important diagnostic clues.
target: A lot of saliva and puckering where the esophagus is connected to the stomach, seen using a flexible tube with a camera, or a widened esophagus and narrowing where the esophagus is connected to the stomach, that looks like a bird's beak, found by X-raying the esophagus suggest achalasia.

source: In this American College of Gastroenterology guideline, we used the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation process to provide clinical guidance on how best to diagnose and treat patients with achalasia.
target: In this guideline, we used a popular method to guide doctors on how best to diagnose and treat people with achalasia.

source: Dysphagia is common but may be underreported.
target: Trouble swallowing is common but may be underreported.

source: Specific symptoms, rather than their perceived location, should guide the initial evaluation and imaging.
