source: Twenty-five patients with a primary DSM-III-R diagnosis of panic disorder with or without agoraphobia were treated openly with the serotonin uptake inhibitor fluoxetine for up to 12 months.
target: Fluoxetine is a type of antidepressant that is also called prozac. Twenty-five patients with a diagnosis of panic disorder, a type of anxiety with unexpected events of intense fear, who either did or did not have agoraphobia (an intense fear of open spaces or leaving the home) are treated openly with fluoxetine for up to 12 months.

source: For most patients, treatment was initiated at 5 mg/day to minimize adverse effects previously reported with initiation at higher doses.
target: For most patients, treatment is started at 5 mg/day to minimize side effects that is connected with starting the medication at a higher dose.

source: Nineteen (76%) experienced moderate to marked improvement in panic attacks.
target: Nineteen (76%) experienced small to major improvement in panic attacks.

source: Four (16%) were unable to tolerate fluoxetine due to adverse effects.
target: Four (16%) were unable to handle fluoxetine because of side effects.

source: Initiating treatment of panic disorder with low doses of fluoxetine may increase its acceptability and permit more patients to benefit from fluoxetine.
target: Starting treatment of panic disorder with low doses of fluoxetine may increase its acceptability and allow more patients to benefit from fluoxetine.

source: Twenty-five patients with a primary DSM-III-R diagnosis of panic disorder with or without agoraphobia were treated openly with the serotonin uptake inhibitor fluoxetine for up to 12 months.
target: Twenty-five patients with panic disorder were treated with the serotonin uptake inhibitor fluoxetine (a common antidepressant) for up to 12 months.

source: For most patients, treatment was initiated at 5 mg/day to minimize adverse effects previously reported with initiation at higher doses.
target: For most, treatment started with 5 mg/day to reduce side effects.

source: Nineteen (76%) experienced moderate to marked improvement in panic attacks.
target: Nineteen (76%) showed improvement in panic attacks.

source: Four (16%) were unable to tolerate fluoxetine due to adverse effects.
target: Four (16%) could not stand fluoxetine due to its side effects.

source: Initiating treatment of panic disorder with low doses of fluoxetine may increase its acceptability and permit more patients to benefit from fluoxetine.
target: Using low starting doses of fluoxetine for panic disorders may improve its acceptability and use.

source: Background: Although several studies have examined the long-term efficacy of antidepressants, relatively little attention has been paid to the management of relapses or recurrences during continued antidepressant treatment.
target: Antidepressants are medications used to treat depression and other disorders and can improve mood, sleep quality, and concentration. Although several studies have examined how well antidepressants work long-term, there is little attention on how to manage relapses (the worsening of a medical condition that had previously improved) or recurrences (when symptoms return months or years after a person has recovered from the last episode) during continued use of antidepressant drugs.

source: This study examined whether depressed patients who had recovered and then relapsed on fluoxetine 20 mg/day would benefit from an increase in fluoxetine dose.
target: This study examined whether depressed patients who had recovered and then relapsed on fluoxetine (a type of antidepressant also called prozac) at 20 mg/day will benefit from an increase dose of fluoxetine.

source: Method: Eighteen patients who relapsed on fluoxetine 20 mg/day during long-term treatment with fluoxetine as part of a placebo-controlled study had their fluoxetine dose raised to 40 mg/day and were followed for at least 1 month (mean time = 4.7 months).
target: In this study, 18 patients who relapsed on fluoxetine 20 mg/day during long-term treatment with fluoxetine as part of a larger study had their fluoxetine dose raised to 40 mg/day and were followed for at least 1 month.

source: Results: Twelve (67%) were full responders, 3 (17%) partial responders, and 3 (17%) dropped out because of side effects (e.g., insomnia and agitation).
target: Twelve (67%) were full responders (patients who reached the expected improvement), 3 (17%) partial responders (patients who reached only part of the expected improvement), and 3 (17%) dropped out of the study because of side effects (e.g. insomnia and agitation).

source: Of those patients who had either full or partial response (N = 15; 83%), 3 complete responders had a recurrence on 40 mg/day after a mean of 5.8 months and 1 partial responder had a recurrence 11 months later.
target: Of those 15 patients who had either full or partial response, 3 complete responders had a recurrence on 40 mg/day after an average of 5.8 months and 1 partial responder had a recurrence 11 months later.

source: Overall, 11 (61%) of 18 patients maintained their response during their follow-up while taking the higher dose of fluoxetine.
target: Overall, 11 (61%) of 18 patients still reacted to the drug during their follow-up while taking the higher dose of fluoxetine.

source: Conclusion: An increase in dose of fluoxetine to 40 mg/day appears to be an effective strategy in the treatment of relapse among depressed patients who had initially responded to fluoxetine 20 mg/day.
target: In conclusion, an increase in dose of fluoxetine to 40 mg/day appears to be an effective way to help treat relapse among people with depression who had initially responded to fluoxetine at 20 mg/day.

source: Fluoxetine (FLX) has a unique pharmacokinetic profile.
target: Fluoxetine is a type of antidepressant that is also called prozac.

source: Its major metabolite, norfluoxetine (NFLX), possesses FLX's antidepressant efficacy and a half-life of 7 to 15 days, suggesting the possibility of nonstandard dosing strategies.
target: There is the possibility that fluoxetine can be given to patients in dose amounts that is different from the regular doses of other medications.

source: This study examined the tolerability of a weekly dose and its equivalence to daily dosing of FLX for the continuation phase of treatment for major depressive disorder (MDD).
target: In order to study the treatment for major depressive disorder, this study examined how well patients handled a weekly dose of fluoxetine and then monitored the same amount but given daily during a following phase of the study.

source: One hundred fourteen subjects initially received open-label treatment with 20 mg of FLX daily for 7 weeks.
target: In this study, 114 patients first receive treatment with 20 mg of fluoxetine daily for 7 weeks.

source: Subsequently, 70 subjects with a score on the Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression (HAM-D) of 12 or less were randomly assigned in a double-blind design to one of three treatment groups: 20 mg FLX daily (N = 21), 60 mg FLX weekly (N = 28), or placebo (N = 21) and were followed for 7 weeks.
target: Next, 70 patients are randomly assigned to one of three treatment groups: 20 mg fluoxetine daily with 21 subjects, 60 mg fluoxetine weekly with 28 patients, or placebo of an inactive substance that looks like the drug with 21patients and are followed for 7 weeks.

source: HAM-D scores and blood levels of FLX and NFLX were analyzed using a repeated-measures analysis of variance.
target: Scores for depression using a rating scale and blood levels of fluoxetine and norfluoxetine, another form of fluoxetine, are analyzed.

source: During the double-blind phase, blood levels for both FLX and NFLX differed across the treatment groups, yet no statistically significant difference in HAM-D scores was observed.
target: Blood levels for both fluoxetine and norfluoxetine differ across the three treatment groups, but there are no major differences in the scores for depression observed.

source: There was no difference in the dropout rate across the groups.
target: There is no difference in the rate of patients who dropped out of the study across the groups.

source: Subjects could not correctly identify the treatment group into which they were assigned.
target: Patients cannot correctly identify the treatment group into which they were assigned.

source: Weekly dosing of FLX seems to be well tolerated and possibly as effective as daily dosing in maintaining the therapeutic response in subjects with MDD.
target: Weekly dosing of fluoxetine seems to be handled well by patients and is  possibly as effective as daily dosing for treating patients with major depressive disorder.

source: Objective: Although continuing antidepressant treatment after patients have responded to medication has been shown to greatly reduce the risk of relapse, this risk is not eliminated.
target: Continuing treatment with antidepressants after patients have responded to medication has been shown to greatly reduce the risk of relapse, which is when a medical condition that had previously improved starts to worsen. However, the risk of a relapse is not completely eliminated.

source: A number of theories have been proposed to account for this apparent loss of efficacy.
target: A number of theories have been suggested to account for this possible decline in how well antidepressant medicines work.

source: A common initial approach to managing relapse is to increase the dose of antidepressant.
target: A common first approach to help a patient who is relapsing is to increase the dose of the antidepressant medicine.

source: We prospectively evaluated the likelihood of response to increasing the fluoxetine doses in patients relapsing during a long-term efficacy study of two fluoxetine dosing regimens.
target: Researchers evaluated the chance patients will respond to an increase in the dose of fluoxetine (a type of antidepressant also called prozac) in patients relapsing during a long-term study of two different dosing plans of fluoxetine.

source: Method: Patients meeting the DSM-IV criteria for major depressive disorder with modified HAMD17 scores > or =18 and CGI-severity scores > or =4 were treated for 13 weeks with open-label 20 mg/day fluoxetine in a multicenter US study.
target: Patients who have major depressive disorder are treated for 13 weeks with 20 mg/day of fluoxetine in a large US study.

source: Responders (n = 501) were randomized to 20 mg fluoxetine daily, placebo, or 90 mg enteric-coated fluoxetine weekly for 25 weeks of double-blind continuation treatment.
target: The 501 patients who responded to the medicine are randomly put into either the group receiving 20 mg/day of fluoxetine, the group receiving sham treatment that has the appearance of the drug but is not the real medicine, or 90 mg of fluoxetine weekly. They will be in these groups for 25 weeks during a part of the study called the continuation phase.

source: If the patients relapsed during the continuation phase, they were offered a 25-week optional rescue treatment phase during which the study medication dose was increased as follows: (1) patients on placebo had treatment with fluoxetine 20 mg/day reinitiated, (2) patients on fluoxetine 20 mg/day had their dose increased to 40 mg/day, and (3) patients on a 90-mg weekly dose had their dose increased to 90 mg twice a week.
target: If the patients relapse during the continuation phase, they are offered a different 25-week treatment plan called a rescue phase where the study medication dose is increased as 1) patients on placebo will restart fluoxetine at 20 mg/day, 2) patients on fluoxetine 20 mg/day will have their dose increased to 40 mg/day, and 3) patients on the 90-mg weekly dose will have their dose increased to 90-mg twice a week.

source: The results of the rescue phase for the latter two groups who relapsed while on continuation treatment with fluoxetine are reported.
target: The results of the rescue phase the patients in group 2 (who received 20 mg/day) and group 3 (who received the 90-mg weekly).

source: Response was defined as a 50% reduction in the modified HAMD17 score since time of relapse and a CGI-severity score < or =2.
target: Researchers used scoring tools that measure depression severity and seeing how much the depression scores are reduced since the time of the last relapse.

source: Additional efficacy analyses included HAMD and CGI-severity changes from baseline to endpoint.
target: Additional analyses to evaluate how well the medicine worked include changes in these scoring tools from the start of the study to the end.

source: Safety measures included assessment of treatment-emergent adverse events, vital signs, and laboratory measures.
target: Safety checks include assessment of unexpected side effects, vital signs (e.g. blood pressure, temperature), and tests done in a lab.

source: Results: Overall, patients relapsing during the continuation treatment responded to an increased dose (57% of the 40-mg-daily group and 72% of the enteric-coated 90-mg-twice-weekly group).
target: Overall, patients relapsing during the continuation treatment phase responded to an increased dose (57% of the 40-mg-daily group and 72% of the 90-mg-twice-weekly group).

source: Mean modified HAMD17 scores decreased from a mean of approximately 20 to below 8 and were maintained for up to 6 months in the responders.
target: The average score of the tool that measure depression severity decreased and was maintained for up to 6 months in the people who responded.

source: Thirty-five percent of patients either did not respond or initially responded but again relapsed after augmentation of medication.
target: There are 35 patients who either did not respond or responded at first but then relapsed after increasing medication.

source: Conclusions: The patients relapsing after initially responding to fluoxetine can benefit from an increase in fluoxetine dose.
target: In conclusion, patients who relapsed after first responding to fluoxetine can benefit from an increase in the dose amount of fluoxetine.

source: These results also generally support increasing dose as a first-line treatment strategy for a patient who has relapsed while taking a previously effective dose of an antidepressant.
target: These results also generally support increasing the dose as a first-line treatment plan for a patient who has relapsed while taking a previously effective dose of an antidepressant.

source: Increasing enteric-coated fluoxetine 90 mg once weekly to twice weekly appeared to be as well-tolerated and effective in restoring response as increasing a daily fluoxetine dose from 20 to 40 mg.
target: Increasing fluoxetine 90 mg once weekly to twice weekly appears to have side effects that patients can handle and is just as effective in restoring response as when daily fluoxetine dose is increased from 20 mg to 40 mg.

source: Patients with major depressive disorder remain at risk for relapse following remission and often continue to experience subthreshold symptoms.
target: Patients with major depressive disorder (a mental health disorder where a person is in a constant depressed mood or has a loss of interest in activities) remain at risk for relapse (the worsening of a medical condition that had previously improved) following remission (a period of improvement where the patient is mostly asymptomatic) and often continue to experience subthreshold symptoms in which a person has  symptoms but the symptoms do not meet the full criteria for a medical diagnosis.

source: This study examined whether depressed patients who had recovered and then relapsed on fluoxetine 20 mg/day would benefit from an increase in fluoxetine dose.
target: This study examines whether depressed patients who had recovered and then relapsed while on fluoxetine (a type of antidepressant also called prozac) at 20 mg/day would benefit from an increase in fluoxetine dose.

source: A total of 132 outpatients with major depressive disorder who achieved remission with 8 weeks of treatment with fluoxetine 20 mg had the dose increased to 40 mg.
target: A total of 132 patients with major depressive disorder who achieved remission with 8 weeks of treatment with fluoxetine at 20 mg had the dose increased to 40mg.

source: They were randomized to receive cognitive therapy or medication management alone and were followed for up to 28 weeks for depressive relapse and change in depressive symptoms.
target: They are randomly put in groups to receive cognitive therapy (a type of behavior therapy that focuses on challenging negative thoughts that can worsen emotional difficulties) or to receive medication only. Patients are followed for up to 28 weeks to check for depressive relapse and changes in depressive symptoms.

source: A total of 47 (35.6%) out of 132 patients did not complete the 28-week continuation phase.
target: A total of 47 out of 132 patients did not complete the 28 weeks phase of the study.

source: Rates of discontinuation or relapse did not differ significantly between the groups.
target: Rates of stopping  the drug or relapsing did not differ significantly between the group receiving cognitive therapy and the group only receiving medication.

source: Change in residual symptoms or wellbeing as measured by Hamilton Depression Scale score or Symptom Questionnaire self-report also did not differ between groups.
target: Changes in subthreshold symptoms or wellbeing did not differ between groups.

source: In this sample of outpatients in continuation phase treatment for major depressive disorder, the combination of cognitive therapy and fluoxetine 40 mg failed to yield any significant benefit in symptoms or relapse rates over fluoxetine 40 mg alone during 28 weeks of follow-up.
target: In this sample of patients being treated for major depressive disorder, the combination of cognitive therapy and fluoxetine at 40 mg failed to lead to any significant benefit in symptoms or in relapse rates over fluoxetine 40 mg alone during the 28 week follow-up period.

source: Patients with major depressive disorder remain at risk for relapse following remission and often continue to experience subthreshold symptoms.
target: Those with depression may fall back after temporary improvement and still experience minor symptoms.

source: This study examined whether depressed patients who had recovered and then relapsed on fluoxetine 20 mg/day would benefit from an increase in fluoxetine dose.
target: This work compares the rate of re-suffering depression and the frequency of depressive symptoms between patients treated with fluoxetine (an antidepressant medication) alone or with thought therapy.

source: A total of 132 outpatients with major depressive disorder who achieved remission with 8 weeks of treatment with fluoxetine 20 mg had the dose increased to 40 mg.
target: 132 patients with temporary improvement from depression after 8 weeks with 20 mg of fluoxetine had the dose increased to 40 mg.

source: They were randomized to receive cognitive therapy or medication management alone and were followed for up to 28 weeks for depressive relapse and change in depressive symptoms.
target: The patients randomly received either cognitive or thought therapy or medication management and were monitored for changes in symptoms or regression for 28 weeks.

source: A total of 47 (35.6%) out of 132 patients did not complete the 28-week continuation phase.
target: 47 patients did not complete the 28-week phase.

source: Rates of discontinuation or relapse did not differ significantly between the groups.
target: Rates of non-completion or regression did not differ between groups.

source: Change in residual symptoms or wellbeing as measured by Hamilton Depression Scale score or Symptom Questionnaire self-report also did not differ between groups.
target: Change in depressive symptoms did not differ between groups.

source: In this sample of outpatients in continuation phase treatment for major depressive disorder, the combination of cognitive therapy and fluoxetine 40 mg failed to yield any significant benefit in symptoms or relapse rates over fluoxetine 40 mg alone during 28 weeks of follow-up.
target: In this work, cognitive or thought therapy with 40 mg of fluoxetine did not give additional benefit compared to fluoxetine 40 mg alone during the 28 weeks of follow-up.

source: Pediatric generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterized by excessive and uncontrollable worry about a variety of events and is accompanied by physical symptoms such as headaches, tension, restlessness, gastrointestinal distress, and heart palpitations.
target: In children, generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) includes having more than normal and uncontrollable worry about different events and is also includes physical symptoms such as headaches, tension, restlessness, digestive problems, and a racing heartbeat.

source: Symptoms impose marked distress and interfere with social, emotional, and educational functioning.
target: Symptoms interfere with social, emotional, and educational experiences.

source: GAD occurs in over 10% of children and adolescents, has an average age of onset of 8.5 years, and is more often reported in girls.
target: GAD occurs in over 10% of children and adolescents and usually starts at the age of 8.5 years. GAD is more often reported in girls.

source: Common co-occurring conditions include separation anxiety disorder and social phobia.
target: Common conditions that occur with GAD include getting very anxious when separated from family or the main caregiver and social phobia, an intense fear of being in front of others or being watched or judged.

source: Assessment involves a multi-informant, multi-method approach involving the child, parents, and school teachers.
target: Multiple methods are used to test for GAD and includes the child, parents, and school teachers.

source: A clinical interview should be conducted to assess for the three primary ways anxiety presents: behaviors, thoughts, and somatic symptoms.
target: An interview is recommended to look for the three main ways anxiety is shown: behaviors, thoughts, and somatic (physical) symptoms.

source: Several semi-structured diagnostic interviews are available, and the Anxiety Disorders Interview Schedule is increasingly used.
target: Several interview formats to make a diagnosis are available. The Anxiety Disorders Interview Schedule is increasingly used.

source: Rating scales completed by the patient, caregivers, and teachers provide useful information for diagnosis and symptom monitoring.
target: Ratings scales are completed by the patient, caregivers, and teachers and provide useful information for diagnosis and symptom monitoring.

source: Several scales are available to assess patients for the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (4th Edition) GAD diagnosis; however, instruments generally cannot distinguish children with GAD from children with similar anxiety disorders.
target: Several scales are able to make a GAD diagnosis which is a diagnosis based on the well-known handbook of mental disorders used by many providers; however, interview tools usually cannot make the distinction between children with GAD and children with similar anxiety disorders.

source: Both cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have demonstrated efficacy for the treatment of pediatric anxiety disorders including GAD.
target: Cognitive-behavioral therapy, which is a type of therapy that focuses on challenging negative thoughts that can worsen emotional difficulties, is shown to be a part positive effect for the treatment of GAD. Additionally, a type of antidepressant called selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) also show benefits for the treatment of pediatric anxiety disorders including GAD.

source: Evidence suggests that the combination of CBT plus sertraline offers additional benefit compared with either treatment alone.
target: Evidence suggests that the combination of cognitive-behavioral therapy plus sertraline (an SSRI medication) offers additional benefit when compared with either treatment alone.

source: With pharmacotherapy, systematic tracking of treatment-emergent adverse events such as headaches, stomach aches, behavioral activation, worsening symptoms, and emerging suicidal thoughts is important.
target: When using medications for treatment, it is important to monitor and track negative side effects that occur such as headaches, stomach aches, behavioral activation, worsening symptoms, and emerging suicidal thoughts.

source: Recommended starting doses are fluvoxamine 25 mg/day, fluoxetine 10 mg/day, and sertraline 25 mg/day, though lower starting doses are possible.
target: Recommended starting doses of SSRIs are fluvoxamine 25 mg/day, fluoxetine 10 mg/day, and sertraline 25 mg/day, though starting with lower doses is possible.

source: Dosing can be adjusted as often as weekly with the goal of achieving a high-quality response, while minimizing side effects.
target: Dosing can be changed as often as weekly with the goal of achieving a high-quality response, while minimizing side effects.

source: Long-term treatment with medication has not been well studied; however, to achieve optimal long-term outcome extended use of medication may be required.
target: Long-term treatment with medication has not been well studied; however, to reach the best long-term outcome, extended use of the medication may be required.

source: It is recommended to continue medication for approximately 1 year following remission in symptoms, and when discontinuing medication to choose a stress-free time of the year.
target: It is recommended to continue medication for about 1 year after remission in symptoms, and to choose a stress-free time of the year when stopping medication.

source: If symptoms return, medication re-initiation should be considered seriously.
target: If symptoms return, restarting the medication should be considered seriously.

source: Background: Depression is the single largest contributor to non-fatal health loss worldwide.
target: Depression is the single largest contributor to non-fatal health loss worldwide.

source: Second-generation antidepressants are the first-line option for pharmacological management of depression.
target: New antidepressants, also called second-generation antidepressants, (medications used to treat depression and other disorders and can improve mood, sleep quality, and concentration) are the first-line option for managing depression using medication.

source: Optimising their use is crucial in reducing the burden of depression; however, debate about their dose dependency and their optimal target dose is ongoing.
target: Their use is important to reduce the burden of depression; however, there is ongoing debate about their dose dependency (when the effects of a drug change when the dose of the drug is changed) and the best target dose (the dose that achieves a target effect).

source: We have aimed to summarise the currently available best evidence to inform this clinical question.
target: We have aimed to summarize the best available evidence to inform this clinical question.

source: Findings: 28 554 records were identified through our search (24 524 published and 4030 unpublished records).
target: Researchers found 28,554 records by searching published and unpublished records.

source: 561 published and 121 unpublished full-text records were assessed for eligibility, and 77 studies were included (19 364 participants; mean age 42·5 years, SD 11·0; 7156 [60·9%] of 11 749 reported were women).
target: Among those records, 561 published and 121 unpublished records that have complete text are reviewed, and 77 are included in the final analysis.

source: For SSRIs (99 treatment groups), the dose-efficacy curve showed a gradual increase up to doses between 20 mg and 40 mg fluoxetine equivalents, and a flat to decreasing trend through the higher licensed doses up to 80 mg fluoxetine equivalents.
target: For SSRIs or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (a type of antidepressant that can increase the amount of serotonin, a mood stabilizer), the dose-efficacy curve (relationship between an effect of a drug and the amount of drug given) showed a gradual increase up to doses between 20mg and 40mg fluoxetine (a type of SSRI also called prozac), and a flat to decreasing trend through the higher doses up to 80 mg fluoxetine equivalents.

source: Dropouts due to adverse effects increased steeply through the examined range.
target: Some patients dropped out due to side effects.

source: The relationship between the dose and dropouts for any reason indicated optimal acceptability for the SSRIs in the lower licensed range between 20 mg and 40 mg fluoxetine equivalents.
target: The relationship between the dose of the drug and dropouts for any reason suggests the best acceptability for using SSRIs is in the lower licensed range between 20 mg and 40 mg fluoxetine equivalents.

source: Venlafaxine (16 treatment groups) had an initially increasing dose-efficacy relationship up to around 75-150 mg, followed by a more modest increase, whereas for mirtazapine (11 treatment groups) efficacy increased up to a dose of about 30 mg and then decreased.
target: Venlafaxine (a type of antidepressant) has an initially increasing  relationship between dose amount and drug performance up to 75-150mg, followed by a more modest increase, whereas for another antidepressant called mirtazapine, performance increased up to a dose of about 30 mg and then decreased.

source: Both venlafaxine and mirtazapine showed optimal acceptability in the lower range of their licensed dose.
target: Both venlafaxine and mirtazapine worked well in the lower range of their licensed dose.

source: These results were robust to several sensitivity analyses.
target: These results performed well in several sensitivity analyses to assess strength and uncertainty in a study.

source: Interpretation: For the most commonly used second-generation antidepressants, the lower range of the licensed dose achieves the optimal balance between efficacy, tolerability, and acceptability in the acute treatment of major depression.
target: For the most common newer antidepressants, the lower range of the dose achieves the best balance between performance, tolerability (the degree to which drugs' negative effects can be handled by patients), and acceptability in the treatment of major depression.

source: Objective: Since several recent meta-analyses report a dose-response relationship for the antidepressant effect of the selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), we investigated how these drugs are dosed in clinical practice.
target: Several analyses using data from multiple studies have found a relationship between the amount of a medicine given (dose) and its effect in a type of antidepressant called selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs). The objective of this study is to investigate how these SSRI drugs are dosed in doctor's offices and health clinics.

source: Methods: Through linkage of nation- or region-wide registers, we describe SSRI doses in 50,365 individuals residing in Region Västra Götaland, Sweden, with an incident diagnosis of depression and initiating SSRI treatment between 2007 and 2016.
target: Researchers describe SSRI doses in 50,365 people living in a specific region in Sweden, with a diagnosis of depression and starting SSRI treatment between 2007 and 2016.

source: The primary question was to elucidate to what extent these individuals had been prescribed a daily dose that according to recent meta-analyses is required to elicit the maximum antidepressant effect, that is >20 mg citalopram, >10 mg escitalopram, >10 mg fluoxetine, >10 mg paroxetine or >50 mg sertraline.
target: The main question is to explain the extent these people are prescribed a daily dose that is expected to get a peak response that would not go higher with a higher dose, which is > 20 mg of citalopram, >10 mg escitalopram, >10 mg fluoxetine, >10 mg paroxetine or >50 mg sertraline - all SSRIs.

source: Results: In all, 21,049 (54%) out of 38,868 individuals <65 years of age, and 9,131 (79%) out of 11,497 individuals ≥65 years of age, never received an SSRI dose reported to exert maximum antidepressant effect.
target: Overall, 21,049 (54%) out of 38,868 individuals <65 years of age, and 9,131 (79%) out of 11,497 individuals ≥65 years of age, never receive an SSRI dose reported to bring about a maximum antidepressant effect.

source: These prescribing practices were seen for citalopram, escitalopram and sertraline, but not for fluoxetine and paroxetine, and were frequent in both primary and secondary/tertiary care.
target: These dosing practices are seen for citalopram, escitalopram and sertraline, but not for fluoxetine and paroxetine, and are frequent in both general care and specialized care.

source: Suggesting that doses here defined as maximum efficacy doses, when prescribed, are usually not intolerable, between 59% and 68% of individuals <65 years of age received such a dose also for the subsequent prescription, that is as frequently as in those prescribed a sub-maximum efficacy dose (52-69%).
target: Between 59% and 68% of individuals <65 years of age received the maximum efficacy dose (the dose where an increased dose given would not lead to improvement) for the next prescription. That percent is as frequently as in those prescribed a sub-maximum efficacy dose which is the dose that has a lower response despite an increase in the dose amount.

source: Conclusion: Most patients being prescribed an SSRI to treat their depression never receive the dose that according to recent meta-analyses is most likely to effectively combat their condition.
target: In conclusion, most patients taking an SSRI to treat their depression never receive the dose that is most likely to effectively fight their condition based on detailed reviews of available data.

source: The lack of consensus regarding effective dosing of SSRIs may have contributed to this state of affairs.
target: The lack of agreement on the effective dose amount of SSRIs may have contributed to this conclusion.

source: Objective: A large number of people experience misophonia.
target: A large number of people experience misophonia (having a strong reaction to specific sounds).

source: In 2013, the Amsterdam Study Group recommended diagnostic criteria for misophonia.
target: In 2013, the Amsterdam Study Group recommended criteria, such as signs and symptoms, to diagnose misophonia.

source: However, misophonia is not yet included in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders.
target: However, misophonia is not yet included in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, a handbook for health care providers to guide the diagnosis of mental disorders.

source: This report is the first report on drug use that directly affects misophonia and demonstrates a 14-year-old adolescent girl with misophonia successfully treated with fluoxetine.
target: This report is the first report on drug use that directly affects misophonia and shows a 14-year old girl with misophonia who is successfully treated with fluoxetine (a type of antidepressant also called prozac).

source: Methods: The patient's misophonia symptoms had been continuing for approximately 2 years, and her quality of life was significantly reduced.
target: The patient's misophonia symptoms had been continuing for approximately 2 years, and her quality of life (a patient's ability to enjoy normal, everyday activities) was significantly reduced.

source: Psychotherapy conditions could not be applied, and fluoxetine 10 mg/d was started and increased to 20 mg/d after a week.
target: Fluoxetine 10 mg/day was started and increased to 20 mg/day after a week.

source: At the second-month follow-up, because of partial improvement, fluoxetine dose was increased to 30 mg/d.
target: At the second-month follow-up with the patient, because of partial improvement, the fluoxetine dose was increased to 30 mg/day.

source: Results: At the fourth-month follow-up, there was a 40% decrease in Amsterdam Misophonia Scale score with a 70% decrease in the children's global assessment scale scores.
target: At the fourth-month follow-up, there was a 40% decrease in Amsterdam Misophonia Scale score (a rating scale to measure how severe symptoms are) with a 70% decrease in the children's global assessment scale scores (a score that measures overall level of functioning).

source: By the 16th week, the overall functionality level was good at the end.
target: By the 16th week, the overall functionality level was good at the end.

source: Conclusions: Fluoxetine may be used as an effective drug in the treatment of misophonia.
target: In conclusion, fluoxetine may be used as an effective drug in the treatment of misophonia.

source: Hiatus hernia refers to conditions in which elements of the abdominal cavity, most commonly the stomach, herniate through the oesophageal hiatus into the mediastinum.
target: Hiatus hernia refers to when the upper part of the stomach pushes up through the diaphragm into the chest.

source: With the most common type (type I or sliding hiatus hernia) this is associated with laxity of the phrenooesophageal membrane and the gastric cardia herniates.
target: The most common type (type 1 or sliding hiatus hernia), looseness of the ligament that attaches the esophagus to the diaphragm, causes the part of the stomach that is closest to the esophagus to push up.

source: Sliding hiatus hernia is readily diagnosed by barium swallow radiography, endoscopy, or manometry when greater than 2 cm in axial span.
target: Sliding hiatus hernia is easily diagnosed by a swallowing test during x-ray, a flexible tube with a light and camera attached, or an esophagus test when more than 2 cm along an axis.

source: However, the mobility of the oesophagogastric junction precludes the reliable detection of more subtle disruption by endoscopy or radiography.
target: However, the movement of the junction between the esophagus and stomach doesn't allow for dependable detection of smaller abnormalities using a flexible tube with a light and camera attached or x-ray.

source: Detecting lesser degrees of axial separation between the lower oesophageal sphincter and crural diaphragm can only be reliably accomplished with high-resolution manometry, a technique that permits real time localization of these oesophagogastric junction components without swallow or distention related artefact.
target: Detecting lesser separation between the esophagus and the diaphragm can only be done reliably with a high-resolution esophagus test, which allows a real time location of the parts of the junction between the esophagus and stomach without misrepresentation due to movement.

source: A Spigelian hernia (or lateral ventral hernia) is a hernia through the spigelian fascia, which is the aponeurotic layer between the rectus abdominis muscle medially, and the semilunar line laterally.
target: A Spigelian hernia (or lateral ventral hernia involving bulging of digestive organs) develops through muscles found in the abdominal wall.

source: So far, about 1000 cases have been reported worldwide.
target: So far, about 1000 of these hernia cases have been seen worldwide.

source: These hernias are difficult to diagnose as they do not present with a subcutaneous swelling and have high risk of going for strangulation.
target: These hernias are hard to diagnose, because they do not cause swelling and often can cut off blood supply to one or more essential organs (e.g., small or large intestine).

source: We discuss the case of a 36 year old female who presented with history of pain and lumpiness in left lower abdomen, both of which decreased on lying down.
target: We look at the case of a 36-year-old female with a history of pain and lumpiness in her left lower stomach, both of which got better when she lay down.

source: She presented to emergency with an episode severe pain at same site which subsided spontaneously.
target: She came to the emergency room with severe pain at the same place that went away on its own.

source: Diagnosis was confirmed on CT scan, plication and onlay prolene mesh repair performed.
target: A CT scan showed a Spigelian hernia, and a doctor performed surgery.

source: Spigelian hernias are rare, interparietal type of hernias which have high risk undergoing strangulation.
target: Spigelian hernias are rare and often can cut off blood supply to one or more essential organs.

source: Knowledge of symptoms and signs is vital to diagnosis and treatment of these rare type of hernias.
target: Knowledge of symptoms and signs is important to diagnose and treat these rare hernias.

source: Purpose: Hiatal hernias are a common finding on radiographic or endoscopic studies.
target: Hiatal hernias, which occur when the upper part of the stomach pushes up through the diaphragm into the chest, are often found by x-ray and using a long, thin tube with a camera to look inside the body.

source: Hiatal hernias may become symptomatic or, less frequently, can incarcerate or become a volvulus leading to organ ischemia.
target: Hiatal hernias may cause symptoms or, less often, can become trapped or twisted, cutting off blood flow to organs.

source: This review examines latest evidence on the diagnostic workup and management of hiatal hernias.
target: This review looks at the latest studies on the diagnosis and treatment of hiatal hernias.

source: Methods: A literature review of contemporary and latest studies with highest quality of evidence was completed.
target: We reviewed the strongest current and most recent studies.

source: This information was examined and compiled in review format.
target: We studied this information and put it in an easily reviewable format.

source: Results: Asymptomatic hiatal and paraesophageal hernias become symptomatic and necessitate repair at a rate of 1% per year.
target: We found that hiatal hernias without symptoms develop symptoms and need repair at a rate of 1% per year.

source: Watchful waiting is appropriate for asymptomatic hernias.
target: Watching without treatment unless symptoms appear or change is suggested for people with hernias with no symptoms.

source: Symptomatic hiatal hernias and those with confirmed reflux disease require operative repair with an anti-reflux procedure.
target: Hiatal hernias with symptoms and those with stomach acid reflux disease need surgery with an anti-reflux procedure.

source: Key operative steps include the following: reduction and excision of hernia sac, 3 cm of intraabdominal esophageal length, crural closure with mesh reinforcement, and an anti-reflux procedure.
target: Surgery has key steps.

source: Repairs not amenable to key steps may undergo gastropexy and gastrostomy placement as an alternative procedure.
target: When these key steps can't be done, stitching the stomach to the diaphragm and inserting a feeding tube is another option.

source: Conclusions: Hiatal hernias are commonly incidental findings.
target: We conclude that hiatal hernias are often found when looking for something else.

source: When hernias become symptomatic or have reflux disease, an operative repair is required.
target: When hernias cause symptoms or have reflux disease, surgery is needed.

source: A minimally invasive approach is safe and has improved outcomes.
target: A minimally invasive treatment is safe and has improved results.

source: Purpose: Hiatal hernias are a common finding on radiographic or endoscopic studies.
target: Hiatal hernias, in which the stomach bulges into the chest, are common clinical findings.

source: Hiatal hernias may become symptomatic or, less frequently, can incarcerate or become a volvulus leading to organ ischemia.
target: Hiatal hernias can lead to unwanted symptoms, trapped or twisted intestines, and reduced blood flow to certain body parts.

source: This review examines latest evidence on the diagnostic workup and management of hiatal hernias.
target: This review examines the latest works on identifying and managing these stomach bulges into the chest.

source: Results: Asymptomatic hiatal and paraesophageal hernias become symptomatic and necessitate repair at a rate of 1% per year.
target: No-symptom stomach or organ bulges into the chest acquire symptoms and need repair at a rate of 1% per year.

source: Watchful waiting is appropriate for asymptomatic hernias.
target: Watchful waiting is appropriate for these no-symptom organ bulges into the chest.

source: Symptomatic hiatal hernias and those with confirmed reflux disease require operative repair with an anti-reflux procedure.
target: Hiatal hernias with unwanted symptoms and stomach acid reflux into throat need special, anti-reflux surgery.

source: Key operative steps include the following: reduction and excision of hernia sac, 3 cm of intraabdominal esophageal length, crural closure with mesh reinforcement, and an anti-reflux procedure.
target: Key surgery steps include: cutting out the bulging sac, 3 cm of the lower esophagus, closing the site with mesh, and an anti-acid reflux operation.

source: Repairs not amenable to key steps may undergo gastropexy and gastrostomy placement as an alternative procedure.
target: Repairs not possible with common surgery may need stitching and opening of the stomach instead.

source: Conclusions: Hiatal hernias are commonly incidental findings.
target: Hiatal hernias are common.

source: When hernias become symptomatic or have reflux disease, an operative repair is required.
target: When organ bulges lead to awful symptoms or stomach acid reflux, surgery is needed.

source: A minimally invasive approach is safe and has improved outcomes.
target: A small incision surgery is safe and has improved outcomes.

source: Inguinal hernia is the most frequently diagnosed hernia and during their lifetime one third of males are diagnosed with an inguinal hernia.
target: Inguinal hernia, a bulging of digestive organs occurring in the groin area, is the most often diagnosed hernia. During their lifetime one third of males are diagnosed with an inguinal hernia.

source: The age distribution is bimodal with the highest incidence in childhood and after 50 years of age.
target: People are most likely to get an inguinal hernia in childhood and after 50 years old.

source: Diagnosis is usually reached through clinical examination of a lump in the inguinal region although some patients can present with intestinal obstruction.
target: Doctors usually diagnose an inguinal hernia by a lump in the groin region although some patients can have blocked intestines.

source: Inguinal hernia repair is the only definitive treatment and is one of the most common surgical procedures performed.
target: Inguinal hernia repair is the only treatment and is one of the most common surgeries done.

source: It is usually performed as an elective procedure in local, spinal or general anasthesia.
target: Surgery is usually non-urgent and done under anesthesia.

source: The repair constitutes of reinforcing the posterior wall of the inguinal canal, often using a polypropylene mesh; either via an open anterior approach or posteriorly from within the abdomen with laparoscopy.
target: The repair involves strengthening the area of the groin, often using a plastic mesh; either by traditional or minimally invasive surgery.

source: The most common complications following a hernia repair are recurrent hernia and chronic -discomfort but recurrence rates have improved with the use of mesh and laparoscopic techniques.
target: The most common complications after a hernia repair are the hernia returning and long-term discomfort, but the rate of a hernia returning has gone down with the use of mesh and minimally invasive surgery.

source: This evidence based review describes the -epidemiology and etiology of inguinal hernia together with the most common surgical procedures; focusing on recent innovations.
target: This data-driven review describes the spread, contributing factors, and causes of inguinal hernias and the most common surgeries, focusing on recent methods.

source: Inguinal hernia is the most frequently diagnosed hernia and during their lifetime one third of males are diagnosed with an inguinal hernia.
target: Inguinal hernias, or organ bulging at the lower ab area, is the most frequent hernia afflicting one third of males.

source: The age distribution is bimodal with the highest incidence in childhood and after 50 years of age.
target: These lower-ab organ bulges occur most frequently in childhood and after 50 years.

source: Diagnosis is usually reached through clinical examination of a lump in the inguinal region although some patients can present with intestinal obstruction.
target: Clinicians identify these inguinal hernias by a lump in the lower-ab area or when patients have intestinal blockage.

source: Inguinal hernia repair is the only definitive treatment and is one of the most common surgical procedures performed.
target: Surgery of the lower-ab organ bulge is the only treatment and is very common.

source: It is usually performed as an elective procedure in local, spinal or general anasthesia.
target: Surgery of the lower-ab organ bulge is usually performed with anasthesia.

source: The repair constitutes of reinforcing the posterior wall of the inguinal canal, often using a polypropylene mesh; either via an open anterior approach or posteriorly from within the abdomen with laparoscopy.
target: The surgery involves reinforcing the lower abdomen wall with mesh.

source: The most common complications following a hernia repair are recurrent hernia and chronic -discomfort but recurrence rates have improved with the use of mesh and laparoscopic techniques.
target: While long-lasting discomfort and reappearing hernias or organ bulges may follow a hernia repair, the rate of reappearance has improved with mesh and small-incisions.

source: This evidence based review describes the -epidemiology and etiology of inguinal hernia together with the most common surgical procedures; focusing on recent innovations.
target: The article reviews the spread and causes of inguinal hernia along with common surgical treatments.

source: Purpose: The aim of the study was to determine which diagnostic modality [Computerized Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), or ultrasound (US)] is more precise in terms of sensitivity and specificity in diagnosing inguinal hernia and sub-type of inguinal hernia (direct or indirect).
target: We aimed to find which diagnostic tool (CT, MRI, or ultrasound) is more accurate for detection and no detection in diagnosing a hernia (bulging of organs) in the groin area and sub-type (direct or indirect; based on location within the groin).

source: Results: Bubble charts depicting the size of each patient cohort and percentual range for both sensitivity and specificity showed that US was better than CT and MRI in diagnosing inguinal hernia.
target: Results showed that ultrasound was better than CT and MRI in accuracy for detection and no detection in diagnosing a hernia in the groin region.

source: Bubble charts for US and CT depicted high values within the studies that reported sensitivity and specificity in diagnosing type of hernia.
target: Results showed ultrasound and CT were very accurate for detection and no detection in diagnosing type of hernia.

source: Conclusions: We found that US had the highest sensitivity and specificity.
target: We found that ultrasound was the most accurate for detection and no detection.

source: However, it must be taken into consideration that performance is highly dependent on the operator's level of expertise.
target: One must remember that the expertise level of the person using the diagnostic tool can determine how well the tool works.

source: Based on this systematic review, ultrasound may be the preferred imaging modality when physical examination is inconclusive, given that local expertise in performing US examination for hernia disease is adequate.
target: Based on this review, ultrasound may be the best option when physical exam is uncertain, given that ultrasound is done acceptably at the local level.

source: Groin hernias are caused by a defect of the abdominal wall in the groin area and comprise inguinal and femoral hernias.
target: Digestive organs (e.g., intestines) coming out through a weak point or tear in the abdomen causes groin hernias and includes inguinal (groin area) and femoral (upper thigh area) hernias.

source: Inguinal hernias are more common in men.
target: Inguinal hernias happen more in men.

source: Although groin hernias are easily diagnosed on physical examination in men, ultrasonography is often needed in women.
target: Although groin hernias are easily seen in a physical exam in men, ultrasound is often needed in women.

source: Ultrasonography is also helpful when a recurrent hernia, surgical complication after repair, or other cause of groin pain (e.g., groin mass, hydrocele) is suspected.
target: Ultrasound is also helpful when a doctor thinks a hernia has returned, there is a complication after surgery to repair the hernia, or there is another cause of pain (e.g., groin mass, fluid collection in the scrotum).

source: Magnetic resonance imaging has higher sensitivity and specificity than ultrasonography and is useful for diagnosing occult hernias if clinical suspicion is high despite negative ultrasound findings.
target: MRI has higher accuracy for detection and no detection than ultrasound and is useful for diagnosing hidden hernias if a doctor thinks it is hernia even if an ultrasound does not show one.

source: Herniography, which involves injecting contrast media into the hernial sac, may be used in selected patients.
target: Injecting a substance into the hernia sac to improve visibility in x-rays may be used in some patients.

source: Becoming familiar with the common types of surgical interventions can help family physicians facilitate postoperative care and assess for complications, including recurrence.
target: Learning about the common types of surgeries can help family doctors improve care after surgery and look for complications, including the hernia returning.

source: Laparoscopic repair is associated with shorter recovery time, earlier resumption of activities of daily living, less pain, and lower recurrence rates than open repair.
target: Minimally invasive repair has a shorter recovery time, earlier return to daily activities, less pain, and lower return rates than traditional repair.

source: Watchful waiting is a reasonable and safe option in men with asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic inguinal hernias.
target: Men with inguinal hernias and with few to no symptoms can reasonably and safely watch the hernia without treatment unless symptoms appear or change.

source: Watchful waiting is not recommended in patients with symptomatic hernias or in nonpregnant women.
target: Watching without treatment unless symptoms appear or change is not suggested in people with hernias with symptoms or in nonpregnant women.

source: Introduction and importance: A Littre's hernia (LH) is defined by the presence of Meckel's diverticulum (MD) in any kind of hernia sac.
target: A Littre's hernia (LH) is characterized by Meckel's diverticulum (MD; a pouch on the wall of the lower part of the small intestine present at birth) in any kind of hernia or bulging organ sac.

source: Preoperative diagnosis of LH is a challenge because of its rarity and the absence of specific radiological findings and clinical presentation.
target: Diagnosing LH before surgery is difficult because it is rare and lacks distinguishing features on x-ray and exam.

source: Surgery is the appropriate treatment of complicated LH that is an extremely rare condition with approximately 50 cases reported in the literature over the past 300 years.
target: Surgery is the best treatment for inflamed LH, a very rare condition with about 50 cases reported in scientific papers over the past 300 years.

source: Case presentation: A 46-year-old Caucasian female was admitted to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of abdominal pain.
target: A 46-year-old white woman went to the emergency room after two days of stomach pain.

source: Physical examination revealed an irreducible and painfull mass in umbilical region.
target: Physical exam showed an irreversible and painful lump near the belly button.

source: Abdominal computed tomography scan showed the protrusion of greater omentum and small bowel loop through the umbilical ring.
target: Stomach CT scan showed the tissue that covers the intestines and loop of the small intestine pushing out through the muscle that surrounds the belly button.

source: Laboratory tests were unremarkable.
target: Lab tests were normal.

source: After diagnosis of strangulated umbilical hernia, the patient underwent exploratory laparotomy: the irreducible umbilical hernial sac was opened with presence of incarcerated and strangulated omentum and uncomplicated MD.
target: After diagnosis of a hernia near the belly button cutting off blood supply, the patient had abdominal surgery. Doctors opened the irreversible hernia sac and saw tissue that covers the intestines trapped and cut off from blood supply along with uninflamed MD.

source: Resection of incarcerated and ischemic greater omentum alone was performed.

source: Clinical discussion: Meckel's diverticulum (MD) is a vestigial remnant of the omphalomesenteric duct, representing the most common congenital malformation of the gastrointestinal tract.

source: Acute traumatic abdominal wall hernia (TAWH) is a rare type of hernia that occurs after a low or high velocity impact of the abdominal wall against a blunt object with few cases reported.
target: Acute traumatic abdominal wall hernia (TAWH) is a rare type of hernia that happens when the stomach wall hits a smooth object at low or high speed with few cases reported.

source: Perforations of the hollow viscera commonly follow abdominal trauma and likely require surgery for hemorrhage and sepsis source control.

source: We report a case where a high velocity impact of the abdominal wall against the stump of a felled tree caused a TAWH with concomitant gastric perforation in a 20-year-old male patient who required exploratory laparotomy with primary repair of the stomach and fascia.

source: Internal hernias are rare, and a delayed diagnosis can lead to dangerous complications.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a large number of mutations at the phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) locus, most of which are strongly associated with specific RFLP or VNTR haplotypes.
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a disorder inherited from both parents caused by many changes at the location of the phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) protein. It is a disorder in which the body cannot properly turn food into energy because it cannot process a molecule (phenylalanine).

source: One of the major questions remaining in PKU research is why this apparently maladaptive disorder has been maintained at a frequency of approximately 1 in 10,000 among Caucasians.
target: One of the main questions scientists still have is why PKU happens in roughly 1 in 10,000 white people at any point in time.

source: A growing number of studies have provided evidence that both the relatively high frequency of PKU and the strong mutation/haplotype associations might reflect the existence of multiple founding populations for PKU.
target: More and more studies have suggested that both the high amount of the population with PKU at any point in time and the strong gene changes that go with this set of changes and variations might show more than one group of people in which PKU first developed.

source: Examples of putative founding populations for PKU in both Europe and Asia will be presented.
target: We will show populations thought to be where PKU first developed in both Europe and Asia.

source: Some PAH mutations are associated with multiple haplotypes, suggesting recurrence.
target: Some PAH changes go with many sets of gene variations, suggesting these changes happen more than once.

source: Evidence for and against recurrence as the mechanism responsible for the association of the R408W mutation with RFLP haplotypes 1 and 2 will be discussed.
target: We will discuss information that supports and rejects these changes happening more than once, causing one change to happen in two sets of gene changes.

source: "Inborn errors of metabolism," first recognized 100 years ago by Garrod, were seen as transforming evidence for chemical and biological individuality.
target: "Inborn errors of metabolism," or inherited disorders in which the body cannot properly turn food into energy, first seen by a doctor 100 years ago, were important support for the idea that people have different chemical and biological makeups.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU), a Mendelian autosomal recessive phenotype, was identified in 1934 by Asbjörn Fölling.
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU), a disorder inherited from both parents, was discovered in 1934 by a doctor.

source: It is a disease with impaired postnatal cognitive development resulting from a neurotoxic effect of hyperphenylalaninemia (HPA).
target: It causes intellectual disability from damage to the brain caused by too much phenylalanine in the blood.

source: Its metabolic phenotype is accountable to multifactorial origins both in nurture, where the normal nutritional experience introduces L-phenylalanine, and in nature, where mutations (>500 alleles) occur in the phenylalanine hydroxylase gene (PAH) on chromosome 12q23.2 encoding the L-phenylalanine hydroxylase enzyme (EC 1.14.16.1).
target: Observable PKU symptoms vary due to nurture, where normal foods contain L-phenylalanine, and in nature, where many changes happen in the gene for phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) that controls the L-phenylalanine hydroxylase protein, which helps convert phenylalanine into energy.

source: The PAH enzyme converts phenylalanine to tyrosine in the presence of molecular oxygen and catalytic amounts of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), its nonprotein cofactor.
target: The PAH protein converts phenylalanine, one molecule, to tyrosine, another molecule.

source: PKU is among the first of the human genetic diseases to enter, through newborn screening, the domain of public health, and to show a treatment effect.
target: Through checks for conditions that affect newborns, PKU is one of the first inherited diseases to be prevented in the community with successful treatments.

source: This effect caused a paradigm shift in attitudes about genetic disease.
target: Successful treatments caused a change in thinking about inherited disease.

source: The PKU story contains many messages, including: a framework on which to appreciate the complexity of PKU in which phenotype reflects both locus-specific and genomic components; what the human PAH gene tells us about human population genetics and evolution of modern humans; and how our interest in PKU is served by a locus-specific mutation database (http://www.pahdb.mcgill.ca; last accessed 20 March 2007).
target: The history of PKU provides a lot of information including a way to grasp how complicated the disease is and where observable symptoms depend on both gene location and makeup; what the human PAH gene tells us about the genetics and evolution of humans; and how our understanding of PKU is helped by a database on where gene changes happen.

source: The individual Mendelian PKU phenotype has no "simple" or single explanation; every patient has her/his own complex PKU phenotype and will be treated accordingly.
target: Observable PKU symptoms have no "simple" or single explanation; every patient is different and will be treated at an individual level.

source: Knowledge about PKU reveals genomic components of both disease and health.
target: PKU knowledge shows that genes play a role in both disease and health.

source: "Inborn errors of metabolism," first recognized 100 years ago by Garrod, were seen as transforming evidence for chemical and biological individuality.
target: "Inborn errors of metabolism," first identified 100 years ago by Garrod, are metabolic errors from birth and important evidence for chemical and biological individuality.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU), a Mendelian autosomal recessive phenotype, was identified in 1934 by Asbjörn Fölling.
target: Asbjörn Fölling identified phenylketonuria (PKU), an inherited physical disorder, in 1934.

source: It is a disease with impaired postnatal cognitive development resulting from a neurotoxic effect of hyperphenylalaninemia (HPA).
target: PKU impairs cognitive development due to toxic buildup of a specific molecule, phenylalanine.

source: Its metabolic phenotype is accountable to multifactorial origins both in nurture, where the normal nutritional experience introduces L-phenylalanine, and in nature, where mutations (>500 alleles) occur in the phenylalanine hydroxylase gene (PAH) on chromosome 12q23.2 encoding the L-phenylalanine hydroxylase enzyme (EC 1.14.16.1).
target: The metabolic characteristics of PKU occur due to nurture, in which a normal diet includes the molecule L-phenylalanine, and nature, where DNA mutations influence the gene that creates the L-phenylalanine breakdown enzyme.

source: The PAH enzyme converts phenylalanine to tyrosine in the presence of molecular oxygen and catalytic amounts of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), its nonprotein cofactor.
target: The phenylalanine breakdown enzyme converts PKU into another molecule, tyrosine, using oxygen and non-protein helpers.

source: PKU is among the first of the human genetic diseases to enter, through newborn screening, the domain of public health, and to show a treatment effect.
target: PKU is among the first genetic diseases to enter, via newborn testing, publich health and demonstrate the use of successful treatment.

source: This effect caused a paradigm shift in attitudes about genetic disease.
target: This success of treatment has shifted attitudes about genetic diseases.

source: The PKU story contains many messages, including: a framework on which to appreciate the complexity of PKU in which phenotype reflects both locus-specific and genomic components; what the human PAH gene tells us about human population genetics and evolution of modern humans; and how our interest in PKU is served by a locus-specific mutation database (http://www.pahdb.mcgill.ca; last accessed 20 March 2007).
target: PKU shows how DNA and genes affect physical traits, how modern humans have evolved, and how genetic databases now exist to document genetic mutations.

source: The individual Mendelian PKU phenotype has no "simple" or single explanation; every patient has her/his own complex PKU phenotype and will be treated accordingly.
target: Every individual has unique, physical characteristics involving PKU that require unique treatment.

source: Knowledge about PKU reveals genomic components of both disease and health.
target: PKU shows the genetic components of disease and health.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU), a Mendelian autosomal recessive phenotype (OMIM 261600), is an inborn error of metabolism that can result in impaired postnatal cognitive development.
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU), a disorder inherited from both parents, is when the body cannot properly turn food into energy and can cause intellectual disability.

source: The phenotypic outcome is multifactorial in origin, based both in nature, the mutations in the gene encoding the L-phenylalanine hydroxylase enzyme, and nurture, the nutritional experience introducing L-phenylalanine into the diet.
target: Observable PKU symptoms vary, due to nature, the changes in the gene for the L-phenylalanine hydroxylase protein which converts L-phenylalanine to energy, and nurture, the amount of L-phenylalanine eaten.

source: The PKU story contains many messages including a framework to appreciate the complexity of this disease where phenotype reflects both locus-specific and genomic components.
target: The history of PKU provides a lot of information including a way to grasp how complicated the disease is and where observable symptoms depend on both gene location and makeup.

source: This knowledge is now being applied in the development of patient-specific therapies.
target: This information is now being used to come up with treatments designed for an individual patient.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU), a Mendelian autosomal recessive phenotype (OMIM 261600), is an inborn error of metabolism that can result in impaired postnatal cognitive development.
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU), an inheritable disorder, is an inborn (i.e, existing from birth) error of metabolism that can lead to impaired cognitive development.

source: The phenotypic outcome is multifactorial in origin, based both in nature, the mutations in the gene encoding the L-phenylalanine hydroxylase enzyme, and nurture, the nutritional experience introducing L-phenylalanine into the diet.
target: The physical effects of PKU come from nature, with DNA mutations in a gene that makes a certain protein, and nurture, with the protein L-phenylalanine in food.

source: The PKU story contains many messages including a framework to appreciate the complexity of this disease where phenotype reflects both locus-specific and genomic components.
target: PKU demonstrates the complexity of a disease where physical traits reflect genetic components.

source: This knowledge is now being applied in the development of patient-specific therapies.
target: Patient-specific therapies are being developed for diseases like PKU.

source: Detection of individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU), an autosomal recessively inherited disorder in phenylalanine degradation, is straightforward and efficient due to newborn screening programs.
target: Checks for conditions that affect newborns make finding individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU), a disorder inherited from both parents in which the body cannot properly turn a substance (phenylalanine) into energy, easy and effective.

source: A recent introduction of the pharmacological treatment option emerged rapid development of molecular testing.
target: A recent drug treatment option brought fast development of a lab method that checks for certain genes, proteins, or other molecules that may be a sign of a disease.

source: However, variants responsible for PKU do not all suppress enzyme activity to the same extent.
target: However, genetic alternations that cause PKU do not equally reduce protein activity.

source: A spectrum of over 850 variants, gives rise to a continuum of hyperphenylalaninemia from very mild, requiring no intervention, to severe classical PKU, requiring urgent intervention.
target: A range of over 850 genetic alterations causes elevated phenylalanine blood levels from very mild with no treatment to severe PKU, which needs immediate treatment.

source: Locus-specific and genotypes database are today an invaluable resource of information for more efficient classification and management of patients.
target: Databases of physical gene locations and alterations carried in a gene allow effective classification and treatment of patients.

source: The high-tech molecular methods allow patients' genotype to be obtained in a few days, especially if each laboratory develops a panel for the most frequent variants in the corresponding population.
target: The lab method that checks for certain genes, proteins, or other molecules determines a patient's genetic alterations in a few days, especially if each lab comes up with a test to look for alterations in more than one gene for the most common alterations in a group of people.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an autosomal recessive inborn error of metabolism caused by a deficiency in the hepatic enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH).
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a disorder inherited from both parents in which the body cannot properly turn food into energy due to lack of a specific protein (phenylalanine hydroxylase, PAH) in the liver.

source: If left untreated, the main clinical feature is intellectual disability.
target: PKU can cause intellectual disability if not treated.

source: Treatment, which includes a low Phe diet supplemented with amino acid formulas, commences soon after diagnosis within the first weeks of life.
target: Treatment, which includes a low phenylalanine diet with formulas containing molecules that form proteins, begins soon after diagnosis within the first weeks of life.

source: Although dietary treatment has been successful in preventing intellectual disability in early treated PKU patients, there are major issues with dietary compliance due to palatability of the diet.
target: Although treatment diets prevent intellectual disability in PKU patients treated early, many patients do not follow the diets because they do not taste good.

source: Other potential issues associated with dietary therapy include nutritional deficiencies especially vitamin D and B12.
target: Treatment diets also may lack certain nutrients, especially vitamin D and B12.

source: Suboptimal outcomes in cognitive and executive functioning have been reported in patients who adhere poorly to dietary therapy.
target: Patients who do not follow the diets generally show poorer performance in some mental skills.

source: There have been continuous attempts at improving the quality of medical foods including their palatability.
target: Work has been done to improve the quality of medical diets including their taste.

source: Advances in dietary therapy such as the use of large neutral amino acids (LNAA) and glycomacropeptides (GMP; found within the whey fraction of bovine milk) have been explored.
target: Using certain molecules that form certain proteins are examples of ways to improve medical diets.

source: Gene therapy and enzyme replacement or substitution therapy have yielded more promising data in the recent years.
target: Transferring genetic material into a patient's cell and replacing or substituting a substance that is lacking in the PKU patient's body have been successful in recent years.

source: In this review the current and possible future treatments for PKU are discussed.
target: In this review, we discuss current and possible future treatments for PKU.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an autosomal recessive inborn error of metabolism caused by a deficiency in the hepatic enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH).
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inherited, metabolic disorder caused by a lack of a specific liver enzyme or protein.

source: If left untreated, the main clinical feature is intellectual disability.
target: If left untreated, phenylketonuria may lead to intellectual disability.

source: Treatment, which includes a low Phe diet supplemented with amino acid formulas, commences soon after diagnosis within the first weeks of life.
target: Treating PKU includes a diet low in the molecule Phenylalanine and completed with other molecules. The diet starts within the first weeks of life after identification.

source: Although dietary treatment has been successful in preventing intellectual disability in early treated PKU patients, there are major issues with dietary compliance due to palatability of the diet.
target: While dietary treatment prevents intellectual disability in patients with early treated PKU, the diet is hard to follow due to its taste.

source: Other potential issues associated with dietary therapy include nutritional deficiencies especially vitamin D and B12.
target: Other issues with dietary treatment include deficiencies in vitamin D and B12.

source: Suboptimal outcomes in cognitive and executive functioning have been reported in patients who adhere poorly to dietary therapy.
target: Patients that do not follow the diet experience low cognitive function.

source: There have been continuous attempts at improving the quality of medical foods including their palatability.
target: There have been many attempts to improve the diet including its taste.

source: Advances in dietary therapy such as the use of large neutral amino acids (LNAA) and glycomacropeptides (GMP; found within the whey fraction of bovine milk) have been explored.
target: Changes like using new amino acids and protein units have been explored.

source: Gene therapy and enzyme replacement or substitution therapy have yielded more promising data in the recent years.
target: Gene therapy and enzyme replacement have been more promising recently.

source: In this review the current and possible future treatments for PKU are discussed.
target: This article reviews current and possible treatments for PKU.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an autosomal recessive inborn error of phenylalanine metabolism caused by deficiency in the enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase that converts phenylalanine into tyrosine.
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a disorder inherited from both parents in which the body cannot properly turn food into energy. This is caused by not enough of a specific protein (phenylalanine hydroxylase, PAH) that changes one molecule into another molecule.

source: If left untreated, PKU results in increased phenylalanine concentrations in blood and brain, which cause severe intellectual disability, epilepsy and behavioural problems.
target: If not treated, PKU causes higher phenylalanine levels in the blood and brain, which causes intellectual disability, seizure disorder and behavioral problems.

source: PKU management differs widely across Europe and therefore these guidelines have been developed aiming to optimize and standardize PKU care.
target: The European guidelines on PKU were made to improve and reduce the variation or differences in care because PKU treatment differs a lot across Europe.

source: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an inborn error of metabolism (IEM) most often caused by missense mutations in the gene encoding phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) which catalyzes the hydroxylation of phenylalanine (Phe) generating tyrosine (Tyr).
target: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a disorder in which the body cannot properly turn food into energy often caused by not enough of a specific protein (phenylalanine hydroxylase, PAH) that changes one molecule (phenylalanine, Phe) into another molecule (tyrosine, Tyr).

source: PKU belongs to a class of amino acid aminoacidopathies termed “toxic accumulation-IEMs” where the circulating toxin is an amino acid or its metabolites.
target: PKU is a type of molecular (amino acid) metabolism disorder called "toxic accumulation IEMs," in which the buildup of the molecule is toxic.

source: Mutations in an enzyme, such as PAH, are recessive since one functioning enzyme with the wild-type allele is sufficient.
target: Changes to DNA of a protein, such as PAH, are made in both copies of the gene that is altered, because one working copy of the gene allows the protein to function.

source: PAH is primarily a hepatic enzyme.
target: PAH is mainly a liver protein.

source: Elevated blood Phe levels and decreased Tyr levels characterize PKU.
target: PKU is associated with high blood Phe levels and low Tyr levels.

source: Newborns with PKU can appear normal at birth with the first signs appearing after several months.
