target: A specific gentoype for MTHFR is linked with higher BP regardless of homocysteine, a molecule that MTHFR acts on, and biases adults to increased risk of high BP and poorer BP control with anti-high-BP treatment. Better riboflavin amount is linked with reduced genetic risk.

source: Riboflavin intervention may thus offer a personalised approach to prevent the onset of hypertension in adults with the TT genotype; however, this requires confirmation in a randomised trial in non-hypertensive adults.
target: Riboflavin treatment may thus give an individualized approach to prevent high BP in adults with a certain genotype for MTHFR. Still, this needs confirmation in a trial in adults without high BP.

source: Gabapentin is an anticonvulsant drug, which in man is cleared solely by renal excretion and is not bound to plasma proteins.
target: Gabapentin is a nerve-related pain drug, which in humans is cleared from the body solely by the kidneys and is not attached to proteins in plasma (the liquid part of blood).

source: Because the clearance of gabapentin is dependent on renal function, the pharmacokinetics of gabapentin were investigated in anuric subjects maintained on hemodialysis.
target: Because the clearance of gabapentin depends on the kidneys, how the body processes gabapentin is investigated in patients who do not make enough urine and who are on dialysis, a process of cleaning the blood of a person whose kidneys are not working normally.

source: Plasma samples were obtained over an 8-day period after administration of single oral 400-mg doses of gabapentin.
target: Plasma samples are collected over an 8-day period after a single oral (by mouth) dose of gabapentin is given.

source: Pre- and post-dialyzer plasma samples and dialysate samples from quantitative collection of dialyzer effluent were obtained during hemodialysis sessions performed 2, 4, and 7 days after dosing.
target: Plasma samples before and after being filtered, as well as dialysis fluid, are collected during dialysis sessions performed 2, 4, and 7 days after receiving the gabapentin dose.

source: A mean (SD) maximum gabapentin plasma concentration of 6.0 (2.4) micrograms/mL was achieved at 4.7 (2.1) hours post-dose.
target: An average maximum amount of gabapentin in plasma is achieved at 4.7 hours after the dose.

source: The elimination half-life of gabapentin on non-hemodialysis days averaged 132 hours.
target: The time it takes for the concentration of the gabapentin in the plasma to be reduced by half on non-dialysis days averages 132 hours.

source: Approximately 35% of the gabapentin dose was recovered in dialysate, and mean hemodialysis clearance of gabapentin was 142 (26) mL/min; approximately 93% of the dialyzer creatinine clearance.
target: About 35% of gabapentin dose is recovered in dialysis fluid, and the average clearance of gabapentin in dialysis was 142 milliliters per minute.

source: Gabapentin elimination half-life during hemodialysis was approximately 4 hours.
target: The time it takes for the concentration of the gabapentin in the plasma to be reduced by half during dialysis is about 4 hours.

source: Systemic plasma gabapentin concentrations increased approximately 30% during the first 2 hours after hemodialysis as a result of drug redistribution in the body.
target: Gabapentin concentrations in the plasma increased about 30% during the first 2 hours after dialysis as a result of the drug being sent to other parts of the body.

source: It is recommended that patients with end-stage renal disease maintained on hemodialysis receive an initial 300-mg to 400-mg gabapentin loading dose.
target: It is recommended that patients with end-stage kidney disease who are on dialysis receive a starting 300-mg to 400-mg gabapentin dose.

source: Plasma gabapentin concentrations can be maintained by giving 200 to 300 mg of gabapentin after every 4 hours of hemodialysis.
target: Plasma gabapentin concentrations can remain stable by giving 200 to 300 mg of gabapentin after every 4 hours of dialysis.

source: Gabapentin is an anticonvulsant drug, which in man is cleared solely by renal excretion and is not bound to plasma proteins.
target: Gabapentin is an antiseizure drug, which in man is cleared just by kidney removal and is not bound to blood proteins.

source: Because the clearance of gabapentin is dependent on renal function, the pharmacokinetics of gabapentin were investigated in anuric subjects maintained on hemodialysis.
target: Because gabapentin clearance is dependent on kidney function, the drug activity of gabapentin was investigated in patients unable to make urine and maintained on artifical kidney machines or hemodialysis.

source: Plasma samples were obtained over an 8-day period after administration of single oral 400-mg doses of gabapentin.
target: We took blood samples over an 8-day period after treatment of single 400-mg swallowed doses of gabapentin.

source: Pre- and post-dialyzer plasma samples and dialysate samples from quantitative collection of dialyzer effluent were obtained during hemodialysis sessions performed 2, 4, and 7 days after dosing.
target: Blood and kidney solution samples were obtained during treatment sessions performed 2, 4, and 7 days after dosing.

source: A mean (SD) maximum gabapentin plasma concentration of 6.0 (2.4) micrograms/mL was achieved at 4.7 (2.1) hours post-dose.
target: An average maximum gabapentin blood level of 6.0 micrograms/mL was measured at 4.7 hours after the dose.

source: The elimination half-life of gabapentin on non-hemodialysis days averaged 132 hours.
target: The time for half of gabapentin to clear on non-hemodialysis days was 132 hours.

source: Approximately 35% of the gabapentin dose was recovered in dialysate, and mean hemodialysis clearance of gabapentin was 142 (26) mL/min; approximately 93% of the dialyzer creatinine clearance.
target: Around 35% of the gabapentin dose was recovered in the fluid of the artificial kidney machine. The average hemodialysis clearance of gabapentin was 142 mL/min; around 93% of the standard dialyzer clearance.

source: Gabapentin elimination half-life during hemodialysis was approximately 4 hours.
target: The time for half of gabapentin to clear during hemodialysis was around 4 hours.

source: Systemic plasma gabapentin concentrations increased approximately 30% during the first 2 hours after hemodialysis as a result of drug redistribution in the body.
target: Blood gabapentin levels increased around 30% during the first 2 hours after hemodialysis due to drug redistribution in the body.

source: It is recommended that patients with end-stage renal disease maintained on hemodialysis receive an initial 300-mg to 400-mg gabapentin loading dose.
target: It is recommended that patients with kidney failure and on hemodialysis get an initial 300-mg to 400-mg gabapentin starting dose.

source: Plasma gabapentin concentrations can be maintained by giving 200 to 300 mg of gabapentin after every 4 hours of hemodialysis.
target: Blood gabapentin levels can be maintained by giving 200 to 300 mg of gabapentin after every 4 hours of hemodialysis.

source: Gabapentin is a new antiepileptic drug (AED) with an attractive pharmacokinetic profile.
target: Gabapentin is a new drug to treat seizures that appears to work well for patients.

source: It is absorbed by an active and saturable transport system, and has a high volume of distribution.
target: It is absorbed into the body by an active and fluid-filled transport system, and is able to be distributed to tissues in the body.

source: Gabapentin is not bound to plasma proteins, does not induce hepatic enzymes and is not metabolized.
target: Gabapentin is not attached to proteins in the blood, does not create liver enzymes that can speed up chemical reactions in the body, and is not broken down.

source: At steady state, it has a half-life of 6-8 h, and is eliminated unchanged by renal route with a plasma clearance proportional to the creatinine clearance.
target: When the amount of the drug in the body is the same amount that is being cleared, the time it takes for the concentration of the gabapentin in the plasma or body to be reduced by half is 6-8 hours and is eliminated through the kidneys.

source: It is devoid of significant drug-drug interactions when administered with the established AEDs or with oral contraceptives.
target: Gabapentin does not have any major interactions with other drugs when given with other standard drugs that treat seizures or with oral (by mouth) birth control.

source: Gabapentin used as an add-on AED significantly reduced the frequency of partial seizures and secondarily generalized tonic-clonic seizures in three large double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group clinical trails.
target: In 3 clinical studies, gabapentin used as an add-on anti-seizure drug to assist the main treatment reduces the frequency of partial seizures (impacting half the brain) and seizures (impacting both halves of the brain).

source: It is well tolerated, with transient somnolence and dizziness being the most frequent adverse effects.
target: The medicine is handled well by patients, with drowsiness and dizziness being the most common side effects.

source: Although the mechanism of action of gabapentin is not fully established, there is strong evidence to suggest a novel mechanism of action.
target: Although the exact process of how gabapentin can lead to an effect is not fully understood, there is strong evidence that suggests a new process in the body.

source: Gabapentin is a unique and promising drug that could improve the quality of life of patients with epilepsy and is a welcome addition to the armamentarium of currently available AEDs for the treatment of patients with seizures of partial onset.
target: Gabapentin is a unique and promising drug that could improve the quality of life of patients with epilepsy (seizure disorders) and is a welcome addition to currently available drugs for the treatment of patients with seizures that start in one part of the brain.

source: Gabapentin is a new antiepileptic drug (AED) with an attractive pharmacokinetic profile.
target: Gabapentin is a new antiseizure or antiepileptic drug (AED) with attractive drug activity.

source: It is absorbed by an active and saturable transport system, and has a high volume of distribution.
target: Gabapentin is absorbed by an active transport system. The drug has a large distribution.

source: Gabapentin is not bound to plasma proteins, does not induce hepatic enzymes and is not metabolized.
target: Gabapentin is not bound to blood proteins, does not activate liver enzymes and is not broken down.

source: At steady state, it has a half-life of 6-8 h, and is eliminated unchanged by renal route with a plasma clearance proportional to the creatinine clearance.
target: Normally, half of the drug is eliminated in 6-8 hours. It is cleared unchanged by the kidney with a blood clearance similar to standard kidney substance clearance.

source: It is devoid of significant drug-drug interactions when administered with the established AEDs or with oral contraceptives.
target: The drug does not have major drug-drug interactions when used with other AEDs or swallowed birth control drugs.

source: Gabapentin used as an add-on AED significantly reduced the frequency of partial seizures and secondarily generalized tonic-clonic seizures in three large double-blind, placebo-controlled, parallel-group clinical trails.
target: Gabapentin used as an add-on AED greatly reduces the amount of partial seizures and partial seizures developing into standard serizures in three clinical trials.

source: It is well tolerated, with transient somnolence and dizziness being the most frequent adverse effects.
target: The drug is well tolerated, with temporary drowsiness and dizziness as the most common side effects.

source: Although the mechanism of action of gabapentin is not fully established, there is strong evidence to suggest a novel mechanism of action.
target: Although how gabapentin works is unknown, there is evidence for a new mechanism of its biological action.

source: Gabapentin is a unique and promising drug that could improve the quality of life of patients with epilepsy and is a welcome addition to the armamentarium of currently available AEDs for the treatment of patients with seizures of partial onset.
target: Gabapentin is a unique and promising drug that could improve life for patients with epilepsy (a brain disorder causing seizures). The drug is a welcome addition to current AEDs for treating patients with seizures of partial onset.

source: The amino acid antiepileptic drug (AED) gabapentin (GBP) is indicated for adjunctive use in the treatment of partial seizures with or without becoming secondarily generalized in individuals older than 12 years.
target: The drug gabapentin (GBP) is an addition to the regular treatment of partial seizures with or without becoming generalized seizures in the entire brain in people older than 12 years.

source: GBP was about as potent as phenytoin in the maximal electroshock test, but had a different profile of efficacy than standard antiepileptics in a range of animal models.
target: Gabapentin has performed differently than other standard drugs that are used to prevent seizures in a number of animal experiments.

source: Possible mechanisms of action include biochemical effects enhancing the ratio of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) to glutamate, ion-channel actions (direct or indirect), and/ or enhancement of nonsynaptic GABA release.
target: Possible processes that lead the drug to have an effect include the response of increasing the development of neurotransmitters (signaling molecules in the brain) and other cell functions.

source: The anticonvulsant effect appears to depend on concentration of gabapentin in neurons, presumably by the L-system amino acid transporter that has been implicated in absorption from the gut.
target: The act of stopping or slowing the excessive rapid firing of neurons (brain cells) during seizures seems to depend on the concentration of gabapentin in neurons.

source: Data from studies for U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval suggested a direct relationship of clinical response to dose and efficacy did not plateau at the doses used.
target: Data from studies for U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval suggest a direct relationship of medical response to the dose and success did not level-off at the doses used.

source: The maximally effective dose, relationship of efficacy to blood level, and maximum tolerable dose are not yet known conclusively.
target: The maximally effective dose (the dose at which any higher dose would not lead to improvement), the relationship of its performance to blood level, and the maximum tolerable dose (the highest dose most people can handle) are not completely known.

source: Lack of significant binding to plasma proteins and lack of liver metabolism contribute to the absence of known limiting drug-drug interactions, particularly with other AEDs.
target: Lack of attachment to plasma (or blood) proteins and lack of liver energy contribute to the absence of known interactions between drugs, particularly with other drugs that are used to prevent seizures.

source: Excretion intact in the urine affords dose adjustment on the basis of creatinine clearance.
target: Doses can be adjusted based on the amount of the drug found in the urine.

source: A half-life of approximately 7 h necessitates multiple doses daily for many individuals.
target: The time it takes for the concentration of the gabapentin in the plasma or in the body to be reduced by half is about 7 hours, requiring multiple doses daily for many people.

source: The medication is well tolerated, in general.
target: In general, people are able to handle the medication and its side effects.

source: Side effects tend to be mild to moderate in intensity, most frequently affect the central nervous system, and resolve with time in many individuals.
target: Side effects tend to be mild to moderate in intensity, most frequently affect the central nervous system (spinal cord and brain), and resolve with time in many individuals.

source: GBP has been prescribed for approximately 70,000 individuals worldwide without untoward incidence of severe systemic toxicity to date.
target: Gabapentin (GBP) is prescribed for about 70,000 people around the world without unexpected incidence of severe toxic effects so far.

source: Safety data continue to accumulate.
target: Safety data continues to be collected.

source: GBP has been labeled category C on the basis of effects on rodent fetuses.
target: Gabapentin has shown to have adverse effects on rodent fetuses.

source: Experience with use in pregnant women is limited and human teratogenic effects have not been reported.
target: Experience with use in pregnant women is limited, and adverse (or bad) effects or abnormalities in human fetuses have not been reported.

source: Data from ongoing monotherapy trials will help to clarify the range of clinical utility of gabapentin.
target: Data from ongoing single drug trials will help to clarify the range of the clinical use of gabapentin.

source: Purpose: This study was conducted to evaluate the effect of age, age-related changes in renal function, and gender on the single-dose pharmacokinetics of orally administered gabapentin (GBP).
target: This study is conducted to evaluate the effect of age, age-related changes in renal or kidney function, and gender on the single-dose pharmacokinetics (or drug activity) of orally (by mouth) administered gabapentin (GBP).

source: Methods: The pharmacokinetics of a single 400-mg oral dose of GBP were studied in 36 healthy subjects (18 men and 18 women) aged 20-78 years.
target: The body's process of absorbing, distributing, using, and removing a single oral dose (by mouth) of gabapentin is studied in 36 healthy patients (18 men and 18 women) aged 20-78 years.

source: Serial blood samples and total urine output were collected for 48 h after the dose.
target: Blood and urine samples are continuously collected for 48 hours after the dose of gabapentin.

source: GBP concentrations in plasma and urine were measured by high-performance liquid chromatography, and pharmacokinetic parameters were calculated by noncompartmental methods.
target: The amount of Gabapentin in plasma (or blood) and urine samples are measured, and other tests are done to understand how the body processes gabapentin.

source: Results: All subjects tolerated the drug well, with only mild symptoms reported.
target: All patients are able to handle the drug well, with only mild symptoms reported.

source: No change in maximal GBP plasma concentration (Cmax), time at which Cmax occurred (tmax), or apparent volume of distribution (V/F) with age was noted.
target: No change in the highest (peak) gabapentin amount, the time at which the highest gabapentin amount occurred, or apparent volume of distribution (the ability of various drugs to distribute through the body fluids) with age was noted.

source: A significant linear decline in apparent oral clearance (CL/F), elimination-rate constant (lambda z), and renal clearance (CLR) with increasing age was observed (p < 0.005).
target: A major decline in clearance of gabapentin that is taken orally, the rate of removal from the body, and clearing it through the kidneys with increasing age is observed.

source: Because total urinary recovery of unchanged drug (an estimate of F for GBP) did not change with age, the decline in CL/F and lambda z can be explained by the decline in CLR.
target: The decline of oral clearance and rate of removal of gabapentin from the body can be explained by the decline in clearance by the kidneys.

source: The only pharmacokinetic parameter that was significantly different between genders was Cmax, which was approximately 25% higher for women than for men (p = 0.016), consistent with gender differences in body size.
target: The only measure that is very different between genders is the highest (peak) gabapentin amount, which is approximately 25% higher for women than for men, consistent with gender differences in body size.

source: Conclusions: The results of this study suggest that changes in renal function are responsible for age-related changes in GBP pharmacokinetics.
target: In conclusion, the results of this study suggest that changes in kidney function are responsible for age-related changes in how the body processes gabapentin.

source: Reduction of GBP dosage may be required in elderly patients with reduced renal function.
target: Giving a lesser dose of gabapentin may be required in elderly patients who have weaker kidney functions.

source: The pharmacokinetics of GBP are similar in men and women.
target: How the body processes gabapentin is similar in men and women.

source: Purpose: The pharmacokinetics of gabapentin in paediatric patients with uncontrolled seizures was studied.
target: How the pediatric or child patients with uncontrolled seizures are able to handle gabapentin (a nerve-related pain medication) and the processes of absorbing, distributing, using, and removing it is studied.

source: Methods: Thirteen paediatric patients (mean age: 9.4 years) with uncontrolled partial seizures were included.
target: In this study, 13 pediatric patients (less the 21 years old) with an average age of about 9 years with uncontrolled partial seizures are included.

source: Patients received gabapentin orally until doses were individualized to 9.6-39.8 mg/kg/day.
target: Patients received gabapentin orally (by mouth) until doses are changed for each patient.

source: Blood samples were obtained just prior to the dose and over 8 h after gabapentin was administered in the fasting state.
target: Blood samples are obtained just before the dose and over 8 hours after gabapentin is given during fasting, when all foods have been completely digested.

source: The plasma concentration of gabapentin was measured by a high-performance liquid chromatography assay.
target: Blood concentrations of gabapentin is measured by blood tests.

source: Pharmacokinetic parameters for gabapentin were determined by non-compartment methods using multivariate regression analysis.
target: Absorption, distribution, use, and removal of gabapentin in the body is analyzed.

source: Results: Data from nine patients were suitable for pharmacokinetic analysis.
target: Data from 9 patients are able to be used for analysis.

source: The C(max) from 0.9 to 5.8 microg/mL (mean: 2.6 +/- 1.7 microg/mL) and T(max) from 0.5 to 2.0 h (mean: 1.6 +/- 1.0 h).
target: The maximum concentration of the drug and the time it takes to reach maximum concentration are estimated.

source: Five patients experienced moderate (n = 4) to severe (n = 1) aggressive behaviour and another gained weight on gabapentin.
target: Five patients experience moderate (4 patients) to severe (1 patient) aggressive behavior, and another gained weight on gabapentin.

source: Conclusions: Our data suggests that gabapentin pharmacokinetics can vary substantially among paediatric patients.
target: In conclusion, the data in this study suggests that how the body processes gabapentin can vary a lot among pediatric patients.

source: Gabapentin was well tolerated in patients with uncontrolled partial seizures up to 6 months of therapy.
target: In this study, patients with uncontrolled partial seizures were able to handle gabapentin well up to 6 months of therapy.

source: Pharmacokinetic data of gabapentin (GBP) in community-dwelling elderly patients show a significant effect of advanced age on GBP pharmacokinetics due to altered renal function.
target: Data on how gabapentin (GBP -a nerve-related pain medication) is used in the body in elderly patients show a major effect of older age on how the drug works due to changes in kidney function.

source: However, there are no data in elderly nursing home (NH) patients to evaluate gabapentin absorption and elimination.
target: However, there are no data in elderly nursing home patients to evaluate how the body absorbs and removes gabapentin.

source: Our objective was to characterize the pharmacokinetics of GBP in elderly nursing home patients maintained on GBP therapy.
target: The objective of this study is to describe how the body uses and processes gabapentin in elderly nursing home patients who are on gabapentin.

source: This was a prospective pharmacokinetic study in elderly nursing home patients (≥60 years) receiving GBP for the management of chronic pain or epilepsy from seven nursing homes.
target: This study observed elderly nursing home patients (≥60 years) receiving gabapentin for chronic (ongoing) pain or epilepsy (seizure disorders) from 7 nursing homes.

source: A one-compartment model described the data and clearance (CL) was associated with estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) (p < 0.0001).
target: Data on gabapentin and the clearance of the drug are associated with the rate that measures how well the kidneys are working.

source: The GBP CL in elderly nursing home patients was 2.93 L/h.
target: The gabapentin clearance in elderly nursing home patients was 2.93 liters per hour.

source: After adjusting for the effect of GFR, GBP CL was not affected by age, sex, body weight, or comorbidity scores.
target: After accounting for the rate that measures how well the kidneys are working, GBP clearance is not affected by age, sex, body weight, or other illnesses and their medications.

source: No significant effects of body size measures, age, and sex were detected on volume of distribution.
target: No significant effects of body size measures, age, and sex are detected on volume of distribution, the ability of various drugs to distribute through the body fluids.

source: Dose-dependent bioavailability of GBP was demonstrated, and the saturable absorption profile was described by a nonlinear hyperbolic function.
target: The availability of the drug being dependent on dosage is shown, and how it is absorbed is calculated.

source: Prediction-corrected visual predictive check (pc-VPC) suggests adequate fixed- and random-effects models that successfully simulated the mean trend and variability in gabapentin concentration-time profiles.
target: Additional calculations are done on the entire group to evaluate the performance of the drug and identify other individual factors that may impact the effect.

source: In this analysis, the parameters of the hyperbolic nonlinearity appear to be similar between elderly and younger adults.
target: In this analysis, the patterns of the drug appear to be similar between elderly and younger adults.

source: Pharmacokinetic data of gabapentin (GBP) in community-dwelling elderly patients show a significant effect of advanced age on GBP pharmacokinetics due to altered renal function.
target: Drug activity data of gabapentin (GBP) in community-dwelling elderly patients show a great effect of advanced age on GVP drug activity due to altered kidney function.

source: However, there are no data in elderly nursing home (NH) patients to evaluate gabapentin absorption and elimination.
target: However, there are no data in elderly nursing home (NH) patients to measure gabapentin absorption and elimination.

source: Our objective was to characterize the pharmacokinetics of GBP in elderly nursing home patients maintained on GBP therapy.
target: We characterize the drug activity of GBP in elderly nursing home patients that are on GBP therapy.

source: This was a prospective pharmacokinetic study in elderly nursing home patients (≥60 years) receiving GBP for the management of chronic pain or epilepsy from seven nursing homes.
target: This is a drug actvity study in elderly nursing home patients (≥60 years) given GBP for long-term pain or epilepsy (brain disorder causing seizures) from seven nursing homes.

source: A one-compartment model described the data and clearance (CL) was associated with estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) (p < 0.0001).
target: A mathematical model showed the data and clearance (CL) was linked with estimated kidney filtration rate.

source: The GBP CL in elderly nursing home patients was 2.93 L/h.
target: The GBP clearance in elderly nursing home patients was 2.93 liters/hour.

source: After adjusting for the effect of GFR, GBP CL was not affected by age, sex, body weight, or comorbidity scores.
target: After adjusting for kidney filtration, GBP clearance was not affected by age, sex, body weight, or associated diseases.

source: No significant effects of body size measures, age, and sex were detected on volume of distribution.
target: No effects of body size, age, and sex were detected on amount of distribution.

source: Dose-dependent bioavailability of GBP was demonstrated, and the saturable absorption profile was described by a nonlinear hyperbolic function.
target: Functional or active GBP amount is linked to dose amount.

source: Prediction-corrected visual predictive check (pc-VPC) suggests adequate fixed- and random-effects models that successfully simulated the mean trend and variability in gabapentin concentration-time profiles.
target: Analysis suggests certain mathematical models successfully tracked the trend and variation in gabapentin amount over time.

source: In this analysis, the parameters of the hyperbolic nonlinearity appear to be similar between elderly and younger adults.
target: In this analysis, certain measures seem similar between elderly and young adults.

source: Gabapentin is almost exclusively cleared by the kidney and thus presents challenges in patients with kidney failure.
target: The nerve-related pain medication gabapentin is cleared from the body almost entirely by the kidney. Because of this, patients with kidney failure face a challenge.

source: Gabapentin is known to be effectively cleared by hemodialysis, but the efficiency of clearance by peritoneal dialysis (PD) has not been previously described.
target: Gabapentin is known to be effectively cleared by hemodialysis, a process of using machines to clean the blood of a person whose kidneys are not working normally, but how well peritoneal dialysis (PD), a process that filters blood using the inside lining of the stomach, clears the drug has not been previously described.

source: We report a case of gabapentin toxicity in a patient on long-term PD who was treated with continuous automated cycling PD.
target: Researchers describe a case of too much gabapentin in a patient with long-term peritoneal dialysis who is treated with continuous automated cycling peritoneal dialysis, which is when a mechanical device filters the blood at night while sleeping.

source: We find that continuous PD provides significant clearance of gabapentin.
target: Researchers find that continuous peritoneal dialysis provides significant clearance of gabapentin.

source: With 2-L exchanges every 2 hours, we document an apparent elimination half-life of 41.33 hours, which is substantially shorter than the reported elimination half-life of 132 hours in the absence of kidney function.
target: The documented elimination half-life (the time it takes for the concentration of the gabapentin in the plasma or body to be reduced by half) is 41.33 hours, which is much shorter than the 132 hours reported in other studies in the absence of kidney function.

source: Further, our patient's symptoms of gabapentin toxicity gradually improved and had fully resolved after about 36 hours of dialysis.
target: In addition, the patient's symptoms of gabapentin toxicity (often muscle weakness, drowsiness, and drooping eyelids) improved and fully resolved after about 36 hours of dialysis.

source: Gabapentin clearance by PD was estimated at 94% of urea clearance.
target: Gabapentin clearance in the body by peritoneal dialysis is estimated at 94% of urea (or waste protein) clearance.

source: We conclude that intensive PD provides gabapentin clearance that approximates that of urea and is an effective but slow method to treat gabapentin overdose and toxicity.
target: In conclusion, intensive peritoneal dialysis provides gabapentin clearance and is an effective but slow method to treat gabapentin overdose and toxicity.

source: The objective of this study was to perform population pharmacokinetic (PK) analysis of gabapentin in healthy Korean subjects and to investigate the possible effect of genetic polymorphisms (1236C > T, 2677G > T/A, and 3435C > T) of ABCB1 gene on PK parameters of gabapentin.
target: The objective of this study is to perform an analysis on how the nerve-related pain medication gabapentin performs in healthy Korean participants and to investigate the possible effect of genetic changes in the drug-resistant ABCB1 gene on how gabapentin is absorbed and distributed in the body.

source: Data were collected from bioequivalence studies, in which 173 subjects orally received three different doses of gabapentin (300, 400, and 800 mg).
target: Data are collected from other studies where 173 people received three different doses by mouth (orally) of gabapentin.

source: Only data from reference formulation were used.
target: Only data on the drug design and performance are used.

source: Population pharmacokinetics (PKs) of gabapentin was estimated using a nonlinear mixed-effects model (NONMEM).
target: How the body handles and uses gabapentin is measured.

source: Gabapentin showed considerable inter-individual variability (from 5.2- to 8.7-fold) in PK parameters.
target: Gabapentin shows a lot of variation or differences between people in how it is absorbed, distributed and released from the body.

source: Serum concentration of gabapentin was well fitted by a one-compartment model with first-order absorption and lag time.
target: Blood concentration of gabapentin is used to understand the process of distributing and eliminating the medication from the body.

source: An inhibitory Emax model was applied to describe the effect of dose on bioavailability.
target: How the dose amount impacts availability in the body is measured.

source: The oral clearance was estimated to be 11.1 L/h.
target: The oral clearance is estimated to be 11.1 liters per hour.

source: The volume of distribution was characterized as 81.0 L.
target: The volume of distribution was characterized as 81.0 L.

source: The absorption rate constant was estimated at 0.860 h-1, and the lag time was predicted at 0.311 h.
target: How quickly the medication enters the body remains steady.

source: Oral bioavailability was estimated to be 68.8% at dose of 300 mg, 62.7% at dose of 400 mg, and 47.1% at dose of 800 mg.
target: Availability of the drug from the oral dose is estimated to be 68.8% at a dose of 300 mg, 62.7% at a dose of 400 mg, and 47.1% at a dose of 800 mg.

source: The creatinine clearance significantly influenced on the oral clearance (P < 0.005) and ABCB1 2677G > T/A genotypes significantly influenced on the absorption rate constant (P < 0.05) of gabapentin.
target: Clearing creatinine (a waste product that comes from wear and tear of the muscles) influences the oral clearance, and ABCB1 genes influence how quickly gabapentin is absorbed by the body.

source: However, ABCB1 1236C > T and 3435C > T genotypes showed no significant effect on gabapentin PK parameters.
target: However, genes showed no significant effect on gabapentin drug activity parameters.

source: The results of the present study indicate that the oral bioavailability of gabapentin is decreased when its dosage is increased.
target: The results of this study suggest that the availability of gabapentin to the body when taken by mouth is decreased when the dosage is increased.

source: In addition, ABCB1 2677G > T/A polymorphism can explain the substantial inter-individual variability in the absorption of gabapentin.
target: In addition, changes in the ABCB1 gene can explain the major differences in how gabapentin is absorbed by the body between people.

source: Gabapentin (GBP) is an organic cation mainly eliminated unchanged in urine, and active drug secretion has been suggested to contribute to its renal excretion.
target: Gabapentin (GBP) is a nerve-related pain medication that is mainly removed from the body in urine, and is also processed by the kidneys.

source: Our objective was to evaluate the potential drug-drug interaction between GBP and cetirizine (CTZ), an inhibitor of transporters for organic cations.
target: The objective of this study is to evaluate how gabapentin may interact with another medicine called cetirizine (CTZ) that is used to temporarily relieve allergies.

source: An open-label, 2-period, crossover, nonrandomized clinical trial was conducted in patients with neuropathic pain to evaluate the effect of CTZ on GBP pharmacokinetics.
target: A clinical trial in patients with a shooting or burning pain caused by damage to the nervous system called neuropathic pain is conducted to understand the effect of cetirizine on how gabapentin is processed in the body.

source: Twelve participants were treated with a single dose of 300 mg GBP (treatment A) or with 20 mg/d of CTZ for 5 days and 300 mg GBP on the last day of CTZ treatment (treatment B).
target: In this study, 12 patients are treated with either one dose of gabapentin (called Treatment A) or with cetirizine for 5 days and gabapentin given on the last day of cetirizine (Treatment B).

source: Blood sampling and pain intensity evaluation were performed up to 36 hours after GBP administration.
target: Blood samples are taken and the level of pain is measured up to 36 hours after gabapentin is taken.

source: The interaction of GBP and CTZ with transporters for organic cations was studied in human embryonic kidney (HEK) cells expressing the organic cation transporters (OCTs), multidrug and toxin extrusion proteins (MATEs), and OCTN1.
target: The interaction of gabapentin and cetirizine is analyzed.

source: CTZ treatment resulted in reduced area under the concentration-time curve and peak concentration compared with treatment A.
target: Cetirizine treatment resulted a reduced time the body is exposed to the medication and reduced peak amount when compared with Treatment A (one dose of gabapentin).

source: In treatment B, the lower plasma concentrations of GBP resulted in reduced pain attenuation.
target: In treatment B, the lower concentrations of gabapentin or GBP in the blood results in reduced pain.

source: GBP renal clearance was similar between treatments.
target: GBP clearance from the kidneys is similar between treatments A and B.

source: GBP has low apparent affinity for OCT2 (concentration of an inhibitor where the response [or binding] is reduced by half [IC50 ] 237 µmol/L) and a high apparent affinity for hMATE1 (IC50 1.1 nmol/L), hMATE2-K (IC50 39 nmol/L), and hOCTN1 (IC50 2.1 nmol/L) in HEK cells.
target: GBP appears to bind less to some receptors or kidney target sites such as OCT2 and attach more towards others such as hMATE1 in certain human kidney cells.

source: At therapeutic concentrations, CTZ interacts with hMATE1 and OCTN1.
target: At certain concentrations, CTZ interacts with transporters in the body that move materials across cells.

source: In summary, CTZ reduced the systemic exposure to GBP and its effect on neuropathic pain attenuation.
target: In summary, cetirizine  (CTZ) reduces the exposure to gabapentin (GBP) and its effect on neuropathic pain.

source: However, CTZ × GBP interaction is not mediated by the renal transporters.
target: However, CTZ × GBP interaction is not influenced by the transporters in the kidney.

source: Aims: Gabapentin (GBP) is widely used to treat neuropathic pain, including diabetic neuropathic pain.
target: Neuropathic pain is caused by damage to the nervous system (like brain and spinal cord) and is often described as a shooting pain, burning sensation, or numbness. Gabapentin is a common medication used to treat neuropathic pain, including neuropathic pain caused by diabetes.

source: Our objective was to evaluate the role of diabetes and glycaemic control on GBP population pharmacokinetics.
target: The objective of this study is to evaluate the role of diabetes and controlling blood sugar on people taking gabapentin.

source: Methods: A clinical trial was conducted in patients with neuropathic pain (n = 29) due to type 2 diabetes (n = 19) or lumbar/cervical disc herniation (n = 10).
target: A clinical trial is conducted in patients with neuropathic pain due to type 2 diabetes (19 people) or a slipped disc in the neck or spine (10 people).

source: All participants were treated with a single oral dose GBP.
target: All participants are treated with a single dose of gabapentin by mouth.

source: Blood was sampled up to 24 hours after GBP administration.
target: Blood is sampled up to 24 hours after gabapentin is given.

source: Data were analysed with a population approach using the stochastic approximation expectation maximization algorithm.
target: Data are analyzed using computer models.

source: Weight, body mass index, sex, biomarkers of renal function and diabetes, and genotypes for the main genetic polymorphisms of SLC22A2 (rs316019) and SLC22A4 (rs1050152), the genes encoding the transporters for organic cations OCT2 and OCTN1, were tested as potential covariates.
target: Other information are included in the model including weight, sex, kidney functions and other changes in genes that impact how the medication works.

source: The total clearance was dependent on estimated glomerular filtration rate.
target: The total clearance of gabapentin from the body depends on how well the kidneys are able to process and filter the drug.

source: Population estimates (between-subject variability in percentage) for lag time, first-order absorption rate, apparent volume of distribution and total clearance were 0.316 h (10.6%), 1.12 h-1 (10.7%), 140 L (7.7%) and 14.7 L/h (6.97%), respectively.
target: The estimated total clearance of the drug (completely removed) in the population is 14.7 liters per hour.

source: No significant association was observed with hyperglycaemia, glycated haemoglobin, diabetes diagnosis, age, sex, weight, body mass index, SLC22A2 or SLC22 A4 genotypes.
target: No major association is found with having high blood sugar levels, a diabetes diagnosis, or with age, sex, weight or other factors evaluated.

source: Conclusion: This population pharmacokinetics model accurately estimated GBP concentrations in patients with neuropathic pain, using estimated glomerular filtrationrate as a covariate for total clearance.
target: This model studying how the drug is processed in the body estimates gabapentin concentrations in patients with neuropathic pain.

source: The distribution and excretion processes of GBP were not affected by hyperglycaemia or diabetes.
target: The distribution and clearance processes of gabapentin are not affected by having high blood sugar levels or diabetes.

source: Aims: Gabapentin (GBP) is widely used to treat neuropathic pain, including diabetic neuropathic pain.
target: Gabapentin (GBP), an antiseizure drug, can treat neuropathic or nerve-related pain, including diabetic neuropathic pain.

source: Our objective was to evaluate the role of diabetes and glycaemic control on GBP population pharmacokinetics.
target: We evaluate the role of diabetes and blood sugar control on drug activity in the GBP population.

source: Methods: A clinical trial was conducted in patients with neuropathic pain (n = 29) due to type 2 diabetes (n = 19) or lumbar/cervical disc herniation (n = 10).
target: A trial was done in 29 patients with neuropathic pain, 19 from type 2 diabetes and 10 from lower back/neck disc herniation or bulging.

source: All participants were treated with a single oral dose GBP.
target: All participants swallowed a single dose of GBP.

source: Blood was sampled up to 24 hours after GBP administration.
target: Blood was taken up to 24 hours after GBP treatment.

source: Population estimates (between-subject variability in percentage) for lag time, first-order absorption rate, apparent volume of distribution and total clearance were 0.316 h (10.6%), 1.12 h-1 (10.7%), 140 L (7.7%) and 14.7 L/h (6.97%), respectively.
target: Measurements for lag time, absorption rate, volume of distribution, and clearance rate were 0.316 h (10.6%), 1.12 h-1 (10.7%), 140 L (7.7%) and 14.7 L/h (6.97%), respectively.

source: No significant association was observed with hyperglycaemia, glycated haemoglobin, diabetes diagnosis, age, sex, weight, body mass index, SLC22A2 or SLC22 A4 genotypes.
target: No link was seen with high blood sugar, blood sugar levels, diabetes diagnosis, age, sex, weight, body mass index, or certain DNA sequences.

source: Conclusion: This population pharmacokinetics model accurately estimated GBP concentrations in patients with neuropathic pain, using estimated glomerular filtrationrate as a covariate for total clearance.
target: This model measuring drug activity accurately estimated GBP concentrations in those with neuropathic pain, using kidney drug filtration rate for total drug clearance.

source: The distribution and excretion processes of GBP were not affected by hyperglycaemia or diabetes.
target: The distribution and removal processes of GBP were not affected by high blood sugar or diabetes.

source: Osteoporosis and its consequent increase in fracture risk is a major health concern for postmenopausal women and older men and has the potential to reach epidemic proportions.
target: Osteoporosis, a condition in which bones become weak and brittle, and the increase in the risk of bone breaks it causes has worried women who have gone through menopause (a point in time 12 months after the last period) and older men and could become a huge problem.

source: The "gold standard" for osteoporosis diagnosis is bone densitometry.
target: The best way to diagnose osteoporosis is bone densitometry, a common test to measure bone density.

source: However, economic issues or availability of the technology may prevent the possibility of mass screening.
target: Large-scale checks may not be possible due to economic issues or availability of densitometry.

source: The goal of this study was to develop and validate a clinical scoring index designed as a prescreening tool to help clinicians identify which women are at increased risk of osteoporosis [bone mineral density (BMD) T-score -2.5 or less] and should therefore undergo further testing with bone densitometry.
target: We aimed to make and test a scoring system to help doctors find which women are more likely to develop osteoporosis and should have more testing with bone densitometry.

source: Records were analyzed for 1522 postmenopausal females over 50 years of age who had undergone testing with dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA).
target: We looked at records for 1522 women who have gone through menopause over 50 years old who had dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA - a common test for bone density).

source: Osteoporosis risk index scores were compared to bone density T-scores.
target: We compared risk scores to bone density tests.

source: Hologic QDR 4500 technology was used to measure BMD at the femoral neck and lumbar spine (L1-L4).
target: We measured BMD at the hip and lower spine.

source: Participants who had a previous diagnosis of osteoporosis or were taking bone-active medication were excluded.
target: We excluded people who were previously diagnosed with osteoporosis or were taking drugs affecting bones.

source: A simple algorithm based on age, weight, history of previous low impact fracture, early menopause, and corticosteroid therapy was developed.
target: We came up with a simple calculation based on age, weight, history of previous falls from a standing height or lower, early menopause, and use of a certain kind of steroid.

source: Validation of this five-item osteoporosis prescreening risk assessment (OPERA) index showed that the tool, at the recommended threshold (or cutoff value) of two, had a sensitivity that ranged from 88.1 [95% confidence interval (CI) for the mean: 86.2-91.9%] at the femoral neck to 90% (95% CI for the mean: 86.1-93.1%) at the lumbar spine area.
target: Testing of this five-item osteoporosis prescreening risk assessment (OPERA) shows high accuracy for osteoporosis detection.

source: Corresponding specificity values were 60.6 (95% CI for the mean: 57.9-63.3%) and 64.2% (95% CI for the mean: 61.4-66.9%), respectively.
target: OPERA shows high accuracy for no detection.

source: The positive predictive value (PPV) ranged from 29 at the femoral neck to 39.2% at the lumbar spine, while the corresponding negative predictive values (NPVs) reached 96.5 and 96.2%, respectively.
target: The accuracy for results detecting osteoporosis ranged from 29 at the femoral neck to 39.2% at the lumbar spine, while the corresponding accuracy for results not detecting osteoporosis reached 96.5 and 96.2%, respectively.

source: We conclude that the OPERA is a free and effective method for identifying Italian postmenopausal women at increased risk of osteoporosis.
target: We conclude that the OPERA is a free and effective way to identify Italian women who have gone through menopause at higher risk of osteoporosis.

source: Its use could facilitate the appropriate and more cost-effective use of bone densitometry in developing countries.
target: Use of OPERA could help the appropriate and more cost-effective use of bone densitometry in developing countries.

source: BACKGROUND: Although bone mineral density (BMD) testing to screen for osteoporosis (BMD T score, −2.50 or lower) is recommended for women 65 years of age or older, there are few data to guide decisions about the interval between BMD tests.
target: Although bone mineral density (BMD) testing to measure how much calcium and other minerals are in bones to look for osteoporosis (a condition in which bones become weak and brittle) is suggested for women 65 years old or older, few studies look at how long to wait between tests.

source: METHODS: We studied 4957 women, 67 years of age or older, with normal BMD (T score at the femoral neck and total hip, −1.00 or higher) or osteopenia (T score, −1.01 to −2.49) and with no history of hip or clinical vertebral fracture or of treatment for osteoporosis, followed prospectively for up to 15 years.
target: We studied 4957 women, 67 years old and older, with normal BMD or weaker than normal bones who had never broken a hip or spine or received treatment for osteoporosis, followed for up to 15 years.

source: The BMD testing interval was defined as the estimated time for 10% of women to make the transition to osteoporosis before having a hip or clinical vertebral fracture, with adjustment for estrogen use and clinical risk factors.
target: The time between BMD testing was the estimated time for 10% of women to develop osteoporosis before breaking a hip or spine, considering estrogen (female-specific hormone) use and risk factors.

source: Transitions from normal BMD and from three subgroups of osteopenia (mild, moderate, and advanced) were analyzed with the use of parametric cumulative incidence models.
target: We used mathematical models to look at changes from normal BMD and from mild, moderate, and advanced osteopenia.

source: Incident hip and clinical vertebral fractures and initiation of treatment with bisphosphonates, calcitonin, or raloxifene were treated as competing risks.
target: We considered hip and spine breaks and people taking drugs to strengthen bones.

source: RESULTS: The estimated BMD testing interval was 16.8 years (95% confidence interval [CI], 11.5 to 24.6) for women with normal BMD, 17.3 years (95% CI, 13.9 to 21.5) for women with mild osteopenia, 4.7 years (95% CI, 4.2 to 5.2) for women with moderate osteopenia, and 1.1 years (95% CI, 1.0 to 1.3) for women with advanced osteopenia.
target: We found the estimated time between BMD testing was 16.8 years for women with normal BMD and 17.3, 4.7. and 1.1 years for women with mild, moderate, and advanced osteopenia, respectively.

source: CONCLUSIONS: Our data indicate that osteoporosis would develop in less than 10% of older, post-menopausal women during rescreening intervals of approximately 15 years for women with normal bone density or mild osteopenia, 5 years for women with moderate osteopenia, and 1 year for women with advanced osteopenia.
target: Our results suggest that less than 10% of older women who had gone through menopause (a point in time 12 months after the last period) would develop osteoporosis if tested about every 15 years for women with normal BMD or mild osteopenia, 5 years for women with moderate osteopenia, and 1 year for women with advanced osteopenia.

source: The US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) guideline for osteoporosis screening concludes that there is a lack of evidence about optimal rescreening intervals and states that intervals >2 years may be necessary to better predict fracture risk.
target: Current expert panel guidance for osteoporosis (a condition in which bones become weak and brittle) testing states that not enough data exist on the best period of time in between tests and that periods of over 2 years may be needed to better predict the risk of breaking bones.

source: In addition, the USPSTF cites a prospective study showing that repeat measurement of BMD after 8 years added little predictive value compared with baseline DEXA scan results.
target: In addition, the guidance mentions a study showing that measuring BMD after 8 years only helped a small amount to predict osteoporosis compared with initial BMD scan results.

source: Reconsider the intervals at which you recommend rescreening for osteoporosis; for post-menopausal women with a baseline of normal bone mineral density (BMD) or mild osteopenia, a 15-year interval is probably sufficient.
target: Re-think the period of time between osteoporosis checks; for women who have gone through menopause (a point in time 12 months after the last period) with an initial normal bone mineral density (BMD) or slightly weaker than normal bones, 15 years between checks is likely enough.

source: The US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) guideline for osteoporosis screening concludes that there is a lack of evidence about optimal rescreening intervals and states that intervals >2 years may be necessary to better predict fracture risk.
target: The US Preventative Services Task Force (USPSTF) guideline for screening osteoporosis, a bone-weakening disease, states that there is a lack of evidence about best rescreening intervals. The guideline states that intervals >2 years may be needed to better preduct fracture risk.

source: In addition, the USPSTF cites a prospective study showing that repeat measurement of BMD after 8 years added little predictive value compared with baseline DEXA scan results.
target: Also, the USPSTF mentions a study in which repeat measurement of bone mineral density (BMD) or hardness after 8 years adds litte value relative to inital bone scans.

source: Reconsider the intervals at which you recommend rescreening for osteoporosis; for post-menopausal women with a baseline of normal bone mineral density (BMD) or mild osteopenia, a 15-year interval is probably sufficient.
target: Reconsider the intervals at which you recommend rescreening for osteoporosis. For post-menopausal or older women with normal BMD or mild bone weakening, a 15-year interval is likely enough.

source: We evaluated the prevalence and geographic variation of short-interval (repeated in under 2 years) dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry tests (DXAs) among Medicare beneficiaries.
target: We rated the number and variation (or differences) across the country of dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry tests (DXAs - useful tests for measuring bone density) repeated in under 2 years (short-interval) in people on Medicare during a specific period.

source: Short-interval DXA use varied across regions (coefficient of variation = 0.64), and unlike other DXAs, rates decreased with payment cuts.
target: Use of short-interval DXAs varied across the country and unlike other DXAs, use went down with Medicare payment cuts.

source: Introduction: The American College of Rheumatology, through the Choosing Wisely initiative, identified measuring bone density more often than every 2 years as care "physicians and patients should question."
target: A campaign to avoid unnecessary medical tests, treatments and procedures found measuring bone density more often than every 2 years as care "physicians and patients should question."

source: We measured the prevalence and described the geographic variation of short-interval (repeated in under 2 years) DXAs among Medicare beneficiaries and estimated the cost of this testing and its responsiveness to payment change.
target: We rated the number and variation across the country of short-interval DXAs in people on Medicare during a specific period and estimated the testing cost and how much is changed based on payment.

source: Methods: Using 100 % Medicare claims data, 2006-2011, we identified DXAs and short-interval DXAs for female Medicare beneficiaries over age 66.
target: We used only information from Medicare claims in 2006-2011 to find DXAs and short-term DXAs for females over age 66 on Medicare.

source: We determined the population rate of DXAs and short-interval DXAs, as well as Medicare spending on short-interval DXAs, nationally and by hospital referral region (HRR).
target: We found out how many DXAs and short-term DXAs were done, and how many Medicare dollars were spent on short-interval DXAs, across the country and by healthcare markets.

source: Results: DXA use was stable 2008-2011 (12.4 to 11.5 DXAs per 100 women).
target: From 2008-2011, DXA use was steady (12.4 to 11.5 DXAs per 100 women).

source: DXA use varied across HRRs: in 2011, overall DXA use ranged from 6.3 to 23.0 per 100 women (coefficient of variation = 0.18), and short-interval DXAs ranged from 0.3 to 8.0 per 100 women (coefficient of variation = 0.64).
target: DXA use varied across healthcare markets.

source: Short-interval DXA use fluctuated substantially with payment changes; other DXAs did not.
target: Short-interval DXA use changed a lot based on payment; other DXAs did not.

source: Short-interval DXAs, which represented 10.1 % of all DXAs, cost Medicare approximately US$16 million in 2011.
target: In 2011, Medicare spent about $16 million USD on short-interval DXAs, which made up 10.1% of all DXAs.

source: Conclusions: One out of ten DXAs was administered in a time frame shorter than recommended and at a substantial cost to Medicare.
target: We conclude that one out of 10 DXAs was done sooner than recommended, costing Medicare considerably.

source: DXA use varied across regions.
target: DXA use varied across the country.

source: Short-interval DXA use was responsive to reimbursement changes, suggesting carefully designed policy and payment reform may reduce this care identified by rheumatologists as low value.
target: Short-interval DXA use changed based on payment, suggesting policy and payment changes may decrease this low-value care.

source: Background: To develop an OSTAi tool and compare this with the National Osteoporosis Foundation recommendations in 2013 (NOF 2013) for bone mineral density (BMD) testing among Taiwan postmenopausal women.
target: We aimed to make an Osteoporosis (a condition in which bones become weak and brittle) Self-Assessment Tool for Taiwan (OSTAi) and compare this with existing 2013 guidelines for bone mineral density (BMD) testing among Taiwan women who had gone through menopause (a point in time 12 months after the last period).

source: Methods: Taiwan Osteoporosis Association (TOA) conducted a nationwide BMD survey by a bus installed with a dual energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) between 2008 and 2011.
target: We tested women nationwide using a dual energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA; a common text to measure bone density) between 2008 and 2011.

source: All of the participants completed questionnaire, which included demographics and risk factors of osteoporotic fracture in FRAX tool.
target: All women filled out a questionnaire, which included individual information and risk factors of bone breaks caused by osteoporosis.

source: We used the database to analyze potential risk factors for osteoporosis and followed the model by Koh et al. to develop a risk index via multiple variable regression analysis and item reduction.
target: We used this information to look at possible risk factors for osteoporosis.

source: We used the index values to set up a simple algorithm (namely OSTAi) to identify those who need BMD measurement.
target: We set up OSTAi to find those who needed BMD measurement.

source: Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and the area under the curve (AUC) was used to compare the sensitivity/specificity analysis of this model with that of recommendations by NOF 2013.
target: We compared accuracy for disease detection and no detection between OSTAi and the 2013 guidelines.

source: Results: A total of 12,175 Taiwan postmenopausal women enrolled in this survey.
target: We studied 12,175 Taiwan women who had gone through menopause.

source: There are 6393 (52.5%) participants whose index value is below "-1" and whose risk of osteoporosis was 57.5% (3674/6393).
target: There are 6393 (52.5%) participants whose risk of osteoporosis was 57.5% (3674/6393).

source: The sensitivity and specificity of OSTAi, at the selected cutoff value of -1, and NOF 2013 to identify osteoporosis were 73.1%, 62.0% and 78.3%, 45.7%, respectively.
target: OSTAi showed slightly lower accuracy for disease detection and higher accuracy for no detection than 2013 guidelines.

source: Conclusions: As OSTA for Asian populations, OSTAi is an useful tool to identify Taiwan postmenopausal women with osteoporosis,
target: OSTAi is useful to find Taiwan women who have gone through menopause with osteoporosis.

source: In comparison with NOF 2013, OSTAi may be an easier and better tool for referral to BMD measurement by DXA in this area.
target: Compared to current guidelines, OSTAi may be an easier and better tool to determine who should have DXA in Taiwan.

source: Background: To develop an OSTAi tool and compare this with the National Osteoporosis Foundation recommendations in 2013 (NOF 2013) for bone mineral density (BMD) testing among Taiwan postmenopausal women.
target: The study aims to create an osteoporosis (or bone weakening) self assessment (OSTAi) tool and compare it with the National Osteoporosis Foundation 2013 recommendations (NOF 2013) or bone mineral density (BMD) or hardness testing among Taiwan postmenopausal women who completed their final menstrual or sexual cycle.

source: Methods: Taiwan Osteoporosis Association (TOA) conducted a nationwide BMD survey by a bus installed with a dual energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) between 2008 and 2011.
target: Taiwan Osteoporosis Associated (TOA) had a nationwide BMD survey by a bus installed with an X-ray machine between 2008 and 2011.

source: All of the participants completed questionnaire, which included demographics and risk factors of osteoporotic fracture in FRAX tool.
target: All participants completed a questionnaire, which had socioeconomic and risk factors of osteoporotic fracture in fracture risk tool.

source: Results: A total of 12,175 Taiwan postmenopausal women enrolled in this survey.
target: 12,175 Taiwan postmenopausal women participated in this survey.

source: There are 6393 (52.5%) participants whose index value is below "-1" and whose risk of osteoporosis was 57.5% (3674/6393).
target: There are 6393 (52.5%) participants below the cutoff and whose risk of osteoporosis was 57.5% (3647/6393).

source: The AUC for OSTAi and NOF 2013 were 0.739 (95% confidence interval (CI), 0.728-0.749, P<0.001) and 0.618 (95% CI, 0.606-0.630, P<0.001), respectively.
target: The measurements for OSTAi and NOF were noteworthy.

source: The sensitivity and specificity of OSTAi, at the selected cutoff value of -1, and NOF 2013 to identify osteoporosis were 73.1%, 62.0% and 78.3%, 45.7%, respectively.
target: The sensitivity (accuracy of detecting people with the disease) and specificity (accuracy of detecting  people without the disease) of OSTAi at the cutoff and NOF 2013 to detect osteoporosis were 73.1%, 62.0% and 78.3%, 45.7%, respectively.

source: Conclusions: As OSTA for Asian populations, OSTAi is an useful tool to identify Taiwan postmenopausal women with osteoporosis,
target: Known as OSTA for Asian populations, OSTAi is useful to identify Taiwan postmenopausal women with osteoporosis.

source: In comparison with NOF 2013, OSTAi may be an easier and better tool for referral to BMD measurement by DXA in this area.
target: Compared to NOF 2013, OSTAi may be an easier, better tool to suggest BMD measurement by bone scans in this area.

source: Osteoporosis-related fractures affect approximately one in two white women and one in five white men in their lifetime.
target: Bone breaks due to osteoporosis (a condition in which bones become weak and brittle) happen in about 50% of white women and 20% of white men in their lifetime.

source: The impact of fractures includes loss of function, significant costs, and increased mortality.
target: Bone breaks cause loss of physical function, high costs, and increased death.

source: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends using dual energy x-ray absorptiometry to screen all women 65 years and older, and younger women who have an increased fracture risk as determined by the World Health Organization's FRAX Fracture Risk Assessment Tool.
target: An expert panel recommends using dual energy x-ray absorptiometry, or bone density scanning, to check all women 65 year and older, and younger women who are more likely to have bone breaks based on a popular measurement tool to measure fracture risk.

source: Although guidelines are lacking for rescreening women who have normal bone mineral density on initial screening, intervals of at least four years appear safe.
target: Although there are not guidelines for rechecking women who have initial normal bone mineral density, four years between checks looks safe.

source: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force found insufficient evidence to recommend screening for osteoporosis in men; other organizations recommend screening all men 70 years and older.
target: The expert panel did not find enough proof to recommend checking for osteoporosis in men; other groups recommend checking all men 70 years and older.

source: In patients with newly diagnosed osteoporosis, suggested laboratory tests to identify secondary causes include serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D, calcium, creatinine, and thyroid-stimulating hormone.
target: In people with newly found osteoporosis, lab tests to find underlying causes include measuring blood levels of different substances.

source: First-line treatment to prevent fractures consists of fall prevention, smoking cessation, moderation of alcohol intake, and bisphosphonate therapy.
target: The best treatment to prevent breaks includes preventing falls, stopping smoking, reducing alcohol consumption, and a group of drugs that help prevent or slow down bone thinning.

source: Clinicians should consider discontinuing bisphosphonate therapy after five years in women without a personal history of vertebral fractures.
target: Doctors should think about stopping a group of drugs that help prevent or slow down bone thinning after five years in women without a history of spine breaks.

source: Raloxifene, teriparatide, and denosumab are alternative effective treatments for certain subsets of patients and for those who are unable to take or whose condition does not respond to bisphosphonates.
target: Other types of drugs for osteoporosis exist for certain groups of patients and for those who are unable to take or whose condition is not helped by one group of drugs that help prevent or slow down bone thinning.

source: The need for follow-up bone mineral density testing in patients receiving treatment for osteoporosis is uncertain.
target: The need to recheck bone mineral density in people taking drugs for osteoporosis is unknown.

source: We investigated the value of routine laboratory testing for identifying underlying causes in older men diagnosed with osteoporosis.
target: We looked at how helpful routine lab testing is for finding underlying causes in older men diagnosed with osteoporosis (a condition in which bones become weak and brittle).

source: Most osteoporotic and nonosteoporotic men had ≥1 laboratory abnormality.
target: Most men with and without osteoporosis had one or more abnormal lab measurement.

source: Few individual laboratory abnormalities were more common in osteoporotic men.
target: Few single lab abnormalities were more common in men with osteoporosis.

source: The benefit of routine laboratory testing in older osteoporotic men may be low.
target: Routine lab testing in older men with osteoporosis may not be helpful.

source: Introduction: To evaluate the utility of recommended laboratory testing to identify secondary causes in older men with osteoporosis, we examined prevalence of laboratory abnormalities in older men with and without osteoporosis.
target: We aim to rate how useful recommended lab testing is to find underlying causes of osteoporosis in older men by looking at the number of older men with lab abnormalities with and without osteoporosis during a specific period.

source: Methods: One thousand five hundred seventy-two men aged ≥65 years in the Osteoporotic Fractures in Men study completed bone mineral density (BMD) testing and a battery of laboratory measures, including serum calcium, phosphorus, alkaline phosphatase, parathyroid hormone (PTH), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), 25-OH vitamin D, total testosterone, spot urine calcium/creatinine ratio, spot urine albumin/creatinine ratio, creatinine-derived estimated glomerular filtration rate, 24-h urine calcium, and 24-h urine free cortisol.
target: We studied 1,572 men 65 years old and older who has bone mineral density (BMD) testing and many other lab measurements.

source: Using cross-sectional analyses, we calculated prevalence ratios (PRs) and 95 % confidence intervals (CI) for the association of any and specific laboratory abnormalities with osteoporosis and the number of men with osteoporosis needed to test to identify one additional laboratory abnormality compared to testing men without osteoporosis.
target: We checked for links of any lab abnormalities with osteoporosis and the number of men with osteoporosis needed to test to find one more lab abnormality compared to testing men without osteoporosis.

source: Results: Approximately 60 % of men had ≥1 laboratory abnormality in both men with and without osteoporosis.
target: We found that about 60% of men with and without osteoporosis had one or more lab abnormality.

source: Among individual tests, only vitamin D insufficiency (PR, 1.13; 95 % CI, 1.05-1.22) and high alkaline phosphatase (PR, 3.05; 95 % CI, 1.52-6.11) were more likely in men with osteoporosis.
target: Among lab tests, only not enough vitamin D and high alkaline phosphatase (suggesting damage to liver or bone disorder) were more likely in men with osteoporosis.

source: Hypercortisolism and hyperthyroidism were uncommon and not significantly more frequent in men with osteoporosis.
target: Too much cortisol (stress hormone) and overactive thyroid (metabolism-regulating hormone) were rare and not meaningfully more common in men with osteoporosis.

source: No osteoporotic men had hypercalciuria.
target: No men with osteoporosis had high levels of calcium in the urine.

source: Conclusions: Though most of these older men had ≥1 laboratory abnormality, few routinely recommended individual tests were more common in men with osteoporosis than in those without osteoporosis.
target: Though most of these older men had one or more lab abnormality, few often-recommended tests were more common in men with osteoporosis than in those without.

source: Possibly excepting vitamin D and alkaline phosphatase, benefit of routine laboratory testing to identify possible secondary causes in older osteoporotic men appears low.
target: Except for possibly vitamin D and alkaline phosphatase, usefulness of routine lab testing to find possible underlying causes in older men with osteoporosis seems low.

source: Results may not be generalizable to younger men or to older men in whom history and exam findings raise clinical suspicion for a secondary cause of osteoporosis.
target: Results may not apply to younger men or to older men thought to have osteoporosis from underlying causes.

source: Background: Clinical practice guidelines recommend use of fracture risk scores for screening and pharmacologic treatment decisions.
target: Guidelines for patient care recommend use of fracture risk scores, or the likelihood of having a major bone break, for checks and drug treatment decisions.

source: The timing of occurrence of treatment-level (according to 2014 National Osteoporosis Foundation guidelines) or screening-level (according to 2011 US Preventive Services Task Force guidelines) fracture risk scores has not been estimated in postmenopausal women.
target: The timing of treatment-level or screening-level fracture risk scores based on guidelines has not been estimated in women who have gone through menopause (a point in time 12 months after the last period).

source: Methods: We conducted a retrospective competing risk analysis of new occurrence of treatment-level and screening-level fracture risk scores in postmenopausal women aged 50 years and older, prior to receipt of pharmacologic treatment and prior to first hip or clinical vertebral fracture.
target: We measured new occurences of teratment-level and testing-level fracture risk scores in postmenopausal women aged 50 years and older, before receiving drug treatment and the first hip or clinical vertebral fracture.

source: Results: In 54,280 postmenopausal women aged 50 to 64 years without a bone mineral density test, the time for 10% to develop a treatment-level FRAX score could not be estimated accurately because of rare incidence of treatment-level scores.
target: In 54,280 women who had gone through menopause aged 50 to 64 years without a bone mineral density test, we could not estimate the time for 10% to need treatment based on a common osteoporosis questionnaire due to too few scores suggesting treatment.

source: In 6096 women who had FRAX scores calculated with bone mineral density, the estimated unadjusted time to treatment-level FRAX ranged from 7.6 years (95% confidence interval [CI], 6.6-8.7) for those aged 65 to 69, to 5.1 years (95% CI, 3.5-7.5) for those aged 75 to 79 at baseline.
target: In 6096 women who had scores from a common osteoporosis questionnaire, the estimated time to need treatment was 7.6 years and 5.1 years for those aged 65 to 69 and aged 75 to 79, respectively.

source: Of 17,967 women aged 50 to 64 with a screening-level FRAX at baseline, 100 (0.6%) experienced a hip or clinical vertebral fracture by age 65 years.
target: Of 17,967 women aged 50 to 64 with an initial screening-level FRAX (or fracture-risk score), 100 (0.6%) broke their hip or spine by age 65 years.

source: Conclusions: Postmenopausal women with sub-threshold fracture risk scores at baseline were unlikely to develop a treatment-level FRAX score between ages 50 and 64 years.
target: We conclude that women who have gone through menopause with questionnaire scores that did not suggest treatment were unlikely to have a score suggesting treatment between ages 50 and 64 years.

source: After age 65, the increased incidence of treatment-level fracture risk scores, osteoporosis, and major osteoporotic fracture supports more frequent consideration of FRAX and bone mineral density testing.
target: After age 65, questionnaire scores suggesting treatment, osteoporosis, and serious bone breaks due to osteoporosis suggest doing more questionnaires and bone mineral density testing more often.

source: Background: Clinical practice guidelines recommend use of fracture risk scores for screening and pharmacologic treatment decisions.
target: Healtchare guidelines recommend using fracture risk scores for screening and drug treatment decisions.

source: The timing of occurrence of treatment-level (according to 2014 National Osteoporosis Foundation guidelines) or screening-level (according to 2011 US Preventive Services Task Force guidelines) fracture risk scores has not been estimated in postmenopausal women.
target: The timing of occurence of treatment-level (according to 2014 guidelines by an expert goup) or screening-level (according to 2011 guidelines by national experts) fracture risk scores has not been estimated in postmenopausal women who finished their last menstrual cycle.

source: Methods: We conducted a retrospective competing risk analysis of new occurrence of treatment-level and screening-level fracture risk scores in postmenopausal women aged 50 years and older, prior to receipt of pharmacologic treatment and prior to first hip or clinical vertebral fracture.
target: We conducted a risk analysis of new occurence of treatment- and screening-level fracture risk scores in postmenopausal women aged 50 years and older, before receiving drug treatment and hip or spine fracture.

source: Results: In 54,280 postmenopausal women aged 50 to 64 years without a bone mineral density test, the time for 10% to develop a treatment-level FRAX score could not be estimated accurately because of rare incidence of treatment-level scores.
target: In 54, 280 postmenopausal women aged 50 to 64 years without a bone hardness test, the time for 10% to get a treatment-level fracture risk score could not be estimated correctly due to rare amount of treatment-level scores.

source: In 6096 women who had FRAX scores calculated with bone mineral density, the estimated unadjusted time to treatment-level FRAX ranged from 7.6 years (95% confidence interval [CI], 6.6-8.7) for those aged 65 to 69, to 5.1 years (95% CI, 3.5-7.5) for those aged 75 to 79 at baseline.
target: In 6096 women who had fracture risk scores calculated with bone hardness, the estimated unadjusted time to treatment-level fracture risk ranged from 7.6 years for those aged 65 to 69, to 5.1 years, for those aged 75 to 79 at the start.

source: Of 17,967 women aged 50 to 64 with a screening-level FRAX at baseline, 100 (0.6%) experienced a hip or clinical vertebral fracture by age 65 years.
target: Of 17,967 women aged 50 to 64 with a screening-level fracture risk score at the start, 100 (0.6%) had a hip or back fracture by age 65 years.

source: Conclusions: Postmenopausal women with sub-threshold fracture risk scores at baseline were unlikely to develop a treatment-level FRAX score between ages 50 and 64 years.
target: Postmenopausal women with sub-threshold fracture risk scores at the start were unlikely to get a treatment-level fracture risk score between 50 and 64 years.

source: After age 65, the increased incidence of treatment-level fracture risk scores, osteoporosis, and major osteoporotic fracture supports more frequent consideration of FRAX and bone mineral density testing.
