target: Because extra fingers are common and may have different causes, no tests were done.

source: He then presented at age 16 years with a headache and subjective visual changes, with brain imaging revealing a hypothalamic hamartoma.
target: At the age of 16 he started having headaches and changes in vision. Brain imaging showed a hypothalamic hamartoma, a non-cancer growth in the brain tissues.

source: He did not have a history of seizures or central precocious puberty.
target: He did not have seizures or premature puberty.

source: Genotyping revealed a pathogenic variant affecting the GLI3 gene.
target: Genetic testing showed harmful variants.

source: We encourage all clinicians to consider PHS or an associated syndrome with a clinical finding of polydactyly.
target: Clinicians should think about PHS when they see extra fingers and toes.

source: Further, as the natural history continues to reveal itself, this patient's presentation provides important new data to the broad phenotypic spectrum of PHS.
target: The disease signs in this patient add new knowledge about PHS.

source: Introduction: Pallister-Hall syndrome (PHS) is a rare autosomal dominant syndrome characterized by polydactyly, bifid or shortened epiglottis, visceral anomalies, hypothalamic hamartoma often combined with hypopituitarism.
target: Pallister-Hall syndrome (PHS) is a rare disease passed down from one of the parents. Its signs are six fingers and toes, shortened or split flap that protects the windpipe, problems with the organs in the chest and belly, non-cancer mass in the brain that often causes problems with pituitary gland, which regulates body functions and growth.

source: PHS is characterized by significant variability in the expression of clinical symptoms.
target: Signs of PHS in different patients are very different.

source: The clinical course ranges from mild with a good prognosis to severe and which can lead to death during the neonatal period.
target: PHS ranges from mild with good outcomes to severe that can lead to death of the newborns.

source: Case report: Two-years-old girl with facial dysmorphia, skeletal malformations of hand and feet and growth hormone deficiency.
target: We describe a two-year-old girl with deformities of the face, hand and feet and dwarfism.

source: PHS was diagnosed on the basis of the presented symptoms and genetic tests.
target: PHS was diagnosed on the basis of the presented symptoms and genetic tests.

source: Summary: Skeletal malformations, such as polydactyly or oligodactyly, are a markers which can be associated with endocrinological disorders.
target: Abnormalities, such as the presence of extra or fewer than five fingers or toes, are associated with disorders of the endocrine system, which includes the glands that regulate body functions through chemicals called hormones.

source: Quick and correct diagnosis would help in planning treatment during childhood and giving family counseling, including prenatal advice regarding the next pregnancy of the child's mother.
target: Quick and correct diagnosis would help in planning treatment for the child and advise the family regarding the next pregnancy of the child's mother.

source: Traumatic injury to the peripheral nerves often results in persistent discomfort.
target: After peripheral nerves (the parts of the nervous system outside the brain and spinal cord) are injured, people often feel discomfort for a long time.

source: Substance P has been implicated as a mediator of pain, and depletion of this neurotransmitter has been shown to reduce pain.
target: A chemical called Substance P is involved in the process of feeling pain. Lowering the amount of this chemical involved in the transmission of nerve signals lessens pain.

source: Subjects suffering from traumatic dysesthesia of the trigeminal nerve were treated with capsaicin, a substance P depleter with significant long-term effects.
target: Capsaicin was a good long-term treatment for people who experienced an unpleasant abnormal sensation after injury to the trigeminal nerve. Trigeminal nerve is a large, three-part nerve in the head that provides sensation.  Capsaicin is a chemical that lowers the amount of Substance P  in the body.

source: This form of therapy may be used individually or in combination with other pharmacologic interventions in the treatment of traumatic trigeminal dysesthesia.
target: Drugs that lower the amount of Substance P  in the body may be used alone or together with other medications to treat traumatic trigeminal dysesthesia (an unpleasant abnormal sensation after injury to the trigeminal nerve.)

source: Objective: To report a novel wireless neuromodulation system for treatment of refractory craniofacial pain.
target: This study presents a new wireless nerve stimulation system for treatment of uncontrollable pain of the head and face.

source: Background: Previous studies utilizing peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) of the occipital and trigeminal nerves reported positive outcomes for alleviating neuropathic pain localized to the craniofacial and occipital areas.
target: Previous studies reported that stimulation of the nerves called trigeminal (meaning "threefold", for the three branches of the nerve)  and occipital (relating to the back of the head) reduces pain in the head and face.

source: However several technological limitations and cosmetic concerns inhibited a more widespread acceptance and use of neuromodulation.
target: Due to the shortcomings of the devices and concerns about the appearance, the nerve stimulation systems are not used widely.

source: Also, a relatively high incidence of adverse events like electrode erosions, dislocation, wire fracture and/or infection at the surgical site mandates a change in our approach to neuromodulation technology and implant techniques in the craniofacial region.
target: The relatively common complications are worn and broken wires, devices moving from the original placement, and infection around the device. To avoid these complications, we need to change the nerve stimulation systems and the way they are implanted (placed) in the head and face.

source: Methods: We report a novel approach for the management of craniofacial pain with a wirelessly powered, minimally invasive PNS system.
target: We describe a new peripheral nerve stimulation (PNS) system for treatment of pain in the head and neck. The system is wireless and is placed using small cuts and few stitches.

source: The system is percutaneously implanted and placed subcutaneously adjacent to affected facial nerves via visual guidance by the clinician.
target: The doctors place the system under the skin, next to the damaged facial nerves

source: In this feasibility study, pilot evidence was gathered in a cohort of ten subjects suffering from a combination of chronic headaches, facial pain for at least 15 days per month and for at least 4h/day.
target: This study included ten patients who experienced chronic headaches and facial pain for four or more hours a day and at least 15 days a month.

source: Results: At four weeks post-implant follow up, all patients reported sustained pain relief of the primary pain area.
target: Four weeks after the systems were implanted, all patients felt continuing ain relief.

source: Electrode location and total number of electrodes used per subject varied across the cohort.
target: The patients had different numbers of wires and implant locations.

source: The average pain reduction using the visual analog scale was ≥82%.
target: On average, patients felt five times less pain after treatment.

source: The procedure had no adverse events or side effects.
target: There were no complications

source: Conclusion: Percutaneous placement of a wireless neurostimulation device directly adjacent to affected craniofacial nerve(s) is a minimally invasive and reversible method of pain control in patients with craniofacial pain refractory to conventional medical managements.
target: Placing a wireless nerve stimulation device under the skin next to the damaged nerves in the head and neck reduces pain that is hard to control otherwise. This treatment is gentle and can be reversed.

source: Trigeminal neuralgia is caused by compression of trigeminal nerve root and it leads to demyelination gradually.
target: Trigeminal nerve pain (neuralgia) is caused by pressure on the trigeminal (meaning "threefold", for the three branches of the nerve)  nerve. The pressure leads to gradual loss of the protecting layer that covers the nerve.

source: It was almost idiopathic and occurred unexpected.
target: It arises spontaneously and unexpected.

source: The upper cervical spinal cord contains the spinal trigeminal tract and nucleus.
target: The spinal cord is the bundle of nerve tissues that connects the brain with the body. In the upper neck region, it contains parts of the trigeminal nerve.

source: Fibers with cell bodies in the trigeminal ganglion enter in the upper pons and descend caudally to C2 level.
target: The trigeminal nerve cells and fibers enter the brainstem, the bottom part of the brain connected to the spinal cord, and go down to the second vertebrae at the top of the cervical spine.

source: We experienced a rare patient with facial pain, which was paroxysmal attack with severe pain after a clear event, cervical spinal injury (C2).
target: A rare patient had severe repeated bouts of facial pain after an injury to the second vertebrae at the top of the neck region.

source: So, this case reminds us of a possible cause of trigeminal neuralgia after a trauma of the head and neck.
target: This patient reminds us that trigeminal neuralgia may be caused by injuries to the head and neck.

source: Orofacial pain syndromes encompass several clinically defined and classified entities.
target: Orofacial pain syndromes are diseases known to cause pain in the face and mouth.

source: The focus here is on the role of clinical neurophysiologic and psychophysical tests in the diagnosis, differential diagnosis, and pathophysiological mechanisms of definite trigeminal neuropathic pain and other chronic orofacial pain conditions (excluding headache and temporomandibular disorders).
target: We discuss clinical examinations of the neural system that help identifying trigeminal nerve pain and other chronic facial pain conditions, but not headaches and problems with TMJ.

source: The International Classification of Headache Disorders 2018 classifies these facial pain disorders under the heading Painful cranial neuropathies and other facial pains.
target: The International Classification of Headache Disorders 2018 classifies these facial pain disorders under the heading Painful cranial neuropathies and other facial pains.

source: In addition to unambiguous painful posttraumatic or postherpetic trigeminal neuropathies, burning mouth syndrome, persistent idiopathic facial and dental pain, and trigeminal neuralgia have also been identified with neurophysiologic and quantitative sensory testing to involve the nervous system.
target: Clinical examinations of the nervous system help identifying painful conditions of the trigeminal nerve, which carries sensation from the face to the brain. These conditions may start after an injury (posttraumatic trigeminal neuropathy) or herpes infection (postherpetic trigeminal neuropathy). Other related conditions are burning mouth syndrome (a burning, scalding, or tingling feeling in the mouth) and persistent idiopathic facial and dental pain, which is described as continuing pain in the face and teeth of unknown cause.

source: Despite normal clinical examination, these all include clusters of patients with evidence for either peripheral or central nervous system pathology compatible with the subclinical end of a continuum of trigeminal neuropathic pain conditions.
target: Even patients who appear normal on clinical examination have damage to their nervous system that can cause trigeminal nerve pain.

source: Useful tests in the diagnostic process include electroneuromyography with specific needle, neurography techniques for the inferior alveolar and infraorbital nerves, brain stem reflex recordings (blink reflex with stimulation of the supraorbital, infraorbital, mental, and lingual nerves; jaw jerk; masseter silent period), evoked potential recordings, and quantitative sensory testing.
target: Diagnostic tests include recording electrical signals in the muscles (electroneuromyography), MRI of the nerves around the eyes, blink reflexes and jaw jerk when different nerves are stimulated, recording electrical signals after the nerves are stimulated, and tests for pain sensation.

source: Habituation of the blink reflex and evoked potential responses to repeated stimuli evaluate top-down inhibition, and navigated transcranial magnetic stimulation allows the mapping of reorganization within the motor cortex in chronic neuropathic pain.
target: Responsiveness of the blink reflex and records of the electrical signals after repeated nerve stimulation show if the response is suppressed. Medical imaging called navigated transcranial magnetic stimulation shows the areas of the brain that change due to chronic nerve pain.

source: With systematic use of neurophysiologic and quantitative sensory testing, many of the current ambiguities in the diagnosis, classification, and understanding of chronic orofacial syndromes can be clarified for clinical practice and future research.
target: Consistent use of the above tests will help understanding the chronic facial pain conditions.

source: Introduction: Trigeminal neuralgia is an exemplary neuropathic pain condition characterized by paroxysmal electric-shock-like pain.
target: Neuralgia is pain in the nerves that feels as bursts of electric-shock-like pain.

source: However, up to 50% of patients also experiences concomitant continuous pain.
target: Up tp half of the patients also experience continuous pain.

source: In this neuroimaging study, we aimed to identify the specific anatomical features of trigeminal nerve root in patients with concomitant continuous pain.
target: We study the specific anatomical features of trigeminal nerve root (a part of the nerve that helps the face recognize pain, touch, heat and cold sensations, as well as chewing. We studied patients with continuous pain.

source: Methods: We enrolled 73 patients with a definitive diagnosis of classical and idiopathic trigeminal neuralgia and 40 healthy participants.
target: We studied 73 patients with trigeminal neuralgia (nerve pain) and 40 healthy participants.

source: The diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia was independently confirmed by two clinicians.
target: The diagnosis of trigeminal neuralgia was independently confirmed by two clinicians.

source: Patients were grouped as patients with purely paroxysmal pain (45 patients) and patients also with concomitant continuous pain (28 patients).
target: 45 patients had only Burts of pain and 28 patients had continuous pain along with the bursts.

source: All participants underwent a structured clinical examination and a 3T MRI with sequences dedicated to the anatomical study of the trigeminal nerve root, including volumetric study.
target: All patients had clinical examination and MRI.

source: Images analysis was independently performed by two investigators, blinded to any clinical data.
target: Two specialists studied the images without losing at the clinical data.

source: Results: In most patients with concomitant continuous pain, this type of pain, described as burning, throbbing or aching, manifested at the disease onset.
target: In most patients with continuous pain it started as burning, throbbing or aching sensation.

source: Demographic and clinical variables did not differ between the two groups of patients; the frequency of neurovascular compression and nerve dislocation were similar.
target: The patients in the two groups and the frequency and location of the nerve damage signs in these patients were similar

source: Conversely, trigeminal nerve root atrophy was more severe in patients with concomitant continuous pain than in those with purely paroxysmal pain (p = 0.006).
target: On the contrary, patients with both the bursts and continuous pain had more atrophy ((decrease in size) of the trigeminal nerve than the patients with bursts only.

source: Conclusions: Our clinical and neuroimaging study found that in patients with trigeminal neuralgia, concomitant continuous pain was associated with trigeminal nerve root atrophy, therefore suggesting that this type of pain is likely related to axonal loss and abnormal activity in denervated trigeminal second-order neurons.
target: Clinical examination of patients with trigeminal nerve pain and their medical images show that patients who experienced continuous pain along with pain bursts lost some nerve tissues. This type of pain is related to wrong signaling in the nerve that lost tissues.

source: Introduction: The complex nature of facial pain conditions creates a diagnostic challenge which may necessitate specialist referral.
target: Facial pain may be hard to recognize and a specialist might need to examine the patient.

source: Aim: To identify the case mix presenting to a specialist tertiary care facial pain clinic.
target: The study's aim was to identify the mix of patient cases at a specialist facial pain clinic.

source: Methods: A retrospective review of 112 patient records was undertaken.
target: We reviewed the records of 112 patients who visited the specialists at the facial pain clinic.

source: Trends in provisional diagnoses from referrers and the correlation to diagnoses made following specialist consultation were reviewed.
target: We compared the reasons for specialist consultation  given by the primary doctors to the diagnoses made by the specialists.

source: Results: The most common provisional diagnoses recorded in referral letters were painful temporomandibular disorders, trigeminal neuralgia and persistent idiopathic facial pain (PIFP).
target: Most often the primary doctors thought the patients' pain was caused by problems with the  temporomandibular joints that connect the lower jaw to the skull; pain in the trigeminal nerve that sends signals about sensations in the face to the brain; and other facial pain.

source: Over a quarter of referrals did not include a provisional diagnosis.
target: Over a quarter of patients referred to the cliniic had no diagnosis.

source: Following assessment, only one case was not given a definitive diagnosis and no patients were diagnosed with PIFP.
target: The specialist could not diagnose only one patient and have not diagnosed any patients with unspecified facial pain.

source: A causative factor was identified in all the initially queried PIFP cases, and painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain was found in multiple patients.
target: The specialists found causes of all previously unspecified facial pain. Many patients had trigeminal nerve pain following an injury (Painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain ).

source: Conclusions: Painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain should be considered if pain onset coincides with dental treatment or other traumatic events.
target: Painful post-traumatic trigeminal neuropathic pain should be considered if pain started after dental treatment or injury.

source: PIFP is a rare facial pain diagnosis and may be over-diagnosed by dental and medical practitioners.
target: Unspecified facial pain is rare and may be overused by dentists and physicians.

source: It is important to systematically exclude other causes before reaching this diagnosis.
target: All other causes should be excluded before diagnosis of the unspecified facial pain.

source: This will facilitate effective treatment, manage patient expectations and potentially reduce unnecessary referrals.
target: This will improve treatment and patients' expectations. It may reduce unnecessary referrals.

source: Background: Neuropathic facial pain occurs due to pathologic dysfunctions of a nerve responsible for mediating sensory fibers to the head.
target: Neural facial pain is caused by the nerves that transmit sensation to the head.

source: Surgical interventions, in cases of failed medical therapy, include microvascular decompression, radiofrequency (RF) ablation, percutaneous balloon decompression, and stereotactic radiosurgery.
target: If medicines do not help, surgery may be used. Surgeries relieve compressed nerves (microvascular decompression); or shrink tissues using radio waves (radiofrequency (RF) ablation) or local radiation (stereotactic radiosurgery). Other surgeries prevent the nerve from transmitting signals (percutaneous balloon decompression.)

source: In this review, we focused on RF ablation as a treatment for chronic facial pain.
target: We discuss using radio waves (RF ablation) to treat chronic facial pain.

source: Objectives: The objective of this review was to summarize available evidence behind RF ablation for facial pain, including pain outcome measures, secondary outcomes, and complications.
target: We summarize what is known about RF ablation for facial pain, including treatment results and complications.

source: Results: We reviewed 44 studies and assessed their short- and long-term pain relief measurements, as well as secondary outcomes including patient satisfaction, quality of life improvements, decrease in oral medication use, and recurrence rates.
target: We summarize the pain relief results, patient satisfaction, quality of life, decrease in the drug use, and how often the pain returns.

source: Maximal pain relief was achieved in treatment groups using combined continuous radiofrequency (CRF) and pulsed radiofrequency (PRF) therapies, followed by CRF therapy alone and finally PRF therapy alone.
target: Combining two different types of radio wave treatments (continuous radiofrequency (CRF) and pulsed radiofrequency (PRF))reduced pain the best.  CRF treatment was second best, followed by PRF.

source: All treatment regimens improved secondary outcomes.
target: All treatments improved patient satisfaction and quality of life, and decreased drug use and return of the pain.

source: Common complications of treatment included facial numbness, masseter weakness, cheek hematomas, diminished corneal reflex, and dry eyes.
target: Common complications of the treatment included facial numbness,  weakness of facial muscles, bruised cheeks, failure to blink when the eye is touched, and dry eyes.

source: Conclusions: This systematic review found evidence that RF ablation is efficient in treating patients with facial pain, as well as in improving quality of life and reducing oral medication use.
target: We conclude that radio wave treatment (RF ablation) is efficient in treating patients with facial pain. It also improves quality of life and reduces drug use.

source: Maximal pain control is achieved using combined CRF and PRF therapy.
target: Maximal pain control is achieved using combined CRF and PRF therapy.

source: Complications are uncommon and include facial numbness, masseter weakness, cheek hematomas, diminished corneal reflex, and dry eyes.
target: Complications of the treatment were not frequent and included facial numbness,  weakness of facial muscles, bruised cheeks, failure to blink when the eye is touched, and dry eyes.

source: Introduction: Facial pain (FP) is a type of neuropathic pain which recognizes both central and peripheral causes.
target: Facial pain is nerve pain caused by nerves in the spinal cord and outside the spinal cord and brain.

source: It can be difficult to treat because it can often become resistant to pharmacological treatments.
target: It can be difficult to treat because pain relieve drugs may stop working.

source: Motor Cortex Stimulation (MCS) has been used in selected cases, but the correct indications of MCS in FP have not been fully established.
target: Motor Cortex Stimulation (MCS), surgical stimulation of the nerves, is sometimes used, but it is not known yet when to use it for facial pain.

source: Here we systematically reviewed the literature regarding MCS in FP analysing the results of this technique and studying the possible role of different factors in the prognosis of these patients.
target: We review what is known about treating patients with facial pain using MCS and the outcomes of the treatment.

source: Results: 111 articles were reviewed, and 12 studies were included in the present analysis for a total of 108 patients.
target: We found information about 108 patients.

source: Overall, at latest follow-up (FU), 70.83% of patients responded to MCS.
target: MCS helped almost three quarters of the patients.

source: The preoperative VAS significantly decreased at the latest FU (8.83 ± 1.17 and 4.31 ± 2.05, respectively; p < 0.0001).
target: The patients felt their pain was reduced almost to half of the pain they felt before surgery.

source: Younger age (p = 0.0478) and a peripheral FP syndrome (p = 0.0006) positively affected the definitive implantation rate on univariate analysis.
target: The treatment was more effective in younger patients and those with pain on the side of the face.

source: Younger age emerged as a factor strongly associated to a higher probability to go to a definitive MCS implant on multivariate analysis (p = 0.0415).
target: Younger patients were more likely to get MCS implanted devices

source: Conclusion: Our results evidenced the effectiveness of MCS in treating FP.
target: MCS is an effective treatment for facial pain.

source: Moreover, the younger age emerged as a positive prognostic factor for definitive implantation.
target: Younger patients were more likely to get permanent implanted devices.

source: Post-traumatic trigeminal neuralgia (PTTN), also known as anesthesia dolorosa, is at times a debilitating affliction, but remains a condition with minimal research and without definitive treatment, specifically in the periorbital and malar regions.
target: Post-traumatic trigeminal neuralgia (PTTN), also known as anesthesia dolorosa, refers to the pain in the trigeminal nerve, a three-part nerve in the head that provides sensation. PTTN is a crippling pain that has no established treatments, specifically for the pain around the eyes and back teeth.

source: Below we present a case of PTTN in a patient with historic facial trauma who has successfully achieved resolution of pain.
target: We describe how trigeminal nerve pain was treated in a patient who had it due to an old injury to his face.

source: We describe diagnostic and therapeutic anesthesia blocks and ablative procedures targeting the zygomaticofacial and zygomaticotemporal nerves.
target: We describe surgery and treatment that blocked sensation in the facial nerves around the temples and cheek bones.

source: We promote awareness for the procedures and the potential large impact on the oral and maxillofacial surgery community when treating those suffering from facial pain.
target: This treatment could be used by dental surgeons who treat patients with facial pain.

source: Finally, we present an algorithm that can aid surgeons in diagnosing and treating patients with PTTN.
target: We also describe a decision support tool for recognizing and treating PTTN.

source: The current understanding of ON is that it causes neuropathic pain in the distribution of the greater occipital nerve, the lesser occipital nerve, the third occipital nerve or a combination of the 3.
target: Occipital Neuralgia is the pain in the occipital nerves, the nerves that run from the top of the spinal cord up in the back of the head.

source: It is currently a subset of headaches although there is some debate if ON should be its own condition.
target: It is considered a type of headaches, but some doctors suggest pain in the nerves in the back of the head is a different problem.

source: Occipital neuralgia causes chronic, sharp, stabbing pain in the upper neck, back of the head, and behind the ears that can radiate to the front of the head.
target: Occipital neuralgia is a continuing, sharp, stabbing pain in the upper neck, back of the head, and behind the ears. It can also extend to the front of the head.

source: Diagnosis is typically clinical and patients present with intermittent, painful episodes associated with the occipital region and the nerves described above.
target: The doctors decide the patients have the disease if the pain in the back of the patients' heads comes and goes.

source: Most cases are unilateral pain, however bilateral pain can be present and the pain can radiate to the frontal region and face.
target: Most patients have pain on one side of their head, but the pain could also be on both sides and extend to the front of the head and face.

source: Physical examination is the first step in management of this disease and patients may demonstrate tenderness over the greater occipital and lesser occipital nerves.
target: When the doctors examine the patients, the patients' skin over the nerves in the back of the head  may be tender.

source: Anesthetics like 1% to 2% lidocaine or 0.25% to 0.5% bupivacaine can be used to block these nerves and anti-inflammatory drugs like corticosteroids can be used in combination to prevent compressive symptoms.
target: Drugs like lidocaine can block the feeling of pain in these nerves. Drugs that reduce inflammation, such as steroids may be added to keep the signs from happening.

source: Other treatments like botulinum toxin and radiofrequency ablation have shown promise and require more research.
target: Other treatments like botulinum toxin that blocks the nerve signals and radiofrequency ablation that uses radio waves to shrink tissues may be promising but need to be studied more.

source: Surgical decompression through resection of the obliquus capitis inferior is the definitive treatment however there are significant risks associated with this procedure.
target: Surgeons may permanently relieve the pressure on the nerve by removing a small muscle between the neck vertebra at the base of the head, but this treatment is risky.

source: Background: Use of heterologous prime-boost COVID-19 vaccine schedules could facilitate mass COVID-19 immunisation.
target: Boosters that are not of the same type as the first COVID-19 vaccine can make global COVID-19 immunization easier.

source: However, we have previously reported that heterologous schedules incorporating an adenoviral vectored vaccine (ChAdOx1 nCoV-19, AstraZeneca; hereafter referred to as ChAd) and an mRNA vaccine (BNT162b2, Pfizer-BioNTech; hereafter referred to as BNT) at a 4-week interval are more reactogenic than homologous schedules.
target: However, we know that getting AstraZeneca and Pfizer 4 weeks apart causes more side effects than getting the same vaccine.

source: Here, we report the safety and immunogenicity of heterologous schedules with the ChAd and BNT vaccines.
target: We report the safety and protective strength of different ways to combine AstraZeneca and Pfizer vaccines.

source: Adults aged 50 years and older with no or well controlled comorbidities and no previous SARS-CoV-2 infection by laboratory confirmation were eligible and were recruited at eight sites across the UK.
target: Healthy adults aged 50 years and older that did not have COVID-19 participated in a clinical trial.

source: The majority of eligible participants were enrolled into the general cohort (28-day or 84-day prime-boost intervals), who were randomly assigned (1:1:1:1:1:1:1:1) to receive ChAd/ChAd, ChAd/BNT, BNT/BNT, or BNT/ChAd, administered at either 28-day or 84-day prime-boost intervals.
target: Most participants got two doses of AstraZeneca or Pfizer or a combination of Pfizer/AstraZeneca or AstraZeneca/Pfizer 28 or 84 days apart.

source: A small subset of eligible participants (n=100) were enrolled into an immunology cohort, who had additional blood tests to evaluate immune responses; these participants were randomly assigned (1:1:1:1) to the four schedules (28-day interval only).
target: 100 of the participants that had the vaccines 28 days apart was checked for the protective chemicals in the blood more often.

source: Participants were masked to the vaccine received but not to the prime-boost interval.
target: Participants did not know which vaccines they got.

source: The primary endpoint was the geometric mean ratio (GMR) of serum SARS-CoV-2 anti-spike IgG concentration (measured by ELISA) at 28 days after boost, when comparing ChAd/BNT with ChAd/ChAd, and BNT/ChAd with BNT/BNT.
target: 28 days after boost, chemicals that fight the SARS-CoV-2 virus were measured in patients' blood.

source: The heterologous schedules were considered non-inferior to the approved homologous schedules if the lower limit of the one-sided 97·5% CI of the GMR of these comparisons was greater than 0·63.
target: Different-vaccine boosters were considered to be as good as the same-vaccine if the increase in the protective chemicals after the booster was the same or higher for the different vaccines.

source: The primary analysis was done in the per-protocol population, who were seronegative at baseline.
target: Protection against COVID-19 was measured in all participants.

source: Safety analyses were done among participants receiving at least one dose of a study vaccine.
target: Safety of the vaccine was measured among participants that had at least one dose of the vaccine.

source: Findings: Between Feb 11 and Feb 26, 2021, 830 participants were enrolled and randomised, including 463 participants with a 28-day prime-boost interval, for whom results are reported here.
target: For 463 participants with 28 days between the first vaccine and the booster, the results are reported here.

source: The mean age of participants was 57·8 years (SD 4·7), with 212 (46%) female participants and 117 (25%) from ethnic minorities.
target: The participants were 54 to 65 years old. About half (212) were women and a quarter (117) were from ethnic minorities.

source: At day 28 post boost, the geometric mean concentration of SARS-CoV-2 anti-spike IgG in ChAd/BNT recipients (12 906 ELU/mL) was non-inferior to that in ChAd/ChAd recipients (1392 ELU/mL), with a GMR of 9·2 (one-sided 97·5% CI 7·5 to ∞).
target: 28 days after the booster, in people who got boosted with Pfizer after AstraZeneca, protection against the SARS-CoV-2 virus was not worse than in those who got the same AstraZeneca booster.

source: In participants primed with BNT, we did not show non-inferiority of the heterologous schedule (BNT/ChAd, 7133 ELU/mL) against the homologous schedule (BNT/BNT, 14 080 ELU/mL), with a GMR of 0·51 (one-sided 97·5% CI 0·43 to ∞).
target: Boosting with AstraZeneca after Pfizer was not as good as getting the second dose of the same Pfizer vaccine.

source: Four serious adverse events occurred across all groups, none of which were considered to be related to immunisation.
target: Four people had serious health problems during the trial. The problems were not related to getting the vaccines.

source: Interpretation: Despite the BNT/ChAd regimen not meeting non-inferiority criteria, the SARS-CoV-2 anti-spike IgG concentrations of both heterologous schedules were higher than that of a licensed vaccine schedule (ChAd/ChAd) with proven efficacy against COVID-19 disease and hospitalisation.
target: Although getting AstraZeneca after Pfizer was not as good as getting only Pfizer or Pfizer after AstraZeneca, there were still more protective chemicals against SARS-CoV-2 in the blood compared to getting only AstraZeneca. Getting only AstraZeneca is known to protect against COVID-19 and hospitalization.

source: Along with the higher immunogenicity of ChAd/BNT compared with ChAD/ChAd, these data support flexibility in the use of heterologous prime-boost vaccination using ChAd and BNT COVID-19 vaccines.
target: This trial supports using boosters that are not the same as the first COVID-19 vaccine.

source: Background: While Coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19) vaccines are highly effective, breakthrough infections are occurring.
target: Coronavirus disease 2019 vaccines protect against Covid-19 well, but some vaccinated people may get sick, which is called breakthrough infections.

source: Booster vaccinations have recently received emergency use authorization (EUA) for certain populations but are restricted to homologous mRNA vaccines.
target: Boosters with the same Pfizer or Moderna vaccines are approved as an emergency measure for some people.

source: We evaluated homologous and heterologous booster vaccination in persons who had received an EUA Covid-19 vaccine regimen.
target: We evaluated boosters with the same and different vaccines in people who got the emergency Covid-19 vaccination.

source: Methods: In this phase 1/2 open-label clinical trial conducted at ten U.S. sites, adults who received one of three EUA Covid-19 vaccines at least 12 weeks prior to enrollment and had no reported history of SARS-CoV-2 infection received a booster injection with one of three vaccines (Moderna mRNA-1273 100-μg, Janssen Ad26.COV2.S 5×10 10 virus particles, or Pfizer-BioNTech BNT162b2 30-μg; nine combinations).
target: In this clinical trial, adults in ten U.S. locations got Moderna, Janssen or Pfizer boosters at least 12 weeks after getting the first vaccine.

source: The primary outcomes were safety, reactogenicity, and humoral immunogenicity on study days 15 and 29.
target: We evaluated the vaccine safety and protection against COVID-19 on study days 15 and 29.

source: Results: 458 individuals were enrolled: 154 received mRNA-1273, 150 received Ad26.CoV2.S, and 153 received BNT162b2 booster vaccines.
target: Out of 458 people, 154 got Moderna, 150 got Janssen and 153 got Pfizer boosters.

source: Reactogenicity was similar to that reported for the primary series.
target: Adverse reactions to the booster were similar to those reported for the first vaccine.

source: Injection site pain, malaise, headache, and myalgia occurred in more than half the participants.
target: More than half of the participants had pain at the injection site, overall weakness, headache, and muscle pains.

source: Booster vaccines increased the neutralizing activity against a D614G pseudovirus (4.2-76-fold) and binding antibody titers (4.6-56-fold) for all combinations; homologous boost increased neutralizing antibody titers 4.2-20-fold whereas heterologous boost increased titers 6.2-76-fold.
target: Boosters with the same and different vaccines increased protection against the virus. The increase in protection after a different booster was higher.

source: Conclusion: Homologous and heterologous booster vaccinations were well-tolerated and immunogenic in adults who completed a primary Covid-19 vaccine regimen at least 12 weeks earlier.
target: Boosters with the same and different vaccines were well-tolerated and increased protection against COVID-19 in adults who had the first vaccine at least 12 weeks earlier.

source: Background: Given the importance of flexible use of different COVID-19 vaccines within the same schedule to facilitate rapid deployment, we studied mixed priming schedules incorporating an adenoviral-vectored vaccine (ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 [ChAd], AstraZeneca), two mRNA vaccines (BNT162b2 [BNT], Pfizer-BioNTech, and mRNA-1273 [m1273], Moderna) and a nanoparticle vaccine containing SARS-CoV-2 spike glycoprotein and Matrix-M adjuvant (NVX-CoV2373 [NVX], Novavax).
target: It is important to have flexibility in using different COVID-19 vaccines for additional doses. We studied mixing AstraZeneca, Pfizer, Moderna and Novavax, which work differently.

source: Methods: Com-COV2 is a single-blind, randomised, non-inferiority trial in which adults aged 50 years and older, previously immunised with a single dose of ChAd or BNT in the community, were randomly assigned (in random blocks of three and six) within these cohorts in a 1:1:1 ratio to receive a second dose intramuscularly (8-12 weeks after the first dose) with the homologous vaccine, m1273, or NVX.
target: In this clinical trial, adults aged 50 years and older who received the first dose of AstraZeneca or Pfizer vaccine got a second dose of the same vaccine or Moderna or Novavax 8-12 weeks after the first dose.

source: The primary endpoint was the geometric mean ratio (GMR) of serum SARS-CoV-2 anti-spike IgG concentrations measured by ELISA in heterologous versus homologous schedules at 28 days after the second dose, with a non-inferiority criterion of the GMR above 0·63 for the one-sided 98·75% CI.
target: 28 days after the second dose, chemicals the body produces to protect itself against COVID-19 were measured in the blood.

source: The primary analysis was on the per-protocol population, who were seronegative at baseline.
target: These people did not have these chemicals before the study.

source: Safety analyses were done for all participants who received a dose of study vaccine.
target: We also studied safety of the vaccines.

source: Findings: Between April 19 and May 14, 2021, 1072 participants were enrolled at a median of 9·4 weeks after receipt of a single dose of ChAd (n=540, 47% female) or BNT (n=532, 40% female).
target: 1072 people participated in the study 9.4 weeks after getting the first vaccine. 540 people got the first dose of AstraZeneca and 532 got Pfizer. There were somewhat more men than women in both groups.

source: In ChAd-primed participants, geometric mean concentration (GMC) 28 days after a boost of SARS-CoV-2 anti-spike IgG in recipients of ChAd/m1273 (20 114 ELISA laboratory units [ELU]/mL [95% CI 18 160 to 22 279]) and ChAd/NVX (5597 ELU/mL [4756 to 6586]) was non-inferior to that of ChAd/ChAd recipients (1971 ELU/mL [1718 to 2262]) with a GMR of 10·2 (one-sided 98·75% CI 8·4 to ∞) for ChAd/m1273 and 2·8 (2·2 to ∞) for ChAd/NVX, compared with ChAd/ChAd.
target: 28 days after the boosters, all people who had the first AstraZeneca vaccine had an increased number of chemicals against COVID-19 in their blood for all boosters.

source: In BNT-primed participants, non-inferiority was shown for BNT/m1273 (GMC 22 978 ELU/mL [95% CI 20 597 to 25 636]) but not for BNT/NVX (8874 ELU/mL [7391 to 10 654]), compared with BNT/BNT (16 929 ELU/mL [15 025 to 19 075]) with a GMR of 1·3 (one-sided 98·75% CI 1·1 to ∞) for BNT/m1273 and 0·5 (0·4 to ∞) for BNT/NVX, compared with BNT/BNT; however, NVX still induced an 18-fold rise in GMC 28 days after vaccination.
target: In people who had the Pfizer vaccine first, getting a Pfizer or Moderna booster increased the chemicals the most. Novavax also increased the amount of anti-COVID-19 chemicals, but not as much as the other boosters.

source: There were 15 serious adverse events, none considered related to immunisation.
target: 15 people had serious problems, but the problems were not caused by the vaccine.

source: Interpretation: Heterologous second dosing with m1273, but not NVX, increased transient systemic reactogenicity compared with homologous schedules.
target: Getting a Moderna dose after AstraZeneca or Pfizer first vaccines temporarily increased protection against COVID-19 more than getting the same second vaccine. Novavax did not work the same way.

source: Multiple vaccines are appropriate to complete primary immunisation following priming with BNT or ChAd, facilitating rapid vaccine deployment globally and supporting recognition of such schedules for vaccine certification.
target: Many vaccines can be used as the second dose after getting the first dose of AstraZeneca or Pfizer. This should help distributing vaccines around the world.

source: Rationale for review: Heterologous prime-boost doses of COVID-19 vaccines ('mix-and-match' approach) are being studied to test for the effectiveness of Oxford (AZD1222), Pfizer (BNT162b2), Moderna (mRNA-1273) and Novavax (NVX-CoV2373) vaccines for COVID in 'Com-Cov2 trial' in UK, and that of Oxford and Pfizer vaccines in 'CombivacS trial' in Spain.
target: ‘Mix-and-match’ boosters against COVID-19 are being studied in UK and Spain using Oxford, Pfizer, Moderna and Novavax vaccines.

source: Later, other heterologous combinatios of CoronaVac (DB15806), Janssen (JNJ-78436735), CanSino (AD5-nCOV), and other were also being trialed to explore their effectiveness.
target: Other combinations of CoronaVac, Janssen, and CanSino are also being tested.

source: Previously, such a strategy was deployed for HIV, Ebola virus, malaria, tuberculosis, influenza, and hepatitis B to develop the artificial acquired active immunity.
target: The ‘mix-and-match’ strategy has been used before to develop body protection against other diseases.

source: The present review explores the science behind such an approach for candidate COVID-19 vaccines developed using eleven different platforms approved by the World Health Organization.
target: We will try to understand why this approach might work for COVID-19.

source: Key findings: The candidate vaccines' pharmaceutical parameters (e.g. platforms, number needed to vaccinate and intervals, adjuvanted status, excipients and preservatives added, efficacy and effectiveness, vaccine adverse events, and boosters), and clinical aspects must be analysed for the mix-and-match approach.
target: To study the mix-and-match approach, we look at how the different vaccines work, the chemicals in the vaccines and their side effects.

source: Heterologous prime-boost trials showed safety, effectiveness, higher systemic reactogenicity, well tolerability with improved immunogenicity, and flexibility profiles for future vaccinations, especially during acute and global shortages, compared to the homologous counterparts.
target: The mix-and-match strategy is safe, improves body protection and allows to deal with vaccine shortages.

source: Background: The humoral immune response after primary immunisation with a SARS-CoV-2 vector vaccine (AstraZeneca AZD1222, ChAdOx1 nCoV-19, Vaxzevria) followed by an mRNA vaccine boost (Pfizer/BioNTech, BNT162b2; Moderna, m-1273) was examined and compared with the antibody response after homologous vaccination schemes (AZD1222/AZD1222 or BNT162b2/BNT162b2).
target: We studied how the immune system responds to Pfizer or Moderna booster after AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccination. We compared these cross-vaccine boosters to using the same boosters as the original vaccine: AstraZeneca or Pfizer.

source: Methods: Sera from 59 vaccinees were tested for anti-SARS-CoV-2 immunoglobulin G (IgG) and virus-neutralising antibodies (VNA) with three IgG assays based on (parts of) the SARS-CoV-2 spike (S)-protein as antigen, an IgG immunoblot (additionally contains the SARS-CoV-2 nucleoprotein (NP) as an antigen), a surrogate neutralisation test (sVNT), and a Vero-cell-based virus-neutralisation test (cVNT) with the B.1.1.7 variant of concern (VOC; alpha) as antigen.
target: We tested the blood of 59 vaccinated people for its ability to neutralize the SARS-CoV-2 virus  and for the  chemicals the body produced to fight the virus.

source: Investigation was done before and after heterologous (n = 30 and 42) or homologous booster vaccination (AZD1222/AZD1222, n = 8/9; BNT162b2/BNT162b2, n = 8/8).
target: The blood was tested before and after the booster vaccine was given.

source: Results: After the first vaccination, the prevalence of IgG directed against the (trimeric) SARS-CoV-2 S-protein and its receptor binding domain (RBD) varied from 55-95% (AZD1222) to 100% (BNT162b2), depending on the vaccine regimen and the SARS-CoV-2 antigen used.
target: After the first vaccine, chemicals against the parts of the virus that damage human cells ranged from 55-95% for AstraZeneca to 100% for Pfizer.

source: The booster vaccination resulted in 100% seroconversion and the occurrence of highly avid IgG, which is directed against the S-protein subunit 1 and the RBD, as well as VNA against VOC B.1.1.7, while anti-NP IgGs were not detected.
target: After the boosters, the blood developed highly effective chemicals that block the parts of the virus that damage human cells.

source: The anti-SARS-CoV-2 IgG concentrations and the B.1.1.7-neutralising titres were significantly higher after heterologous vaccination compared to the homologous AZD1222 scheme.
target: Vaccination with different boosters caused the blood to produce more anti-COVID-19 chemicals that boosting with the same AstraZeneca vaccine.

source: Conclusions: Heterologous SARS-CoV-2 vaccination leads to a strong antibody response with anti-SARS-CoV-2 IgG concentrations and VNA titres at a level comparable to that of a homologous BNT162b2 vaccination scheme.
target: Boosting with different vaccines strongly protects against COVID-19 at about the same level as boosting with the same original Pfizer vaccine.

source: Irrespective of the chosen immunisation regime, highly avid IgG antibodies can be detected just 2 weeks after the second vaccine dose indicating the development of a robust humoral immunity.
target: All vaccines caused the blood to develop strong protection 2 weeks after the second vaccine dose.

source: The reduction in the VNA titre against VOC B.1.617.2 observed in the subgroup of 26 individuals is remarkable and confirms the immune escape of the delta variant.
target: The protection against the delta variant of COVID-19 was not as strong in the 26 people in whom it was measured.

source: Background: Although the three vaccines against coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19) that have received emergency use authorization in the United States are highly effective, breakthrough infections are occurring.
target: The three vaccines against coronavirus disease 2019 (Covid-19) work well and fewer vaccinated people get sick. Breakthrough infections still sometimes happen, which means vaccinated people may get sick.

source: Data are needed on the serial use of homologous boosters (same as the primary vaccine) and heterologous boosters (different from the primary vaccine) in fully vaccinated recipients.
target: We need data on the use of the same booster and boosters that are different from the primary vaccine in fully vaccinated people.

source: Methods: In this phase 1-2, open-label clinical trial conducted at 10 sites in the United States, adults who had completed a Covid-19 vaccine regimen at least 12 weeks earlier and had no reported history of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection received a booster injection with one of three vaccines: mRNA-1273 (Moderna) at a dose of 100 μg, Ad26.COV2.S (Johnson & Johnson-Janssen) at a dose of 5×1010 virus particles, or BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) at a dose of 30 μg.
target: In this clinical trial, fully vaccinated adults with no reported history of COVID-19 infection got a booster injection at least 12 weeks after the primary vaccination. They got one of the three vaccines: Moderna, Johnson & Johnson-Janssen, or Pfizer.

source: The primary end points were safety, reactogenicity, and humoral immunogenicity on trial days 15 and 29.
target: On trial days 15 and 29 they were checked for booster safety, signs of the body’s response to the vaccine, and ability to build up protection against the COVID-19 virus.

source: Results: Of the 458 participants who were enrolled in the trial, 154 received mRNA-1273, 150 received Ad26.COV2.S, and 153 received BNT162b2 as booster vaccines; 1 participant did not receive the assigned vaccine.
target: 458 people participated in the trial. 154 got Moderna, 150 got Johnson & Johnson, and 153 got Pfizer.

source: Reactogenicity was similar to that reported for the primary series.
target: Signs of the body’s response to the booster were similar to the primary vaccine.

source: More than half the recipients reported having injection-site pain, malaise, headache, or myalgia.
target: More than half had injection-site pain, general discomfort, headache, or muscle aches.

source: For all combinations, antibody neutralizing titers against a SARS-CoV-2 D614 G pseudovirus increased by a factor of 4 to 73, and binding titers increased by a factor of 5 to 55.
target: All boosters increased the body's ability to protect itself against the SARS-CoV-2 virus from 4-fold to over 70-fold. The numbers of the antibodies that protect against COVID-19 increased from 5-fold to over 50-fold.

source: Homologous boosters increased neutralizing antibody titers by a factor of 4 to 20, whereas heterologous boosters increased titers by a factor of 6 to 73.
target: Using the same vaccine for the boosters increased the protection up to 20-fold, but using a different vaccine increased the protection more.

source: Spike-specific T-cell responses increased in all but the homologous Ad26.COV2.S-boosted subgroup.
target: All boosters, except for Johnson & Johnson, increased the body's ability to protect itself agains the spike that the virus uses to get into the body cells.

source: CD8+ T-cell levels were more durable in the Ad26.COV2.S-primed recipients, and heterologous boosting with the Ad26.COV2.S vaccine substantially increased spike-specific CD8+ T cells in the mRNA vaccine recipients.
target: The Johnson & Johnson booster increased the number of cells that block the virus spike in people who were vaccinated with Moderna or Pfizer.

source: Conclusions: Homologous and heterologous booster vaccines had an acceptable safety profile and were immunogenic in adults who had completed a primary Covid-19 vaccine regimen at least 12 weeks earlier.
target: All boosters were safe and increased protection against Covid-19 in adults who were fully vaccinated at least 12 weeks earlier.

source: Introduction: The vital renal replacement therapy makes it impossible for dialysis patients to distance themselves socially.
target: Dialysis patients cannot distance themselves socially.

source: This results in a high risk of SARS-CoV-2 infection and developing COVID-19 with excess mortality due to disease burden and immunosuppression.
target: They are at high risk of developing COVID-19 with high mortality.

source: We determined the efficacy of a 100 µg booster of mRNA-1273 (Moderna, Inc., Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA) 6 months after two doses of BNT162b2 (BioNTech/Pfizer, Mainz, Germany/New York, NY, USA) in 194 SARS-CoV-2 naïve dialysis patients.
target: We studied Moderna boosters 6 months after two doses of Pfizer in 194 dialysis patients who did not have SARS-CoV-2 infection.

source: Methods: Anti-SARS-CoV-2-spike antibodies were measured with the Elecsys® Anti-SARS-CoV-2 S assay (Roche Diagnostics GmbH, Germany) 4 and 10-12 weeks after two doses of BNT162b2 as well as 4 weeks after the mRNA-1273 booster.
target: We measured the chemicals that protect against the COVID-19 virus 4 weeks after Pfizer and Moderna boosters, and also 10-12 weeks after Pfizer

source: Results: Any level of immunoreactivity determined by anti-SARS-CoV-2-spike antibody assay was found in 87.3% (n = 144/165) and 90.6% (n = 164/181) 4 and 10-12 weeks after two doses of BNT162b2.
target: Protective chemicals were found in about 9 out of 10 people 4 and 10-12 weeks after two doses of Pfizer.

source: This was reduced to 68.5% or 60.6% 4 weeks and 51.7% or 35.4% 10-12 weeks, respectively, when using the ROC revealed cut-offs for neutralizing antibodies in the surrogate neutralization test (manufacturer given cut-off ≥ 103 U/ml and cut-off correlating with PRNT ≥ 196 U/ml).
target: Looking at different protective thresholds, protection was reduced to 7 out of 10 after 4 weeks and around half after 10-12 weeks.

source: Four weeks after the mRNA-1273 booster, the concentration of anti-SARS-CoV-2-spike antibodies increased to 23 119.9 U/ml and consecutively to 97.3% for both cut-offs of neutralizing antibodies.
target: For Moderna, almost all people had strong protection after 4 weeks.

source: Conclusion: Two doses of BNT162b2 followed by one dose of mRNA-1273 within 6 months in patients receiving maintenance dialysis resulted in significant titers of SARS-CoV-2-S-Ab.
target: People on dialysis who were vaccinated with Pfizer and got Moderna booster within the next 6 month had significant protection against the COVID-19 virus.

source: While two doses of mRNA vaccine only achieved adequate humoral immunity in a minority, the third vaccination boosts the development of virus-neutralizing quantities of SARS-CoV-2 spike antibodies (against wild type SARS-CoV-2) in almost all patients.
target: While the first two doses of Moderna or Pfizer vaccine gave adequate protection to some patients, the booster increased protection against the COVID-19 virus in almost all patients.

source: Background: Heterologous vaccinations against SARS-CoV-2 with ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 and a second dose of an mRNA-based vaccine have been shown to be more immunogenic than homologous ChAdOx1 nCoV-19.
target: Getting a Pfizer or Moderna booster after the AstraZeneca vaccine protects against COVID-19 better than getting the same AstraZeneca booster.

source: In the current study, we examined the kinetics of the antibody response to the second dose of three different vaccination regimens (homologous ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 vs. ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 + BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273) against SARS-CoV-2 in a longitudinal manner; whether there are differences in latency or amplitude of the early response and which markers are most suitable to detect these responses.
target: We studied how a booster with the same AstraZeneca or a different Pfizer or Moderna vaccine protects against COVID-19 over time.

source: Methods: We performed assays for anti-S1 IgG and IgA, anti-NCP IgG and a surrogate neutralization assay on serum samples collected from 57 participants on the day of the second vaccination as well as the following seven days.
target: We analyzed chemicals that protect against COVID-19 in the blood of 57 people on the day of the second vaccination and the following seven days.

source: Results: All examined vaccination regimens induced detectable antibody responses within the examined time frame.
target: All boosters increased the protective chemicals in the blood.

source: Both heterologous regimens induced responses earlier and with a higher amplitude than homologous ChAdOx1 nCoV-19.
target: Different boosters had earlier and stronger protection than boosting with the same AstraZeneca vaccine.

source: Between the heterologous regimens, amplitudes were somewhat higher for ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 + mRNA-1273.
target: People who had Moderna booster after the first AstraZeneca vaccine had more protective chemicals than those who got Pfizer.

source: There was no difference in latency between the IgG and IgA responses.
target: The body produced different protective chemicals at the same time.

source: Discussion: Both examined heterologous vaccination regimens are superior in immunogenicity, including the latency of the response, to homologous ChAdOx1 nCoV-19.
target: The different boosters were more protective than the same AstraZeneca booster.

source: In Spain, 1.5 million essential < 60-year-old workers were vaccinated with a first AstraZeneca vaccine dose.
target: In Spain, 1.5 million essential workers under 60 years of age got a first AstraZeneca vaccine dose.

source: After assessing the cases of thrombosis with thrombocytopenia associated to this vaccine, the European Medicines Agency (EMA) supported the administration of 2 doses of the AstraZeneca vaccine with no age restrictions.
target: Although this vaccine was associated with blood clots and reduction in blood cells, the European Medicines Agency recommended two doses of AstraZeneca for all ages.

source: Nevertheless, Spain decided not to administer the second dose of this vaccine to < 60-year-olds.
target: Spain decided not to give the second dose of this vaccine to people under 60 years old.

source: The government sponsored a clinical trial (CombiVacS) to assess the immunogenicity response to a Pfizer/BioNTech vaccine dose in adults primed with the AstraZeneca vaccine.
target: The government sponsored a clinical trial to evaluate if a dose of Pfizer vaccine works in adults who first got the AstraZeneca vaccine.

source: The positive results backed the Public Health Commission and the Spanish Ministry of Health to offer the Pfizer/BioNTech vaccine as the booster.
target: The positive results supported giving the Pfizer vaccine as the booster.

source: Nevertheless, regional public health authorities-responsible for administering vaccines-believed that, following the EMA's decision, an AstraZeneca booster dose should be given.
target: Some local authorities decided to follow the recommendation that an AstraZeneca booster should be given.

source: The public confrontation of these 2 positions forced the Spanish Health Ministry to request the signature of an informed consent form to those individuals willing to receive the AstraZeneca vaccine booster and rejecting the Pfizer/BioNTech vaccine dose.
target: Because of these contradictions, the Spanish Health Ministry requested those who decide to get the AstraZeneca booster and decline Pfizer should sign an informed consent form.

source: Eventually, it was decided that these essential workers could choose the vaccine but signing an informed consent form.
target: Later these essential workers could choose the vaccine but still sign an informed consent form.

source: All relevant information was posted on the Ministry of Health and regional health authorities' websites and provided to potential vaccine recipients at vaccination sites.
target: Relevant information was posted on the web and given to people at vaccination sites.

source: Most individuals (≥ 75%) chose the AstraZeneca vaccine: perhaps because they likely trusted the EMA more than the CombiVacS results.
target: Three quarters of people getting the vaccine chose AstraZeneca; maybe because they trusted the European Medicines Agency more.

source: Exercising individual autonomy may contribute to the success of future COVID-19 booster vaccination campaigns.
target: Giving people a choice may make future COVID-19 boosters more successful.

source: Reports of waning antibody levels and breakthrough infections among vaccinated individuals1 have prompted the recommendation for vaccine boosters to prevent SARS-CoV-2 infections.
target: Boosters to prevent SARS-CoV-2 infections in those who had the first vaccines are needed because the amount of body substances that protect against the virus goes down and people get COVID-19, which is called breakthrough infections.

source: Despite more than 80% of the population in Singapore having received 2 doses of a COVID-19 vaccine, cases surged in September 2021 with the relaxation of social distancing and quarantine measures.
target: Although 80 out of 100 people in Singapore had 2 doses of a COVID-19 vaccine, COVID-19 surged in September 2021 as the social distancing and quarantine rules were relaxed.

source: In response, adults 60 years and older who completed their primary vaccination series at least 6 months prior were invited to receive a booster injection and given a choice of either 30-μg BNT162b2 (Pfizer-BioNTech) or 50-μg mRNA-1273 (Moderna).
target: Adults 60 years and older who got the first vaccine at least 6 months before were invited to get a booster of Pfizer or Moderna.

source: We estimated SARS-CoV-2 infections and disease severity with the receipt of a booster and by type of booster.
target: We estimated SARS-CoV-2 infections and disease severity after getting a booster and by type of booster.

source: Rates and severity of SARS-CoV-2 infections between September 15 and October 31, 2021, among those eligible to receive vaccine boosters between September 15 and October 15, 2021, were analyzed based on official data reported to the Singapore Ministry of Health.
target: Rates and severity of SARS-CoV-2 infections between September 15 and October 31, 2021, among those eligible to receive vaccine boosters were studied.

source: Cases were identified through testing of symptomatic individuals and nonsymptomatic high-risk workers and close contacts.
target: COVID-19 patients were found through testing of those with symptoms and high-risk workers and close contacts without symptoms.

source: Outcomes included polymerase chain reaction–confirmed infections and severe disease (requiring oxygen supplementation, intensive care admission, or death due to COVID-19).
target: We measured infections confirmed by tests and severe disease (requiring oxygen support, intensive care admission, or death due to COVID-19).

source: Individuals were classified under the booster group 12 days after receiving a vaccine booster and under the nonbooster group otherwise to account for time required for antibody levels to rise.
target: People who got the boosters 12 days before were included in the booster group, and non-booster group otherwise.

source: Person-days at risk were reported because individuals could contribute observations to both the nonbooster and booster groups.
target: Depending on the day of observation, a person could be in either group.

source: Using a Poisson regression, we estimated the incidence rate ratio (IRR) of confirmed infections and severe disease between booster and nonbooster groups by type of vaccine received for the primary series (BNT162b2 or mRNA-1273).
target: We estimated getting infected and severe disease between booster and non-booster groups that got the first vaccination with Pfizer or Moderna.

source: Covariates included sex, race (4 official racial categories reported in Singapore are Chinese, Malay, Indian, and others and registered at birth according to the child’s parents’ race), housing type as a marker of socioeconomic status, age group, date of second vaccine dose to account for possible waning of immunity, and individual dummy variables for calendar date to adjust for the varying force of infection over the study period (eMethods in the Supplement).
target: We accounted for the patients' sex, race, housing type, age, and the date of second vaccine that may be related to the strength of protection.

source: We obtained IRRs for individuals receiving the same vaccine as a booster (homologous boosted) and those receiving a different vaccine (heterologous boosted).
target: We observed the rate of infection in those who got the same booster as the first vaccine and a different vaccine as booster.

source: Results: Among 703 209 eligible individuals during the study period, 576 132 received boosters.
target: Out of 703,209 people who were allowed to have a booster during the study, 576,132 got boosters.

source: By person-days, 59% were 60 to 69 years, 29% were 70 to 79 years, and 11% were aged 80 years and older, with 53% being female.
target: More than half of the people were 60 to 69 years old, a third 70 to 79 years, and a tenth of the people were 0 years and older. Slightly more than half of the people were women.

source: Among individuals who received BNT162b2 for their primary series, the incidences (per million person-days) of confirmed and severe infections were 227.9 and 1.4 for the homologous boosted compared with 600.4 and 20.5 for the nonboosted.
target: Among those for whom the first vaccine and the booster was Pfizer, about 230 people got COVID-19 and about 2 had severe disease. Among those who had the first Pfizer vaccine but no booster, 600 got COVID-19 and 20 had severe disease.

source: For the heterologous-boosted individuals, the incidences of confirmed and severe infections were 147.9 and 2.3 cases per million person-days, respectively, with IRRs of 0.177 (95% CI, 0.138-0.227) and 0.078 (95% CI, 0.011-0.560).
target: Among those who had a different booster, about 150 people got COVID-19 and about 2 had severe disease.

source: For individuals who received mRNA-1273 for their primary series, the incidence of confirmed infections for the homologous boosted was 133.9 cases per million person-years (IRR, 0.198 [95% CI, 0.144-0.271]).
target: Among those who had Moderna as their first vaccine and booster, about 134  got COVID-19.

source: For heterologous-boosted individuals, the incidence of confirmed infections was 100.6 per million person-days (IRR, 0.140 [95% CI, 0.052-0.376]).
target: Among those who first had Moderna and then a Pfizer booster, about 100 had COVID-19.

source: The number of severe infections among individuals receiving mRNA-1273 for their primary series was too small to assess IRRs.
target: The number of severe infections among those who had Moderna as the first vaccine was too small to count.

source: Discussion: Heterologous boosting was associated with lower SARS-CoV-2 incidence rates than homologous boosting.
target: People who got different boosters had less COVID-19 infections than those boosted with the same vaccine as the first.

source: Severe infections were lower among those receiving a booster after BNT162b2 as the primary series compared with the nonboosted individuals, regardless of the type of booster.
target: People who first had Pfizer and then any booster had less severe disease than those who did not have boosters.

source: Limitations of the study include potential confounding from unobservable individual characteristics that may influence individuals’ choice of booster, a short follow-up period, small numbers of infections after mRNA-1273 administration, and lack of data from younger age groups.
target: This study has limitations, such as not knowing why people decided for or against boosters, short time between the booster and the study, and no data from younger age groups.

source: The study results support recommendations for vaccine boosters and suggest that heterologous boosting may provide greater protection against COVID-19.
target: The study supports having vaccine boosters. Boosting with different vaccines may provide greater protection against COVID-19.

source: We experienced a 72-year-old man who developed laboratory-confirmed human coronavirus HKU1 pneumonia.
target: A 72-year-old man has a confirmed human coronavirus HKU1 pneumonia.

source: PCR testing for SARS-CoV-2 from a nasopharyngeal specimen was negative twice, and rapid immunochromatographic antibody test (RIAT) using a commercially available kit for IgM and IgG against SARS-CoV-2 showed him turning positive for IgG against SARS-CoV-2.
target: His nasal swab test for COVID-19 virus was negative twice, but his antibody (chemicals the body produces to fight the virus) test was positive.

source: We then performed RIAT in stored serum samples from other patients who suffered laboratory-confirmed human common cold coronaviruses (n = 6) and viruses other than coronavirus (influenza virus, n = 3; rhinovirus, n = 3; metapneumovirus, n = 1; adenovirus, n = 1) admitted until January 2019.
target: We then did the antibody test on the stored blood of other patients who had confirmed viral infections, such as flu and common cold, before January 2019.

source: Including the present case, four of 7 (57%) showed false-positive RIAT results due to human common cold coronaviruses infection.
target: Including the present case, four of 7 antibody test results were falsely positive for COVID-19.

source: Because of high incidence of false positive RIAT results, cross antigenicity between human common cold coronaviruses and SARS-CoV-2 can be considered.
target: The high rate of the falsely positive antibody test results might be due to similarities between the human common cold coronaviruses and the SARS-CoV-2 virus that causes COVID-19.

source: Results of RIAT should be interpreted in light of epidemics of human common cold coronaviruses infection.
target: The results of the antibody tests may depend on epidemics of human common cold coronavirus infection.

source: Prevalence of past SARS-CoV-2 infection may be overestimated due to high incidence of false-positive RIAT results.
target: The spread of the past SARS-CoV-2 infections may be overestimated because of the falsely positive antibody test results.

source: Early detection of affected patients within the large and heterogeneous group of patients with common cold symptoms is an important element of this effort, but often hindered by limited testing resources, false-negative test results and the lack of pathognomonic symptoms in COVID-19.
target: It is hard to detect COVID-19 patients in a group of patients with common cold symptoms because there are no COVID-specific symptoms, there are not enough test supplies, and test results can be falsely negative in patients with COVID-19.

source: Therefore, we aimed to identify anamnestic items with an increased/decreased odds ratio for a positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR (CovPCR) result in a primary care setting.
target: We aim to find some strong signs in the COVID test results that show SARS-CoV-2 infection.

source: Results: In total, 374 patients in 14 primary care centers received CovPCR and were included in this analysis.
target: We studied 374 patients who got rapid COVID tests.

source: The median age was 44.0 (IQR: 31.0-59.0) and a fraction of 10.7% (n = 40) tested positive for COVID-19.
target: Patients were 31 to 59 years old, and one tenth of them had positive COVID-19 test results.

source: Patients who reported anosmia had a higher odds ratio (OR: 4.54; 95%-CI: 1.51-13.67) for a positive test result while patients with a sore throat had a lower OR (OR: 0.33; 95%-CI: 0.11-0.97).
target: Patients who lost their sense pf smell had a higher chance to test positive for COVID-19. Patients with a sore throat had a lower chance to test positive for COVID-19.

source: Furthermore, patients who had a first grade contact with an infected persons and showed symptoms themselves also had an increased OR for positive testing (OR: 5.16; 95% CI: 1.72-15.51).
target: Patients who had contact with an infected persons and had symptoms themselves also had an increased chance to test positive.

source: This correlation was also present when they themselves were still asymptomatic (OR: 12.55; 95% CI: 3.97-39.67).
target: Even if they did not have symptoms, these patients had higher chances to test positive.

source: Antigen-detecting rapid diagnostic tests (Ag-RDTs) can complement molecular diagnostics for COVID-19.
target: Rapid diagnostic tests can support other COVID-q9 tests.

source: The recommended temperature for storage of SARS-CoV-2 Ag-RDTs ranges between 2-30 °C.
target: Rapid covid tests could be refrigerated or stored at room temperature under  30 degrees C (86 Fahrenheit)

source: We assessed analytical sensitivity and specificity of eleven commercially-available SARS-CoV-2 Ag-RDTs using different storage and operational temperatures, including short- or long-term storage and operation at recommended temperatures or at either 2-4 °C or at 37 °C.
target: We tested the accuracy of eleven rapid COVID tests that were stored and used at different temperatures. The tests were stored for a short or long time at recommended temperatures or at either 2-4 degrees C (35-39 Fahrenheit) or at 37 degrees C (98-99 Fahrenheit).

source: Despite long-term storage at recommended conditions, 10 min pre-incubation of Ag-RDTs and testing at 37 °C resulted in about ten-fold reduced sensitivity for five out of 11 SARS-CoV-2 Ag-RDTs, including both Ag-RDTs currently listed for emergency use by the World Health Organization.
target: The tests were stored for a long-term at recommended temperatures and then at the high temperature (37C) for 10 minutes. For five out of 11 rapid tests, the accuracy was reduced ten-fold.

source: After 3 weeks of storage at 37 °C, eight of the 11 SARS-CoV-2 Ag-RDTs exhibited about ten-fold reduced sensitivity.
target: After 3 weeks of storage at high temperature (37 C), eight of the 11 rapid tests were  ten-fold less accurate.

source: Specificity of SARS-CoV-2 Ag-RDTs using cell culture supernatant from common respiratory viruses was not affected by storage and testing at 37 °C, whereas false-positive results occurred at outside temperatures of 2-4 °C for two out of six tested Ag-RDTs, again including an Ag-RDT recommended by the WHO.
target: Two out of six rapid tests had false positive results at low temperatures (2-4 degrees C).

source: In summary, elevated temperatures impair sensitivity, whereas low temperatures impair specificity of SARS-CoV-2 Ag-RDTs.
target: In summary, high temperatures reduced how well the test identified people who had COVID-19 and low temperatures caused false positive results, showing people who did not have COVID-19 as having it.

source: Consequences may include false-negative test results at clinically relevant virus concentrations compatible with transmission and false-positive results entailing unwarranted quarantine assignments.
target: The false negative test results allow people who have the virus transmit it to others. Due to the false-positive results healthy people will be quarantined.

source: Storage and operation of SARS-CoV-2 Ag-RDTs at recommended conditions is essential for successful usage during the pandemic.
target: Storing the rapid tests at recommended temperatures is important during the pandemic.

source: Prompt and accurate detection of SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19, has been important during public health responses for containing the spread of COVID-19, including in hospital settings (1-3).
target: Fast and accurate COVID-19 tests help reduce the spread of the disease, including in hospitals.

source: In vitro diagnostic nucleic acid amplification tests (NAAT), such as real-time reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) can be expensive, have relatively long turnaround times, and require experienced laboratory personnel.*
target: Some of the known tests are expensive, take a long time and need training to do the test.

source: Antigen detection tests can be rapidly and more easily performed and are less expensive.
target: Other tests are fast and easy to do without training.

source: The performance† of antigen detection tests, compared with that of NAATs, is an area of interest for the rapid diagnosis of SARS-CoV-2 infection.
target: Accuracy of the easier and faster tests compared to the traditional tests is an area of interest.

source: The Quidel Sofia 2 SARS Antigen Fluorescent Immunoassay (FIA) (Quidel Corporation) received Food and Drug Administration Emergency Use Authorization for use in symptomatic patients within 5 days of symptom onset (4).
target: The Quidel rapid test was approved for emergency use in patients within the first 5 days of COVID-19 symptoms.

source: The reported test positive percentage agreement§ between this test and an RT-PCR test result is 96.7% (95% confidence interval [CI] = 83.3%-99.4%), and the negative percentage agreement is 100.0% (95% CI = 97.9%-100.0%) in symptomatic patients.¶
target: These tests agreed with the traditional test for 83 to 99 out of 100 patients with positive results and 97 to all 100 patients with negative results.

source: However, performance in asymptomatic persons in a university setting has shown lower sensitivity (5); assessment of performance in a clinical setting is ongoing.
target: For the COVID-19 patients that did not have symptoms, the tests were not as accurate as the traditional test.

source: Data collected during June 30-August 31, 2020, were analyzed to compare antigen test performance with that of RT-PCR in a hospital setting.
target: We compared test results at a hospital in June - August 2020.

source: Among 1,732 paired samples from asymptomatic patients, the antigen test sensitivity was 60.5%, and specificity was 99.5% when compared with RT-PCR.
