source: Conclusion: After recent primary total knee replacement, interventions including physiotherapy and exercise show short-term improvements in physical function.
target: In conclusion, after recently having knee replacement, care including PT and exercise improve physical function short-term.

source: However this conclusion is based on meta-analysis of a few small studies and no long-term benefits of physiotherapy exercise interventions were identified.
target: However, this conclusion is based on a few small studies, and we found no long-term benefits of PT exercise.

source: Future research should target improvements to long-term function, pain and performance outcomes in appropriately powered trials.
target: Future research should focus on improving long-term function, pain and performance with larger studies.

source: Purpose: The aim of the present study was to evaluate the efficacy and safety of non-supervised home-based exercise versus individualized and supervised programs delivered in clinic-based settings for the functional recovery immediately after discharge from a primary TKA.
target: The aim of this study was to see how effective and safe different functional recovery strategies were immediately after discharge from knee replacement surgery. It compared non-supervised, home-based exercise to individualized and supervised programs delivered in clinic-based settings.

source: Methods: Medline, Embase, Cochrane, and PEDro databases were screened, from inception to April 2015, in search for randomized clinical trials (RCT) of home-based exercise interventions versus individualized and supervised outpatient physical therapy after primary TKA.
target: We searched several medical literature databases for papers published up until April 2015. We were looking for clinical trials of home-based exercise programs versus individualized and supervised outpatient physical therapy (PT) after knee replacement.

source: Target outcomes were: knee range of motion (ROM), patient-reported pain and function, functional performance, and safety.
target: The outcomes we compared were knee range of motion (ROM), patient-reported pain and function, functional performance, and safety.

source: Results: The search and selection process identified 11 RCT of moderate quality and small sample sizes.
target: Our search and selection process found 11 clinical trials of decent quality but that looked at small numbers of patients.

source: ROM active extension data suitable for meta-analysis was available from seven studies with 707 patients, and ROM active flexion from nine studies with 983 patients.
target: Seven studies, with a total of 707 patients, had data on ROM for extending the leg that could be used. Nine studies, with a total of 983 patients, had data on ROM for bending the leg.

source: Most studies showed no difference between groups.
target: Most studies showed no difference between groups.

source: Pooled differences were within the non-inferiority margin.
target: Even taken altogether, differences between the treatment types were small enough to be by chance.

source: Conclusion: Short-term improvements in physical function and knee ROM do not clearly differ between outpatient physiotherapy and home-based exercise regimes in patients after primary TKA; however, this conclusion is based on a meta-analysis with high heterogeneity.
target: In conclusion, short-term improvements in physical function and knee ROM are not clearly different between outpatient PT and home-based exercise regimes in patients after knee replacement. However, this conclusion is based on combining studies with many differences.

source: Background: Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) alleviates pain, but muscle strength and function is reduced for a long period postoperatively.
target: Complete knee replacement surgery alleviates pain, but muscle strength and function is reduced for a long period after the operation.

source: Aim: To investigate whether maximal strength training (MST) is more effective in improving muscle strength than standard rehabilitation (SR) after TKA.
target: Our aim was to investigate whether Maximal Strength Training (MST) is more effective in improving muscle strength than standard rehab knee replacement.

source: Design: A randomized, controlled study.
target: This study involved two groups who randomly got either the standard care or the experimental care.

source: Setting: Community physical therapy centers and University hospital research department.
target: It was done in community physical therapy centers and a university hospital research department.

source: Population: Forty-one adults <75 years with primary, unilateral osteoarthritis of the knee scheduled for TKA.
target: The study included 41 adults under 75 with arthritis in one knee and who were scheduled for knee replacement surgery.

source: Methods: Participants were randomized to supervised MST of the lower extremities 3 times/week for 8 weeks and physiotherapy session1/week (N.=21) or to SR, including physiotherapy sessions/telephone contact 1/week and writing home exercise logs (N.=20).
target: Participants randomly got either supervised MST for the legs 3 times a week for 8 weeks and one physiotherapy session per week or the standard rehab, including physiotherapy sessions and/or telephone contact once a week and writing home exercise logs. The MST group had 21 patients, while the standard rehab group had 20 patients.

source: Maximal strength in leg press and knee extension, 6-minute walk test, patient-reported functional outcome score and pain were assessed preoperatively, 7 days, 10 weeks and 12 months postoperatively.
target: We measured maximum strength in leg press and knee extension, 6-minute walk test, patient-reported functional outcome score and pain. We looked at these 7 days after the operation, 10 weeks after, and 12 months after.

source: Results: The MST group exceeded preoperative levels of muscle strength in leg press and knee extension by 37% and 43%, respectively at 10 weeks' follow-up, and the increase was higher than in the SR group (P≤0.001).
target: The MST group had 37% more strength in leg press and 43% more strength in knee extension at 10 weeks. These increases were higher than for the group that had standard rehab.

source: Strength differences persisted up to 12-months follow-up.
target: Strength differences continued up to the 12-month follow-ups.

source: At 12 months, both groups recovered to normative levels in the 6-Minute Walk Test, with no statistically significantly difference between the groups.
target: At 12 months, both groups recovered to normal levels in the 6-Minute Walk Test, with no significant difference between the groups.

source: Conclusions: Participants undergoing MST experienced superior increases in leg press and knee extension muscle strength compared with those managed with SR from 7-day to 10-week follow-up.
target: In conclusion, participants getting Maximal Strength Training (MST) had better increases in leg press and knee extension muscle strength compared to those who had standard rehab, as measured at 7-day to 10-week follow-ups after surgery.

source: The difference in muscle strength was maintained at 12-month follow-up.
target: The difference in muscle strength was maintained at 12-month follow-up.

source: No differences in functional performance were found at any time-point.
target: No differences in functional performance were found at any point.

source: Clinical rehabilitation impact: Exercises after TKA should be performed with high intensity and target the operated leg specifically.
target: The impact of this on clinical rehab is that exercises after knee replacement should be done with high intensity and should target the operated leg specifically.

source: This study evaluated the use of telerehabilitation during the postoperative period for patients who underwent total knee arthroplasty (TKA) or unicompartmental knee arthroplasty (UKA).
target: This study looked at using tele-rehab (where providers interact with patients remotely using the internet) after complete or partial knee replacement surgery.

source: Specifically, this study evaluated the following: (1) patient compliance and adherence to the program, (2) time spent performing physical therapy exercises, (3) the usability of the virtual rehabilitation platform, and (4) clinical outcome scores in a selected primary knee arthroplasty cohort.
target: Specifically, the study looked at: (1) how well patients kept up with the program, (2) time spent performing physical therapy exercises, (3) how easy it was to use the virtual rehab software, and (4) outcomes for a certain group of patients that had knee replacement surgery.

source: A total of 157 consecutive patients underwent TKA (n = 18) or UKA (n = 139).
target: A total of 157 patients in a row had complete (18) or partial (139) knee replacement.

source: These patients used a telerehabilitation system with an instructional avatar, three-dimensional motion measurement and analysis software, and real-time televisit capability designed for arthroplasty patients.
target: These patients used a tele-rehab system with an instructional avatar, three-dimensional motion measurement and analysis software, and real-time tele-visit capability designed for joint surgery patients.

source: Compliance was determined by how many times the patients followed prescribed repetitions of exercises.
target: We measured how well patients kept up with the program by how many times the patients followed the recommended repetitions of exercises.

source: The total time spent performing exercises for each patient was collected.
target: We collected the total each patient time spent performing exercises.

source: The usability of the virtual rehabilitation platform (on the patient's end) was evaluated using the system usability scale (SUS) questionnaire.
target: We evaluated how easy it was for patients to use the virtual rehab software using a questionnaire.

source: The number of in-person and televisits was recorded for each patient.
target: We also recorded the number of in-person and virtual visits for each patient.

source: Patient-reported outcomes were collected through the patient and clinician interfaces and included the Knee Society Score (KSS) for pain and functions, the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) score, and Boston University Activity Measure for Post-Acute Care (AM-PAC) score.
target: We collected patient-reported outcomes through patient/doctor portals. Measurements included were the Knee Society Score (KSS) for pain and functions, the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) score, and Boston University Activity Measure for Post-Acute Care (AM-PAC) score.

source: Patients spent an average of 29.5 days partaking in the therapy.
target: Patients spent an average of about 30 days participating in the therapy.

source: TKA and UKA patients had a mean of 3.5 and 3.2 outpatient follow-up visits, each, for in-office therapy with a physical therapist, respectively.
target: Complete knee replacement patients, on average, had 3.5 outpatient follow-up visits each for in-office therapy with a physical therapist. Partial knee replacement patients had, on average, 3.2 of these visits.

source: This figure exceeded the mean number of real-time virtual patient-clinician visits by 0.8 visits per patient in the TKA cohort and by 1 visit per patient in the UKA cohort.
target: For complete knee replacement patients, this was on average 0.8 more visits than for real-time virtual patient-clinician. For partial hip replacement patients, it was 1 more visit.

source: Patients spent on average 26.5 minutes per day conducting an average of 13.5 exercises.
target: Patients spent, on average, 26.5 minutes per day doing an average of 13.5 exercises.

source: By the end of rehabilitation, patients had spent an average of 10.8 hours performing exercises, and of all the exercises performed, approximately 21 exercises were uniquely designed.
target: By the end of rehab, patients had spent an average of 10.8 hours doing exercises, and of all the exercises performed, around 21 were uniquely designed.

source: Following therapy, KSS pain and function scores improved markedly and the improvements were measured at 368% for TKA and 350% for UKA (pain) and 27% for UKA and 33% for TKA (function).
target: After therapy, KSS pain and function scores improved notably. The scores for pain improved by 368% for complete knee replacement and 350% for partial. The scores for function improved by 33% for complete knee replacement 27% for partial.

source: In addition, WOMAC scores improved by 57% and 66% for UKA and TKA patients while the improvement in AM-PAC scores was at 22% and 24%.
target: Additionally, WOMAC scores improved by 57% for partial knee replacement and 66% for compete knee replacement. The improvement in AM-PAC scores was at 22% for partial and 24% for complete.

source: This telerehabilitation platform encouraged clinician-patient interaction beyond the hospital setting and offers the advantage of cost savings, convenience, at-home monitoring, and coordination of care, all of which are geared to improve adherence and overall patient satisfaction.
target: This tele-rehab platform encouraged interaction between doctors and patients beyond the hospital setting. It offers the advantage of cost savings, convenience, at-home monitoring, and coordination of care. All of these are geared to improve how well patients keep up and overall patient satisfaction.

source: Additionally, the biometric data can be used to develop custom physical therapy regimens to assure proper rehabilitation, which is lacking in other telerehabilitation applications that use noninteractive videos that can be watched on mobile devices and tablets.
target: Additionally, the data from the platform can be used to make custom physical therapy regimens to assure proper rehabilitation. This is lacking in other tele-rehab applications that use non-interactive videos that can be watched on mobile devices and tablets.

source: Objective: Although total knee replacement (TKR) surgery is highly prevalent and generally successful, functional outcomes post-TKR vary widely.
target: Although knee replacement surgery is very common and usually turns out well, amount of function after varies widely.

source: Most patients receive some physical therapy (PT) following TKR, but PT practice is variable and associations between specific content and dose of PT interventions and functional outcomes are unknown.
target: Most patients have some physical therapy (PT) after knee replacement, but PT practice varies. We don’t know which amounts and kinds of PT are associated with which outcomes.

source: Research has identified exercise interventions associated with better outcomes but studies have not assessed whether such evidence has been translated into clinical practice.
target: Research has shown that exercise is associated with better outcomes, but studies have not looked at whether this has made its way into clinical practice.

source: We characterized the content, dose, and progression of usual post-acute PT services following TKR, and examined associations of specific details of post-acute PT with patients' 6-month functional outcomes.
target: We looked at the content, amount, and progression of usual PT services after knee replacement. We also examined associations of specific details of PT after surgery with how much function patients had after 6 months.

source: Methods: Post-acute PT data were collected from patients who were undergoing primary unilateral TKR and participating in a clinical trial of a phone-based coaching intervention.
target: We collected PT data from patients who were having knee replacement surgery on one side and participating in a clinical trial of a phone-based coaching program.

source: PT records from the terminal episode of care were reviewed and utilization and exercise content data were extracted.
target: We reviewed PT records from final sessions to find how much patients used the PT and what exercises were done.

source: Descriptive statistics and linear regression models characterized PT treatment factors and identified associations with 6-month outcomes.
target: We used statistical methods to see which parts of PT treatment were associated with 6-month outcomes.

source: Results: We analyzed 112 records from 30 PT sites.
target: In all, we looked at 112 records from 30 PT sites.

source: Content and dose of specific exercises and incidence of progression varied widely.
target: The types and amounts of exercises, and how they progressed, varied widely.

source: Open chain exercises were utilized more frequently than closed chain (median 21 [interquartile range (IQR) 4-49] versus median 13 [IQR 4-28.5]).
target: Exercises that used weights or other resistance were use more than body-weight exercises (on average 21 versus 13).

source: Median (IQR) occurrence of progression of closed and open chain exercise was 0 (0-2) and 1 (0-3), respectively.
target: Progression of exercises was used on average 0 times for exercises with resistance and 1 time for exercises that just used body weight.

source: Shorter timed stair climb was associated with greater total number of PT interventions and use and progression of closed chain exercises.
target: Climbing stairs faster was associated with more PT sessions and the use and progression of body-weight exercises.

source: Discussion: Data suggest that evidence-based interventions are underutilized and dose may be insufficient to obtain optimal outcomes.
target: The evidence suggests that treatments backed by studies are not used as much as they should be and the amount of PT may not be enough to create the best possible outcomes.

source: Background: Early ambulation with physical therapy (PT) following total knee arthroplasty (TKA) has demonstrated benefits in the literature.
target: Research has shown that having patients walk early with physical therapy (PT) after knee replacement has benefits.

source: However, the impact of early PT on rehabilitation performance and opioid consumption has not been elucidated.
target: However, the impact of early PT on rehab performance and opioid use is still unclear.

source: We evaluate the effect of same-day PT on inhospital functional outcomes and opioid consumption.
target: We looked at the effect of same-day PT on how well patients functions and opioid use while in the hospital.

source: Methods: We retrospectively identified 2 cohorts of primary TKA patients from July 2016 to December 2017: PT0 (n = 295) received PT on the day of surgery, and PT1 (n = 392) received PT on postoperative day (POD) 1.
target: To do this, we looked at 2 past groups of knee replacement patients from July 2016 to December 2017. The first group had 295 patients and had PT on the day of surgery. The second group had 392 patients and received PT on the day after surgery.

source: Outcomes studied included number of feet walked on POD0-3, visual analog scale pain scores, morphine equivalents (ME) consumed, length of stay, and discharge disposition.
target: The outcomes we looked at included the number of feet walked in the first 3 days after surgery, patient reported pain scores, amount of morphine or similar drugs used, length of stay, and where the patient was sent on discharge.

source: Results: In comparison to the PT1 group, the PT0 group walked significantly more steps on POD1 (347.6 vs 167.4 ft, P < .0001), POD2 (342.1 vs 203.5 ft, P < .0001), and POD3 (190.3 vs 128.9 ft, P = .00028).
target: In comparison to the second group (which had PT the day of after surgery), the first group (which had PT the day of the surgery) walked significantly more steps on the first 3 days after surgery. The first group walked, on average, 348 feet the first day after, while the second group walked 167 feet. The second day, the first group walked 342 feet while the second group walked 203. The third day after surgery, the first group walked 190 feet while the second group walked 129 feet.

source: There was no difference between the 2 groups for visual analog scale.
target: There was no difference between the 2 groups for pain reported.

source: The PT0 group also consumed significantly fewer total ME when compared to the PT1 group (149.0 vs 200.3 mg, P = .0002).
target: The first group (which had PT the day of the surgery) also used significantly less morphine or similar drugs when compared to the second group (which had PT the day after surgery).

source: The PT0 group had a significantly shorter length of stay when compared to the PT1 group (2.7 vs 3.2 days, P = .00075).
target: The first group also had a significantly shorter length of stay compared to the second group (on average 2.7 days vs 3.2 days).

source: More patients were discharged home in the PT0 group (81.7% vs 54.8%, P < .0001).
target: More patients were discharged home in the first group (81.7% vs 54.8%).

source: Conclusion: We observed that initiation of PT on POD0 led to better PT performance, reduced ME during hospitalization, and more patients discharged home.
target: In conclusion, we observed that starting PT the day of surgery led to better PT performance, reduced drug use during hospitalization, and more patients discharged home.

source: Total knee arthroplasty (TKA) is among the most common elective procedures performed worldwide.
target: Knee replacement is one of the most common types of surgery that people choose to have worldwide.

source: Recent efforts have been made to significantly improve patient outcomes, specifically with postoperative rehabilitation.
target: Recently, doctors have tried to make big improvements in outcomes, especially during the rehab process.

source: Despite the many rehabilitation modalities available, the optimal rehabilitation strategy has yet to be determined.
target: Even though there are lots of ways to do rehab, doctors have not figured out the best strategy.

source: Therefore, this systematic review focuses on evaluating existing postoperative rehabilitation protocols.
target: Because of this, we will focus on rating different rehab strategies that are in published papers.

source: Specifically, this review analyses the study designs, rehabilitation methods, and outcome measures of postoperative rehabilitation protocols for TKA recipients in the past five years.
target: Specifically, we will look at how studies were done, which rehab methods they used, and what the measured outcomes were. We will do this for rehab strategies for people that had knee replacement in the last 5 years.

source: The PubMed, EMBASE, and Cochrane Library databases were queried for studies evaluating rehabilitation protocols following primary TKA.
target: We searched several databases of biomedical literature for studies that looked at rehab strategies after knee replacement.

source: Of the 11,013 studies identified within the last five years, 70 met the inclusion and exclusion criteria.
target: Out of more than 11,000 from our search, 70 met our criteria for including them.

source: After assessing for relevance and removing duplicates, a final count of 20 studies remained for analysis.
target: After considering relevance and removing duplicates, we were left with 20 studies.

source: Our findings demonstrated that continuous passive motion and inpatient rehabilitation may not provide additional benefit to the patient or healthcare system.
target: We found that Continuous Passive Motion (CPM), where a device moves the leg for the patient, and inpatient rehab may not provide any benefit to the patient or the healthcare system.

source: However, early rehabilitation, telerehabilitation, outpatient therapy, high intensity, and high velocity exercise may be successful forms of rehabilitation.
target: However, early rehab, tele-rehab, where providers interact with patients remotely using the internet, and outpatient therapy may be successful types of rehab. Exercise at high intensity and high speeds may also be successful types of rehab.

source: Additionally, weight-bearing biofeedback, neuromuscular electrical stimulation, and balance control appear to be beneficial adjuncts to conventional rehabilitation.
target: One method that seems to help as a supplement is “weight-bearing biofeedback,” where the patient puts some weight on the joint while wearing sensors to ensure it is not too much. Electrical stimulation of the muscles and balancing exercises also appear to be helpful supplements to typical rehab methods.

source: Postoperative rehabilitation following TKA facilitates patient recovery and improves quality of life.
target: Rehab after knee replacement surgery helps patients recover and improves their quality of life.

source: This systematic review analyzed the existing rehabilitation protocols from the past five years.
target: This article looked at published rehab strategies used in the last 5 years.

source: Some studies did not accurately describe the conventional rehabilitation protocols, the duration of therapy sessions, and the timing of these sessions.
target: Some studies did not accurately describe standard rehab methods, duration of therapy sessions, and the timing of these sessions.

source: As such, future studies should explicitly describe their methodology.
target: Studies done in the future should be more explicit about their methods.

source: This will allow high-quality assessments and the conception of standardized protocols.
target: This will let doctors know what the methods are and evaluate them.

source: Physical therapy is routinely delivered to patients after discharge from the hospital following knee arthroplasty.
target: Physical therapy (PT) is often given to patients that had knee replacement after after leaving the hospital.

source: Posthospitalization physical therapy is thought to be beneficial, particularly for those patients most at risk of poor outcome, the subgroup with persistent function-limiting pain, despite an apparently successful surgery.
target: PT after leaving the hospital is thought to be helpful, especially for patients with high risk of bad outcomes. These are patients with persistent pain that impairs function, even though the surgery seemed to go well.

source: Research teams have undertaken 3 large-scale multicenter Phase 3 randomized clinical trials designed specifically for patients at risk of poor outcome following knee arthroplasty.
target: Research teams have done 3 large-scale trials across different health care facilities, designed specifically for patients at risk of poor outcome following knee replacement.

source: All 3 trials screened for poor outcome risk using different methods and investigated different physical therapist interventions delivered in different ways.
target: All 3 trials checked for poor outcome risk using different methods and looked at different ways for physical therapists to try to help.

source: Despite the variety of types of physical therapy and mode of delivery, all trials found no effects of the enhanced treatment compared with usual care.
target: Despite the variety of types of physical therapy and how they were given, all trials found no effects of the enhanced treatment compared with usual care.

source: In all cases, usual care required a lower dosage of physical therapy compared with the enhanced interventions.
target: In all cases, usual care required a lower amount of physical therapy compared with the enhanced interventions.

source: This Perspective compares and contrasts the 3 trials, speculates on factors that could explain the no-effect findings, and proposes areas for future study designed to benefit the poor outcome phenotype.
target: This article expresses our opinions on recent research. It compares and contrasts the 3 trials, guesses what could explain the findings that there were no effects, and proposes areas for future studies that could help the people at high risk of bad outcomes.

source: Postnatal depression is a major public health problem affecting about one in seven women after childbirth.
target: Postnatal depression (depression after childbirth) is a major public health problem. It affects about one in seven women after childbirth.

source: Depression is also common during pregnancy and throughout the perinatal period it is associated with symptoms of anxiety.
target: Depression is also common during pregnancy and throughout the perinatal period (time of pregnancy and after childbirth ). it is associated with symptoms of anxiety.

source: Apart from the adverse consequences for women themselves becoming depressed when they are going through demanding physical and social changes, there are additional concerns.
target: There are harmful effects for women themselves to become depressed when they are going through demanding physical and social changes. But there are also other concerns.

source: There is the possible negative impact of maternal depression on the relationship between mother and child and on the child's emotional, behavioural and cognitive development.
target: Maternal depression can cause problems with the relationship between mother and child. There can also be problems with the child's emotional, behavioral and cognitive (thinking) development.

source: Primary prevention and early intervention/secondary prevention strategies are potentially important in view of the frequent contact pregnant women, new mothers and infants have with health services, but the effectiveness of these strategies needs to be tested.
target: Prevention and early interventions may be important in view of the frequent contact pregnant women, new mothers and infants have with health services. The effectiveness of these interventions needs to be tested.

source: In the past year there have been five new studies of antenatal screening for postnatal depression.
target: In the past year there have been five new studies of antenatal (during pregnancy) screening for postnatal depression.

source: These studies are consistent with nine earlier studies in showing that there is no evidence to support routine antenatal screening for postnatal depression.
target: These studies agree with nine earlier studies showing that there is no evidence to support routine antenatal screening for postnatal depression.

source: Seven new primary prevention/early intervention trials add evidence on a wide range of interventions ranging from practical support to individual interpersonal therapy, but without identifying significant differences in depression as an outcome.
target: Seven new prevention and early intervention studies add evidence on a wide range of treatments ranging from practical support to individual interpersonal therapy.  These studies have not found significant differences in the outcome of depression.

source: Two new trials of secondary prevention, one involving interpersonal therapy and the other including partners in a series of psychoeducational visits, show promise but neither is large enough to form a basis for practice change.
target: Two new studies on the prevention of the recurrence of depression show promise of success. One invention study involves interpersonal therapy and the other includes partners in psychoeducational visits. Neither study is large enough to form a basis for practice change.

source: Novel interventions, or promising findings, with a strong basis in theory need to be tested in trials which are appropriately sized and which comply with internationally accepted design and reporting guidelines.
target: New interventions, or promising findings, with a strong basis in theory need to be tested in trials (human studies) which are large enough and which meet internationally accepted standards and guidelines.

source: Postpartum depression (PPD) is a common and serious mental health problem that is associated with maternal suffering and numerous negative consequences for offspring.
target: Postpartum depression (PPD-depression after childbirth) is a common and serious mental health problem. It is associated with maternal suffering and numerous negative consequences for offspring.

source: The first six months after delivery may represent a high-risk time for depression.
target: The first six months after delivery may represent a high-risk time for depression.

source: Estimates of prevalence range from 13% to 19%.
target: Estimates of occurrence range from 13% to 19%.

source: Risk factors mirror those typically found with major depression, with the exception of postpartum-specific factors such as sensitivity to hormone changes.
target: Risk factors mirror those typically found with major depression, except the hormone changes found after childbirth.

source: Controlled trials of psychological interventions have validated a variety of individual and group interventions.
target: Studies of psychological treatments have proved a variety of individual and group interventions work.

source: Medication often leads to depression improvement, but in controlled trials there are often no significant differences in outcomes between patients in the medication condition and those in placebo or active control conditions.
target: Medication often leads to depression improvement. But In research studies, there are often no significant differences in outcomes between patients on medication and those not on medication (placebo or active control groups).

source: Reviews converge on recommendations for particular antidepressant medications for use while breastfeeding.
target: Reviews meet up on recommendations for particular antidepressant medications for use while breastfeeding.

source: Prevention of PPD appears to be feasible and effective.
target: Prevention of PPD can be done and treatments are effective.

source: Finally, there is a growing movement to integrate mental health screening into routine primary care for pregnant and postpartum women and to follow up this screening with treatment or referral and with follow-up care.
target: There is a growing movement to add mental health screening into routine primary care for pregnant and postpartum women. This screening should be followed-up with treatment or referral and with follow-up care.

source: Research and clinical recommendations are made throughout this review.
target: Research and patient recommendations are made throughout this review.

source: Nearly 20% of mothers will experience an episode of major or minor depression within the first 3 months postpartum, making it the most common complication of childbearing.
target: Nearly 20% of mothers will experience an episode of major or minor depression within the first 3 months after childbirth (postpartum depression). This shows it is the most common complication of childbearing.

source: Postpartum depression (PPD) is significantly undertreated, and because prospective mothers are especially motivated for self-care, a focus on the prevention of PPD holds promise of clinical efficacy.
target: Many times postpartum depression (PPD) is not treated adequately. Because people planning to become mothers are especially motivated for self-care, focusing on preventing PPD brings hope for the best outcome.

source: This study is a qualitative review of existing approaches to prevent PPD.
target: This research reviews the accepted ways to prevent PPD.

source: A PubMed search identified studies of methods of PPD prevention.
target: A search of articles written about ways to prevent PPD was done using the PubMed search engine.

source: The search was limited to peer-reviewed, published, English-language, randomized controlled trials (RCTs) of biological, psychological, and psychosocial interventions.
target: The search only included published articles, written in English, that were evaluated by experts in the same field as the authors.  These articles were on studies about the psychology, biology (bodily function), and social aspects of PPD.

source: Eighty articles were initially identified, and 45 were found to meet inclusion criteria.
target: This search found 88 studies. Only 45 of them met the rules to be included in this research.

source: Eight RCTs of biological interventions were identified and 37 RCTs of psychological or psychosocial interventions.
target: Of the 45 studies that were included, 8 studies were about biological treatments, and 37 were about psychological or social treatments.

source: Results were mixed, with 20 studies showing clear positive effects of an intervention and 25 showing no effect.
target: Of these 45 studies, 20 studies showed good effects from the treatment and 25 studies showed no effect from the treatment.

source: Studies differed widely in screening, population, measurement, and intervention.
target: The studies were very different in how they recognized and treated PPD. There were also differences in the people studied and how the findings were measured.

source: Among biological studies, anti-depressants and nutrients provided the most evidence of successful intervention.
target: Anti-depressants and nutrients showed the best proof of treatment success in the biological studies.

source: Among psychological and psychosocial studies, 13/17 successful trials targeted an at-risk population, and 4/7 trials using interpersonal therapy demonstrated success of the intervention versus control, with a further two small studies showing trends toward statistical significance.
target: Of the psychological and psychosocial studies, 13 out of 17 successful studies focused on mothers at risk for PPD. Interpersonal therapy showed success in 4 out of 7 studies. Two small studies showed a tendency toward success also.

source: Existing approaches to the prevention of PPD vary widely, and given the current literature, it is not possible to identify one approach that is superior to others.
target: Currently,there are many different ways to prevent PPD. Researchers cannot pinpoint one approach that is better than others.

source: Interpersonal therapy trials and trials that targeted an at-risk population appear to hold the most promise for further study.
target: Clinical trials (studies in people) that focus on at-risk populations and clinical trials of interpersonal therapy seem to hold the most hope for further study.

source: Objectives: Both antidepressant medications and psychological therapy are common treatments for depression in postpartum women.
target: Both antidepressant medications and psychological therapy are common treatments for depression in women after childbirth (postpartum depression-PPD).

source: Antidepressant treatment may have a number of practical disadvantages, including a preference by women to avoid medication while breastfeeding.
target: Antidepressant treatment may have a number of practical disadvantages. Women may prefer to avoid medication while breastfeeding.

source: Consequently, more information about the relative benefits of the two modalities in the perinatal period is helpful.
target: More information about the benefits of these two ways of treating PPD  in the perinatal period (time around pregnancy and childbirth) is helpful.

source: In the treatment of depressive disorders there is some evidence that combination therapies (pharmacological plus psychological treatment) may be more efficacious than either form of mono-therapy in isolation.
target: In treating of depression, there is some evidence that combining both therapies (medications plus psychological treatment) may be more successful than either treatment alone.

source: However, in the treatment of postnatal depression, such evidence is limited.
target: However, in the treatment of postnatal depression, such evidence is limited.

source: Method: Forty five postpartum women with a DSM-IV diagnosis of depression were randomised to receive either: 1) cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT); 2) sertraline, or 3) a combination of both treatment modalities.
target: Researchers did a study in 45 postpartum women with depression. The treatments were assigned randomly. They received either cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT), sertraline (an antidepressant drug), or a combination of both.

source: Psychometric measures were collected weekly for 12 weeks, with a follow-up at 24 weeks.
target: Psychological measurements were collected weekly for 12 weeks, with a follow-up at 24 weeks.

source: Results: Symptoms of depression and anxiety were reduced to a significant degree following all three treatments.
target: Symptoms of depression and anxiety were reduced to a significant degree following all three treatments.

source: CBT mono-therapy was found to be superior to both sertraline mono-therapy and combination therapy after 12 weeks.
target: CBT alone was found to be superior to both sertraline alone and combination therapy after 12 weeks.

source: The CBT mono-therapy group appeared to display the most rapid initial gains after treatment commencement.
target: The CBT therapy group appeared to show the most rapid initial gains after treatment began.

source: Conclusions: In this sample, a specialised CBT program for postnatal depression was found to be superior as a mono-therapy compared to sertraline, a commonly prescribed SSRI antidepressant.
target: In this study, a special CBT program for postnatal depression was found to be superior as a single therapy when compared to sertraline, a commonly prescribed antidepressant.

source: This is in contrast to previous studies which have found no detectable difference in the efficacies of drug and psychological treatment for postnatal depression.
target: This is in disagreement with other studies that found no difference noted in the outcomes between the drug and the psychological treatment for postnatal depression.

source: Unlike some previous work, this study allowed a statistically independent evaluation of CBT mono-therapy for postnatal depression compared to both antidepressant and combination therapy.
target: Unlike some previous work, this study had an independent analysis of CBT single therapy for postnatal depression as compared to both antidepressant and combination therapy.

source: In line with previous studies in postpartum women, there was no detectable advantage of combining pharmacological and psychological treatments in the short term.
target: Like previous studies in postpartum women, there was no advantage noticed by combining drug and psychological treatments in the short term.

source: Postpartum depression is one of the most prevalent psychopathologies.
target: Postpartum depression (depression after childbirth) is one of the most common mental disorders.

source: Its prevalence is estimated to be between 10% and 15%.
target: Its occurance is estimated to be between 10% and 15%.

source: Despite its multifactorial etiology, it is known that genetics play an important role in the genesis of this disorder.
target: Even though the origin of postpartum depression has many causes, it is known that genetics play an important role in the onset of this disorder.

source: This paper reviews epidemiological evidence supporting the role of genetics in postpartum depression (PPD).
target: This research reviews evidence supporting the role of genetics in postpartum depression (PPD).

source: The main objectives of this review are to determine which genes and polymorphisms are associated with PPD and discuss how this association may occur.
target: This research will determine which genes and mutations are associated with PPD. The review will discuss how this association may occur.

source: In addition, this paper explores whether these genes are somehow related to or even the same as those linked to Major Depression (MD).
target: Also, this research studies if these genes are related to (or the same as) genes linked to Major Depression (MD).

source: To identify gaps in the current knowledge that require investigation, a systematic review was conducted in the electronic databases PubMed, LILACS and SciELO using the index terms "postpartum depression" and "genetics".
target: To seek out gaps in the current knowledge that require more research, a thorough review was done in the electronic databases PubMed, LILACS and SciELO. The search words "postpartum depression" and "genetics" were used.

source: Literature searches for articles in peer-reviewed journals were made until April 2014.
target: Literature searches were done for articles in peer-reviewed journals up until April 2014. Peer-reviewed journals use experts in the same field to go over the studies before publishing the articles.

source: PPD was indexed 56 times with genetics.
target: During the literature search, the search words "postpartum depression" and "genetics" were linked 56 times..

source: The inclusion criteria were articles in Portuguese, Spanish or English that were available by institutional means or sent by authors upon request; this search resulted in 20 papers.
target: The rules for including the articles in this study were articles written in Portuguese, Spanish or English that were available to the institutional or the authors. This search resulted in 20 papers.

source: Genes and polymorphisms traditionally related to MD, which are those involved in the serotonin, catecholamine, brain-derived neurotrophic factor and tryptophan metabolism, have been the most studied, and some have been related to PPD.
target: Genes and mutations related to Major Depression (MD) have been the most studied. These genes are involved in the metabolism of chemicals that effect emotions (such as serotonin, catecholamines, brain-derived neurotrophic factor, and tryptophan. )Some of these genes and mutations have been related to PPD.

source: The results are conflicting and some depend on epigenetics, which makes the data incipient.
target: The results are not all in agreement. Some depend on epigenetics (which can change the way your genes work without changing the DNA itself). The results are in the early stages.

source: Further studies are required to determine the genes that are involved in PPD and establish the nature of the relationship between these genes and PPD.
target: Further studies are required to determine the genes that are involved in PPD. More studies are needed to define the relationship between these genes and PPD.

source: Most interventions to prevent postpartum depression (PPD) focus on the mother rather than the mother-infant dyad.
target: Most treatments to prevent depression after giving birth, postpartum depression (PPD), focus on the mother instead of the total relationship between mother and infant.

source: As strong relationships between infant sleep and cry behavior and maternal postpartum mood have been demonstrated by previous research, interventions targeted at the dyad may reduce symptoms of PPD.
target: Research showed strong relationships between infant sleep and cry behavior and mother's mood after childbirth. Interventions focusing on the relationship between mother and infant (mother-infant dyad) may reduce PPD.

source: The goal of the current study was to examine the effectiveness of Practical Resources for Effective Postpartum Parenting (PREPP).
target: This study examines the success of the PREPP (Practical Resources for Effective Postpartum Parenting) intervention.

source: PREPP is a new PPD prevention protocol that aims to treat women at risk for PPD by promoting maternally mediated behavioral changes in their infants, while also including mother-focused skills.
target: PREPP is a new PPD prevention program that treats women at risk for PPD. This intervention teaches skills to help mothers themselves as well as skills to help make changes in their infant's behavior.

source: Results of this randomized control trial (RCT) (n = 54) indicate that this novel, brief intervention was well tolerated and effective in reducing maternal symptoms of anxiety and depression, particularly at 6 weeks postpartum.
target: This study showed that this new and brief intervention was accepted well and successful in reducing maternal symptoms of anxiety and depression, particularly at 6 weeks postpartum (after childbirth).

source: Additionally, this study found that infants of mothers enrolled in PREPP had fewer bouts of fussing and crying at 6 weeks postpartum than those infants whose mothers were in the Enhanced TAU group.
target: This study found that infants of mothers in the PREPP program had fewer bouts of fussing and crying at 6 weeks postpartum compared to infants whose mothers were in another group, (the Enhanced TAU group).

source: These preliminary results indicate that PREPP has the potential to reduce the incidence of PPD in women at risk and to directly impact the developing mother-child relationship, the mother's view of her child, and child outcomes.
target: These early results show that PREPP  may be able to reduce PPD in women at risk and to directly affect the developing mother-child relationship, the mother's view of her child, and child outcomes.

source: Background: Post-partum depression is a serious mood disorder in women that might be triggered by peripartum fluctuations in reproductive hormones.
target: Post-partum depression (depression after childbirth) is a serious mood disorder in women that might be triggered by changes in reproductive hormones during the period of time around pregnancy.

source: This phase 2 study investigated brexanolone (USAN; formerly SAGE-547 injection), an intravenous formulation of allopregnanolone, a positive allosteric modulator of γ-aminobutyric acid (GABAA) receptors, for the treatment of post-partum depression.
target: This research study examined the drug, brexanolone (formerly SAGE-547 injection). Brexanolone is a naturally occurring reproductive steroid hormone that affects receptors in the brain and nervous system.  It is used as an IV medicine for the treatment of post-partum depression.

source: Methods: For this double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled trial, we enrolled self-referred or physician-referred female inpatients (≤6 months post partum) with severe post-partum depression (Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression [HAM-D] total score ≥26) in four hospitals in the USA.
target: Researchers studied female inpatients who gave birth within 6 months of the study with severe post-partum depression (in four hospitals in the USA. The severity of depression was checked by the questionnaire-Hamilton Rating Scale for Depression [HAM-D].

source: Eligible women were randomly assigned (1:1), via a computer-generated randomisation program, to receive either a single, continuous intravenous dose of brexanolone or placebo for 60 h.
target: Eligible women received either one, continuous IV dose of brexanolone for 60 hours or a placebo (a procedure that appears like the real treatment but has no treatment value). The women were chosen at random by a computer program to receive the real treatment or the placebo.

source: Patients and investigators were masked to treatment assignments.
target: Both patients and researchers did not know who received the real treatment and who received the placebo.

source: The primary efficacy endpoint was the change from baseline in the 17-item HAM-D total score at 60 h, assessed in all randomised patients who started infusion of study drug or placebo and who had a completed baseline HAM-D assessment and at least one post-baseline HAM-D assessment.
target: The most important outcome was the change in the HAM-D score between the start of the study and at 60 hours .

source: Patients were followed up until day 30.
target: Patients were followed up until for 30 days.

source: This trial is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, number NCT02614547.
target: This study (trial) is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov, number NCT02614547.

source: Findings: This trial was done between Dec 15, 2015 (first enrolment), and May 19, 2016 (final visit of the last enrolled patient).
target: This trial was done between Dec 15, 2015 (first enrolment), and May 19, 2016 (final visit of the last enrolled patient).

source: 21 women were randomly assigned to the brexanolone (n=10) and placebo (n=11) groups.
target: 21 women were randomly assigned to the brexanolone (10 women) and placebo (11 women) groups.

source: At 60 h, mean reduction in HAM-D total score from baseline was 21·0 points (SE 2·9) in the brexanolone group compared with 8·8 points (SE 2·8) in the placebo group (difference -12·2, 95% CI -20·77 to -3·67; p=0·0075; effect size 1·2).
target: At 60 hours, the average decrease in the depression score by the HAM-D questionnaire was 21 points in the brexanolone group (compared to the score at the start of the study).  In the placebo group, the HAM-D score only decreased by an average of 8.8 points.

source: No deaths, serious adverse events, or discontinuations because of adverse events were reported in either group.
target: No deaths, serious adverse (harmful) events, or stopping of the procedures because of adverse events were reported in either group.

source: Four of ten patients in the brexanolone group had adverse events compared with eight of 11 in the placebo group.
target: Four of ten patients in the brexanolone group had adverse events compared with eight of 11 in the placebo group.

source: The most frequently reported adverse events in the brexanolone group were dizziness (two patients in the brexanolone group vs three patients in the placebo group) and somnolence (two vs none).
target: The most frequently reported adverse events in the brexanolone group were dizziness (two patients in the brexanolone group vs three patients in the placebo group) and sleepiness (two vs none).

source: Moderate treatment-emergent adverse events were reported in two patients in the brexanolone group (sinus tachycardia, n=1; somnolence, n=1) and in two patients in the placebo group (infusion site pain, n=1; tension headache, n=1); one patient in the placebo group had a severe treatment-emergent adverse event (insomnia).
target: Other adverse events reported in two patients in the brexanolone group were fast heart rate and sleepiness. Two patients in the placebo group had adverse events; pain at the IV site and tension headache. One patient in the placebo group had a severe treatment adverse event (insomnia).

source: Interpretation: In women with severe post-partum depression, infusion of brexanolone resulted in a significant and clinically meaningful reduction in HAM-D total score, compared with placebo.
target: In women with severe post-partum depression, brexanolone IV resulted in a significant and clinically meaningful reduction in HAM-D total score of depression, compared with placebo.

source: Our results support the rationale for targeting synaptic and extrasynaptic GABAA receptors in the development of therapies for patients with post-partum depression.
target: Our results support the reasons for focusing on GABAA receptors in order to development therapies for patients with post-partum depression.

source: A pivotal clinical programme for the investigation of brexanolone in patients with post-partum depression is in progress.
target: A major clinical program for the study of brexanolone in patients with post-partum depression is in progress.

source: Objective: Postpartum mood disorders represent a serious problem affecting 10-20% of women and support groups offer a promising intervention modality.
target: Postpartum (after giving birth) mood disorders represent a serious problem affecting 10-20% of women. Support groups offer a promising treatment.

source: The current study examined participant satisfaction with and effectiveness of a peer-facilitated postpartum support group.
target: This research studied women's satisfaction with and the success of a postpartum support group. This support group was guided by peers (others who had similar experiences).

source: Intervention: The program consists of a free, peer-support group, developed to increase social support and destigmatise postpartum mood symptoms.
target: The program consists of a free, peer-support group, developed to increase social support and take away the shame of postpartum mood symptoms.

source: The weekly group is co-facilitated by former group attendees and maternal health professionals.
target: The weekly group is assisted by former group participants and maternal health professionals.

source: Setting: The peer-support program is offered in an urban city in the southeastern United States.
target: The peer-support program is offered in an urban city in the southeastern United States.

source: Design: To address study aims, a community-based participatory research approach was implemented.
target: The research study was done with community participation.

source: Participant satisfaction was assessed via mixed methods analyses.
target: The satisfaction of the participants was analyzed

source: Differences in depression scores at follow-up between program attendees and a community sample were examined via weighted linear regression analysis following propensity score analysis.
target: Differences in depression measured at follow-up between program participants and a sample of community members that did not take part in the program were analyzed.

source: Finally, within-group change in depression scores for program attendees was examined using a repeated measures ANOVA.
target: Changes in depression measured for the participant group (program attendees) was also analyzed.

source: Participants: Intake program data were provided by the sponsoring organisation (n = 73) and follow-up data were collected via an online survey from program attendees (n = 45).
target: Facts (data) about the participants at the beginning of the program were provided by the organisation backing the support group.  The follow-up data was given by the participants through an online questionnaire.

source: A community sample was recruited to establish a comparison group (n = 152).
target: A community sample of women who did not participate in the program was enrolled to compare with the participants.

source: Measurements and findings: Participant satisfaction was high with overwhelmingly positive perceptions of the program.
target: Participant satisfaction was high with overwhelmingly positive perceptions of the program.

source: Postparticipation depression scores were similar to those of the community sample at follow-up (p = .447).
target: After participation in the program, depression scores were similar to those of the community sample at follow-up.

source: Among attendees, pre-post analyses revealed reductions in depression symptoms with significant interactions for time × complications (p ≤ .001) and time × delivery method (p ≤ .017).
target: Analyses comparing data of participants both before and after the program showed decreases in depression symptoms. This decrease in depression was significantly associated with time, complications, and delivery method.

source: Key conclusions: Overall, findings indicate this peer-support program is not only acceptable to program attendees but also they provide a potential mechanism for improving mental health outcomes; however, further evaluation is needed.
target: The research found that this peer-support program is acceptable to program attendees and it provides a possible way to improve mental health outcomes. More research is needed.

source: Findings also emphasise the importance of integrating evaluation procedures into community-based mental health programming to support effectiveness.
target: The research also stress the importance of combining analysis procedures into community-based mental health programs in order to support successful outcomes.

source: Implications for practice: Peer-support groups are an acceptable form of intervention for women experiencing postpartum depression.
target: Peer-support groups are an acceptable form of treatment for women experiencing postpartum depression.

source: Postpartum depression (PPD) is a major public health problem affecting 10-57% of adolescent mothers which can affect not only adolescent mothers but also their infants.
target: Depression after giving birth, postpartum depression (PPD) is a major public health problem. PPD affects 10-57% of adolescent mothers. It can affect not only adolescent mothers but also their infants.

source: Thus, there is a need for interventions to prevent PPD in adolescent mothers.
target: There is a need for treatments to prevent PPD in adolescent mothers.

source: However, recent systematic reviews have been focused on effective interventions to prevent PPD in adult mothers.
target: Recent thorough reviews of studies on preventing PPD  have been focused on successful treatments in adult mothers.

source: These interventions may not necessarily be applicable for adolescent mothers.
target: These treatments to prevent PPD may not necessarily apply to adolescent mothers.

source: Therefore, the purpose of this review was to examine the effectiveness of the existing interventions to prevent PPD in adolescent mothers.
target: This research judged the benefit of accepted treatments to prevent PPD in adolescent mothers.

source: A systematic search was performed in MEDLINE, CINAHL, and SCOPUS databases between January 2000 and March 2017 with English language and studies involving human subjects.
target: A thorough search was performed in MEDLINE, CINAHL, and SCOPUS databases (organized collection of articles including health topics) to find English language articles written between January 2000 and March 2017 on studies involving human subjects.

source: Studies reporting on the outcomes of intervention to prevent PPD particularly in adolescent mothers were selected.
target: Studies reporting on the outcomes of intervention (treatment) to prevent PPD particularly in adolescent mothers were selected.

source: Non-comparative studies were excluded.
target: Researchers did not include studies that were not comparative studies.

source: From 2002 identified records, 13 studies were included, reporting on 2236 adolescent pregnant women.
target: Of the 2002 articles found, researchers included 13 studies. These 13 studies included information on a total of 2236 adolescent pregnant women.

source: The evidence from this systematic review suggests that 6 of 13 studies from both psychological and psychosocial interventions including (1) home-visiting intervention, (2) prenatal antenatal and postnatal educational program, (3) CBT psycho-educational, (4) the REACH program based on interpersonal therapy, and (5) infant massage training is successful in reducing rates of PPD symptoms in adolescent mothers in the intervention group than those mothers in the control group.
target: Six out of 13 studies suggests interventions are successful in lowering rates of PPD symptoms in adolescent mothers (compared to the group that did not receive interventions). The research was from the psychological and social intervention studies. The interventions included: home visits; education (before, during, and after pregnancy and childbirth); psychological therapy; interpersonal therapy, and infant massage training.

source: These interventions might be considered for incorporation in antenatal care interventions for adolescent pregnant women.
target: These interventions might be included in maternity care for adolescent pregnant women.

source: However, this review did not find evidence identifying the most effective intervention for preventing postpartum depression symptoms in adolescent mothers.
target: This research did not find out the most successful intervention to prevent postpartum depression in adolescent mothers.

source: Postpartum depression (PPD) is common, disabling, and treatable.
target: Postpartum depression (PPD) is depression that occurs after childbirth. It is common disorder that is disabling, but it is treatable.

source: The strongest risk factor is a history of mood or anxiety disorder, especially having active symptoms during pregnancy.
target: The strongest risk factor for PPD is a history of mood or anxiety disorders, especially if women have active symptoms during pregnancy.

source: As PPD is one of the most common complications of childbirth, it is vital to identify best treatments for optimal maternal, infant, and family outcomes.
target: PPD is one of the most common complications of childbirth. It is very important to discover the most effective treatments in order to have the best outcomes for mothers, infants, and their families.

source: New understanding of PPD pathophysiology and emerging therapeutics offer the potential for new ways to add to current medications, somatic treatments, and evidence-based psychotherapy.
target: There is new understanding of how PPD changes the body's functions. Also, new treatments offer possible different ways to add to current medications, mind-body treatments, and evidence-based psychotherapy.

source: The benefits and potential harms of treatment, including during breastfeeding, are presented.
target: The benefits and potential harms of treatment, including during breastfeeding, are presented.

source: Animal and human studies suggest that vitamin B6 deficiency affects both humoral and cell-mediated immune responses.
target: Animal and human research studies suggest that vitamin B6 deficiency (not enough vitamin B6) affects the immune system.

source: Lymphocyte differentiation and maturation are altered by deficiency, delayed-type hypersensitivity responses are reduced, and antibody production may be indirectly impaired.
target: White blood cell function and antibody production are damaged. Delayed allergy reactions are reduced (like the reaction to a tuberculosis (TB) skin test)).

source: Although repletion of the vitamin restores these functions, megadoses do not produce benefits beyond those observed with moderate supplementation.
target: Taking enough vitamin B6 to get  levels back to normal corrects the immune system problems. Taking very high doses supplements does not cause further improvement.

source: Additional human studies indicate that vitamin B6 status may influence tumor growth and disease processes.
target: Human research also show that the amount of vitamin B6 in the body may affect tumor growth and disease.

source: Deficiency of the vitamin has been associated with immunological changes observed in the elderly, persons infected with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), and those with uremia or rheumatoid arthritis.
target: Vitamin B6 deficiency (not enough vitamin B6 in the body) has been associated with immune system changes in the elderly.Vitamin B6 deficiency also affects people with HIV infections, kidney disease, and rheumatoid arthritis.

source: Future research efforts should focus on establishing the mechanism underlying the effects of vitamin B6 on immunity and should attempt to establish safe intake levels that optimize immune response.
target: Future research studies should focus on explaining exactly  how vitamin B6 affects the immune system and the right amount of vitamin B6 to take for the best immune system function.

source: The vitamins folic acid, B12 and B6 and B2 are the source of coenzymes which participate in one carbon metabolism.
target: There are several B vitamins: folic acid, B12 and B6 and B2. They are important in one carbon metabolism of protein and DNA metabolism.

source: In this metabolism, a carbon unit from serine or glycine is transferred to tetrahydrofolate (THF) to form methylene-THF.
target: Here, one carbon unit is transferred from an amino acid (building blocks of proteins) by a B vitamin, tetrahydrofolate (THF), to form a new chemical, methylene-THF.

source: This is either used as such for the synthesis of thymidine, which is incorporated into DNA, oxidized to formyl-THF which is used for the synthesis of purines, which are building blocks of RNA and DNA, or it is reduced to methyl-THF which used to methylate homocysteine to form methionine, a reaction which is catalyzed by a B12-containing methyltransferase.
target: Methylene-THF is used  in the synthesis of DNA,  RNA, or in the metabolism of important amino acids (such as the reaction that changes one amino acid, homocysteine, to another amino acid, methionine).

source: Much of the methionine which is formed is converted to S-adenosylmethionine (SAM), a universal donor of methyl groups, including DNA, RNA, hormones, neurotransmitters, membrane lipids, proteins and others.
target: Much of the methionine which is formed is made into S-adenosylmethionine (SAM) and then used in the metabolism of DNA, RNA, hormones, neurotransmitters (nerve impulse chemicals), cell lipids (fats), proteins and others.

source: Because of these functions, interest in recent years has been growing particularly in the area of aging and the possibility that certain diseases that afflict the aging population, loss of cognitive function, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, cancer and others, may be in part explained by inadequate intake or inadequate status of these vitamins.
target: Because of these functions, interest in recent years has been growing particularly in the area of aging. The possibility that certain diseases associated with aging  such as the loss of thinking, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, cancer and others, may be in part explained by inadequate intake or inadequate amounts in the body of these vitamins.

source: Homocysteine, a product of methionine metabolism as well as a precursor of methionine synthesis, was shown recently to be a risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke and thrombosis when its concentration in plasma is slightly elevated.
target: Homocysteine, an amino acid that is both needed to make methionine as well as made from methionine metabolism was shown recently to be a risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke and blood clots  when its amount in blood is slightly elevated.

source: There are now data which show association between elevated plasma homocysteine levels and loss of neurocognitive function and Alzheimer's disease.
target: Facts now show association between increased blood levels of homocysteine and loss of brain function and Alzheimer's disease.

source: These associations could be due to a neurotoxic effect of homocysteine or to decreased availability of SAM which results in hypomethylation in the brain tissue.
target: These associations could be due to harmful effects of homocysteine on the nervous system or on the decreased amount of SAM in the brain.

source: Hypomethylation is also thought to exacerbate depressive tendency in people, and for (colorectal) cancer DNA hypomethylation is thought to be the link between the observed relationship between inadequate folate status and cancer.
target: Problems with methionine metabolism are also thought to increase depressive tendency in people.These problems with methionine and DNA metabolism are also thought to be the link between the relationship seen between low levels of vitamin B folate and colorectal cancer.

source: There are many factors that contribute to the fact that the status of these vitamins in the elderly is inadequate.
target: Many factors(reasons) contribute to the fact that the amount of these vitamins in the elderly is inadequate.

source: These factors are in part physiological such as the achlorhydria which affects vitamin B12 absorption and in part socioeconomic and habitual.
target: One factor is not having enough acid in the stomach (achlorhydria). This decreases the amount of  vitamin B12 your stomach can absorb from food. Other factors include socioeconomic and habits.

source: We need more studies to confirm that these vitamins have important functions in the etiology of these diseases.
target: We need more studies to prove that these vitamins have important functions in the causes of these diseases.

source: We also need to establish if these diseases can be prevented or diminished by proper nutrition starting at a younger age.
target: We also need to prove iif these diseases can be prevented or lessened by proper nutrition starting at a younger age.

source: Background: Vitamin B6 is thought to be a most versatile coenzyme that participates in more than 100 biochemical reactions.
target: Vitamin B6 is thought to be a most versatile chemical that participates in more than 100 biochemical reactions in the body.

source: It is involved in amino acid and homocysteine metabolism, glucose and lipid metabolism, neurotransmitter production and DNA/RNA synthesis.
target: It is involved in protein and amino acid (building blocks of protein) metabolism, carbohydrate (sugar) and lipid (fat) metabolism. It is also involved in neurotransmitter (chemicals involved in nerve impulses) production and DNA/RNA synthesis.

source: Vitamin B6 can also be a modulator of gene expression.
target: Vitamin B6 can also be a chemical that regulates gene expression.

source: Nowadays, clinically evident vitamin B6 deficiency is not a common disorder, at least in the general population.
target: Currently, signs and symptoms of vitamin B6 deficiency is not a common disorder in the general population.

source: Nevertheless, a subclinical, undiagnosed deficiency may be present in some subjects, particularly in the elderly.
target: But Vitamin B6 deficiency may be present without signs or symptoms especially in the elderly.

source: Objective: This review gives a complete overview over the metabolism and interactions of vitamin B6.
target: This research give a complete overview of the actions  of vitamin B6 in the body.

source: Further, we show which complications and deficiency symptoms can occur due to a lack of vitamin B6 and possibilities for public health and supplemental interventions.
target: Researchers show which specific problems can occur due to a lack of vitamin B6. Researchers also discuss possibilities for public health and adding supplements to foods.

source: Methods: The database Medline (www.ncvi.nlm.nih.gov) was searched for terms like "vitamin B6", "pyridoxal", "cancer", "homocysteine", etc.
target: The database Medline (www.ncvi.nlm.nih.gov) was searched on the computer for terms like "vitamin B6", "pyridoxal", "cancer", "homocysteine", etc.

source: For a complete understanding, we included studies with early findings from the forties as well as recent results from 2006.
target: Researchers included studies with early findings from the forties as well as recent results from 2006 for a complete understanding.

source: These studies were summarised and compared in different chapters.
target: This research was summarised and compared in different chapters.

source: Results and conclusion: In fact, it has been proposed that suboptimal vitamin B6 status is associated with certain diseases that particularly afflict the elderly population: impaired cognitive function, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and different types of cancer.
target: Researchers have proposed that less than adequate (suboptimal) vitamin B6 levels are associated with certain diseases that particularly trouble the elderly population such as: decreased ability to think clearly, Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and different types of cancer.

source: Some of these problems may be related to the elevated homocysteine concentrations associated to vitamin B6 deficiency, but there is also evidence for other mechanisms independent of homocysteine by which a suboptimal vitamin B6 status could increase the risk for these chronic diseases.
target: Some of these problems may be related to an increased homocysteine (amino acid) levels that is associated with vitamin B6 deficiency. There is also evidence for other ways suboptimal vitamin B6 levels could increase the risk for these chronic diseases.

source: Pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP), the bioactive form of vitamin B6, reportedly functions as a prosthetic group for >4% of classified enzymatic activities of the cell.
target: Pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) is the active form of vitamin B6 in the body. PLP is involved in more than 4% of the chemical reactions of proteins in the cell.

source: It is therefore not surprising that alterations of vitamin B6 metabolism have been associated with multiple human diseases.
target: Changes in vitamin B6 metabolism have been associated with multiple human diseases.

source: As a striking example, mutations in the gene coding for antiquitin, an evolutionary old aldehyde dehydrogenase, result in pyridoxine-dependent seizures, owing to the accumulation of a metabolic intermediate that inactivates PLP.
target: For example, mutations in the gene coding for antiquitin, a protein that appeared early in evolution, result in seizures. These seizures are due to an increase in a chemical (made during vitamin B6 metabolism) that stops the function of PLP.

source: In addition, PLP is required for the catabolism of homocysteine by transsulfuration.
target: PLP is also required for the metabolism of homocysteine (an amino acid involved in vitamin B metabolism)..

source: Hence, reduced circulating levels of B6 vitamers (including PLP as well as its major precursor pyridoxine) are frequently paralleled by hyperhomocysteinemia, a condition that has been associated with an increased risk for multiple cardiovascular diseases.
target: Lower blood levels of B6 vitamers (active vitamin B6 chemicals-PLP and pyridoxine) are associated with  hyperhomocysteinemia (excess blood homocysteine). Hyperhomocysteinemia is associated with a higher risk of many cardiovascular diseases.

source: During the past 30 years, an intense wave of clinical investigation has attempted to dissect the putative links between vitamin B6 and cancer.
target: During the past 30 years, a lot of research has tried to explain links between vitamin B6 and cancer.

source: Thus, high circulating levels of vitamin B6, as such or as they reflected reduced amounts of circulating homocysteine, have been associated with improved disease outcome in patients bearing a wide range of hematological and solid neoplasms.
target: High blood levels of vitamin B6, or the related lower blood levels of homocysteine, have been associated with improved disease outcome in patients with blood and solid organ neoplasms.

source: More recently, the proficiency of vitamin B6 metabolism has been shown to modulate the adaptive response of tumor cells to a plethora of physical and chemical stress conditions.
target: Recently, vitamin B6 metabolism has been shown to control tumor cells' ability to adjust to many different types of stress.

source: Moreover, elevated levels of pyridoxal kinase (PDXK), the enzyme that converts pyridoxine and other vitamin B6 precursors into PLP, have been shown to constitute a good, therapy-independent prognostic marker in patients affected by non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC).
target: Also, increased levels of pyridoxal kinase (PDXK-a protein involved in producing PLP), have been shown to predict good  outcomes in patients with non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC).

source: Here, we will discuss the clinical relevance of vitamin B6 metabolism as a prognostic factor in cancer patients.
target: This research discusses the importance of vitamin B6 metabolism in the disease outcome of cancer patients.

source: Background: Higher plasma concentrations of the vitamin B-6 marker pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) have been associated with reduced colorectal cancer (CRC) risk.
